title: Application of trace formula to trigonometric sums

pages.title: Application of trace formula to trigonometric sums
pages.html:
6 Application of trace formula to trigonometric sums

Applications to trigonometric sums

In this paper, I explain how the trace formula can be used to calculate or study various trigonometric amounts and how, together with Weil's conjecture, it can increase them.

The first two paragraphs give a "how to use" of these tools. Paragraph 3 is a presentation, in a cohomological language, of Weil's results on the amounts to \(1\) variable. The paragraphs \(4\) to \(6\) form a detailed study of the sums of Gauss and Jacobi – including Weil's old and recent results on Hecke's characters defined by Jacobi sums. In paragraph \(7\) we study a generalization to several variables of the sums of Kloosterman. Finally, in paragraph \(8\) we find some indications of other uses that have been made of these methods.

6 Notes

0.1

The ratings of [II]Report, paragraph 1. One will often have to consider a finite extension \(\mathbb{F}_{q^n}\subset \mathbb{F}\) of \(\mathbb{F}_q\). One will notice with an index \(0\) an object on \(\mathbb{F}_q\), and with an index \(1\) an object on \(\mathbb{F}_{q^n}\). Replace an index \(0\) with an index \(1\) (resp. removing the acid) means that the scalar is extended to \(\mathbb{F}_{q^n}\) (resp. to \(\mathbb{F}\)).

0.2

\(\ell\) a prime number \(\ne p\). We use freely the language of \(\mathbb{Q}_\ell\)-sheaves, as well as that of \(E_\lambda\)-sheaves, for \(E_\lambda\) a finite extension of \(\mathbb{Q}_\ell\) (cf. [II]Report, paragraph 2 and especially 2.11).

0.3

Either \(H^\bullet\) a graduated vector space. If \(T\) is an endomorphism of \(H^\bullet\), one poses (cf. [IV]Cycle, 1.3.5)

\[ \operatorname{tr}(T,H^\bullet) = \sum (-1)^i \operatorname{tr}(T,H^i) \text{.} \]

pages.title: Principles
pages.html:
1 Principles

1.1

Be \(X_0\) a separate scheme of finite type on \(\mathbb{F}_q\), \(E_\lambda\) a finite extension of \(\mathbb{Q}_\ell\) and \(\mathscr{F}_0\) a \(E_\lambda\)-sheaf on \(X-0\). The formula of traces says that

\[ \tag{1.1.1} \sum_{x\in X^F} \operatorname{tr}(F_x^\ast,\mathscr{F}) = \sum (-1)^i \operatorname{tr}\left(F^\ast,\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(X,\mathscr{F})\right) \text{.} \]

With notation 0.3, the right-hand member is simply written \(\operatorname{tr}(F^\ast,\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^\bullet(X,\mathscr{F}))\).

This trace formula for \(E_\lambda\)-sheaves can be deduced from [II]Report 4.10 limit passage (cf. [II]Report 4.11 to 4.13), or be deducted from the trace formula for \(\mathbb{Q}_\ell\)-sheaves ([II]Report 3.2) by the method of ([III]Functions \(L\) mod. \(\ell^n\), 4.3).

We will interpret various trigonometrics as the left-hand member of (1.1.1), for \(X_0\) and \(\mathscr{F}_0\) suitable.

1.2

Either \(A\) a finite group. \(A\)-torsor on a diagram \(X\) is a sheaf \(T\) on \(X\), equipped with a right-hand action of \(A\), which, locally (for the live topology) on \(X\), is isomorphic to the constant sheaf \(A\) on which \(A\) acts by translation to the right.

If \(\tau:A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) is a homomorphism, and \(T\) a \(A\)-torseur, there is a unique isomorphism near one and only one \(B\)-torseur \(\tau(T)\), with sheaf morphism \(\tau:T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \tau(T)\) such as

\[ \tag{1.2.1} \tau(t a) = \tau(t) \tau(a)\text{.} \]

If \(T\) is a \(A\)-torsor on \(X\), and \(\rho\) a linear representation of \(A\): \(\rho:A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{gl}(V)\) (\(V\) a vector space of finite dimension on \(E_\lambda\)), there is a single isomorphism near a single \(E_\lambda\)-sheaf \(\mathscr{F}\), smooth row \(\dim(V)\), equipped with sheaf morphism

\[ \tag{1.2.2} \rho:T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{Isom}}(V,\mathscr{F}) \text{.} \]

\(\rho(t a) = \rho(t) \rho(a)\). It is noted \(\rho(T)\). For \(\tau:A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) and \(\rho\) a representation of \(B\), one has canonical isomorphism \(\rho(\tau(T)) = (\rho\tau)(T)\).

This paragraph (except the appendix) only considers the case where \(A\) is commutative and \(V=E_\lambda\): \(\rho\) is a character \(A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} E_\lambda^\times\), and \(\rho(T)\) a \(E_\lambda\)-smoothed sheaf of rank one. Morphism (1.2.2) interprets itself as a morphism everywhere non-zero

\[ \tag{1.2.3} \rho:T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \rho(T) \]

such as \(\rho(t a) = \rho(a)\rho(t)\).

1.3

Be \(S\) a scheme, \(G\) a scheme in a commutative group on \(S\), and \(G'\) an extension of scheme in commutative groups on \(S\)

\[ \xymatrix{ 0 \ar[r] & A \ar[r] & G' \ar[r]^-\pi & G \ar[r] & 0 . } \]

The sheaf \(T\) of the local sections of \(\pi\) is then a \(A\)-torse on \(G\).

If \(X\) is a scheme on \(S\), and \(f,g\) two \(S\)-morphisms of \(X\) in \(G\), because \(T\) is defined by an extension, one has canonical isomorphism

\[ \tag{1.3.1} (f+g)^\ast T = f^\ast T + g^\ast T \text{.} \]

On the left, \(f+g\) is the sum of \(f\) and \(g\), within the meaning of group law \(G\): on the right, \(+\) means a sum of torsors.

If \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G\) is factored by \(G'\), \(f^\ast T\) is trivial (and a factorization gives a trivialization); to isomorphism (not unique) near \(A\)-torseur \(f^\ast T\) depends only on the image of \(f\) in \(\hom_S(X,G)/\pi\hom_S(X,G')\): it is given by morphism \(\partial\) in the exact sequence

\[ \xymatrix{ \hom(X,G') \ar[r] & \hom(X,G) \ar[r]^-\partial & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(X,A) . } \]

We will apply these constructions to Kummer's exact suites \(0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \boldsymbol{\mu}_n \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbb{G}_m \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbb{G}_m \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0\), and Lang's torsors.

1.4. The Lang torsor

Either \(G_0\) a related commutative algebraic group on \(\mathbb{F}_q\) (for non- commutative cases, see Appendix). The group law is noted multiple times. Lang's isogeny

\[ \mathfrak{L}:G_0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G_0: x\mapsto F x\cdot x^{-1} \]

is spread; its image, an open sub-group of \(G_0\), can only be \(G_0\) itself; its kernel is the finite group \(G_0(\mathbb{F}_q)\). Lang torsor \(L\) is the \(G_0(\mathbb{F}_q)\)-torsor on \(G_0\) defined by the exact later

\[ \tag{1.4.1} \xymatrix{ 0 \ar[r] & G_0(\mathbb{F}_q) \ar[r] & G_0 \ar[r]^-{\mathfrak{L}} & G_0 \ar[r] & 0 . } \]

Rating

Note again \(L\) and \(\mathfrak{L}\) (or \(L_{(q)}\) and \(\mathfrak{L}_{(q)}\)) the items deducted from \(L\) and \(\mathfrak{L}\) by extension of the base field. If necessary, an index shall be given \(0\) or \(1\).

Examples

For \(G_0=\mathbb{G}_a\) or \(\mathbb{G}_m\), the suite(1.4.1) write

\begin{align*} \tag{1.4.2} &\xymatrix{ 0 \ar[r] & \mathbb{F}_q \ar[r] & \mathbb{G}_a \ar[r]^-{x^q-x} & \mathbb{G}_a \ar[r] & 0 } \\ \tag{1.4.3} &\xymatrix{ 0 \ar[r] & \boldsymbol{\mu}_{q-1} \ar[r] & \mathbb{G}_m \ar[r]^-{x^{q-1}} & \mathbb{G}_m \ar[r] & 0 } \end{align*}

1.5

Let's calculate the endomorphism \(F^\ast\) of the Lang torsor fiber in \(\gamma\in G^F=G_0(\mathbb{F}_q)\). If \(g\in \mathfrak{L}^{-1}(\gamma)\subset G\), we have \(F g = F g\cdot g^{-1}\cdot g = \gamma g\). From then on ([II]Report, 1.2)

\[ \tag{1.5.1} \text{on } L_0(G_0)_\gamma\simeq \mathfrak{L}^{-1}(\gamma) \text{, } F^\ast \text{ is } g\mapsto g\gamma^{-1} \text{.} \]

1.6

On \(\mathbb{F}_{q^n}\), the identity \(F_{(q^n)}=F_{(q)}^n\) between \(G_1\) implies that \(\mathfrak{L}_{(q^n)}=\mathfrak{L}_{(q)}\circ \prod_{i=0}^{n-1} F_{(q)}^i\). On \(G_0(\mathbb{F}_{q^n})\), \(F_{(q)}^i\) acts as the element \(x\mapsto x^{q^i}\) of \(\operatorname{Gal}(\mathbb{F}_{q^n}/\mathbb{F}_q)\). On \(G_0(\mathbb{F}_{q^n})\), \(\prod F_{(q)}^i\) is therefore composed of the standard \(G_0(\mathbb{F}_{q^n})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G_0(\mathbb{F}_q)\) and inclusion \(G_0(\mathbb{F}_q)\subset G_0(\mathbb{F}_{q^n})\): the diagram

\[ \tag{1.6.1} \xymatrix{ 0 \ar[r] & G_0(\mathbb{F}_{q^n}) \ar[r] \ar[d]^-N & G_1 \ar[r]^-{\mathfrak{L}_{(q^n)}} \ar[d]^-{\prod_{i=0}^{n-1} F_{(q)}^i} & G_1 \ar[r] \ar@{=}[d] & 0 \\ 0 \ar[r] & G_0(\mathbb{F}_q) \ar[r] & G_q \ar[r]^-{\mathfrak{L}_{(q)}} & G_1 \ar[r] & 0 } \]

In the language of the torsors, this provides canonical isomorphism between \(G_0(\mathbb{F}_q)\)-torsors on \(G_1\)

\[ \tag{1.6.2} N L_{(q^n)} = L_{(q)} \text{.} \]
Definition 1.7

Be \(f_0:X_0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G_0\) a morphism and \(\chi:G_0(\mathbb{F}_q)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} E_\lambda^\times\) a character. We pose \(\mathscr{F}(\chi,f_0) = \chi^{-1}(f_0^\ast (L_0(G_0))) = f_0^\ast\chi^{-1}(L_0(G_0))\).

We have the following functional properties:

  1. \(\mathscr{F}(\chi,f_0)\) is bimultiplicative in \(\chi\) and \(f_0\):

    \begin{align*} \tag{1.7.1} \mathscr{F}(\chi,f_0'\cdot f_0'') &= \mathscr{F}(\chi,f_0')\otimes \mathscr{F}(\chi,f_0'') \text{,} \\ \tag{1.7.2} \mathscr{F}(\chi'\chi'',\mathscr{F}_0) &= \mathscr{F}(\chi',f_0)\otimes \mathscr{F}(\chi'',f_0) \text{.} \end{align*}

    This results from the definition of \(\chi(T)\), for \(T\) a torsor, attached to (1.3.1) for (1.7.1).

  2. For \(g_0:Y_0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X_0\) a morphism, we have

    \[ \tag{1.7.3} \mathscr{F}(\chi,f_0\circ g_0) = g_0^\ast \mathscr{F}(\chi,f_0) \text{.} \]

    In particular, \(\mathscr{F}(\chi,f_0) = f_0^\ast \mathscr{F}(\chi)\), where \(\mathscr{F}(\chi)\) designates \(\mathscr{F}(\chi,\operatorname{id}_{G_0})\),

  3. For \(u_0:G_0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H_0\) a morphism, and \(\chi:H_0(\mathbb{F}_q) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} E_\lambda^\times\), we have

    \[ \tag{1.7.4} \mathscr{F}(\chi,u_0 f_0) = \mathscr{F}(\chi u_0,f_0) \text{.} \]

  4. For \(G_0=\prod_{i\in I} G_0^i\), \(\chi\) of coordinates \(\chi_i\) (\(i\in I\)) and \(f_0\) of coordinates \(f_0^i\), it results from (1.7.1) to (1.7.4) that

    \[ \tag{1.7.5} \mathscr{F}(\chi,f_0) = \bigotimes_{i\in I} \mathscr{F}(\chi_i,f_0^i) \text{.} \]

Note \(\chi^{-1}\) in the definition 1.7. It ensures that, for \(x\in X^F\), we have on \(\mathscr{F}(\chi,f_0)_x\)

\[ \tag{1.7.6} F_x^\ast = \chi f_0(x) \]

(use only the fiber in \(x\) of morphism (1.2.2), for \(\rho=\chi^{-1}\), switch to \(F_x^\ast\), and (1.5.1)).

If \(\mathscr{F}(\chi,f_0)_1\) is the sheaf deduced from \(\mathscr{F}(\chi,f_0)\) by extension of the base field to \(\mathbb{F}_{q^n}\), it is deduced from (1.6.2) that

\[ \tag{1.7.7} \mathscr{F}(\chi,f_0)_1 = \mathscr{F}(\chi\circ N,f_1) \text{.} \]

1.8 Abuse of ratings

(i) If \(\Xi\) is a notation for the compound application \(\chi f_0:X_0(\mathbb{F}_q) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} E_\lambda^\times\), we will sometimes write \(\mathscr{F}(\Xi)\) instead of \(\mathscr{F}(\chi,f_0)\).1.7.1) to (1.7.5There is little risk of ambiguity, e.g.:

  1. \(\mathscr{F}(\chi)\) for \(\mathscr{F}(\chi,\operatorname{id}_{G_0})\) (notations already used in 1.7(b));

  2. in writing \(\mathscr{F}(\chi f_0)\) for \(\mathscr{F}(\chi,f_0)\);

  3. with the ratings of 1.7d), we write \(\mathscr{F}(\prod \chi_i f_0^i)\) for \(\mathscr{F}(\chi,f))\).

With this notation, (1.7.4) expresses that the writing \(\mathscr{F}(\chi u_0 f_0)\) is not ambiguous, (1.7.1) (1.7.2) (1.7.5) express a multiplicity of \(\mathscr{F}(\Xi)\) in \(\Xi\), and (1.7.6) rewrites \(F_x^\ast = \Xi(x)\) on \(\mathscr{F}(\Xi)_x\).

(ii) If \(X\) is a scheme on an extension \(k\) of the \(\mathbb{F}_q\), and that \(f\) is a morphism of \(X\) in \(G_0\otimes_{\mathbb{F}_q} k\), we will still note \(\mathscr{F}(\chi,f)\), \(\mathscr{F}(\chi f)\), or \(\mathscr{F}(\chi)\) (for \(f\) inclusion) the reciprocal image by \(f\) of the \(E_\lambda\)-sheaf deducted from \(\chi^{-1}(L_0(G))\) by extension of scalar \(\mathbb{F}_q\) to \(k\). If \(f_0\) is the compound \(X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G_0\otimes_{\mathbb{F}_q} k \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G_0\), it's still the \(\mathscr{F}(\chi,f_0)\) of the 1.7. For \(k\) is a finite extension of \(\mathbb{F}_q\), we have \(\mathscr{F}(\chi f) = \chi(\chi N_{k/\mathbb{F}_q} f)\).

Applicant (1.1.1) to (1.7.6) and (1.7.7), there are:

Theorem 1.9

Be \(S_0\) a separate scheme of finite type on \(\mathbb{F}_q\), \(G_0\) a related commutative algebraic group on \(\mathbb{F}_q\), \(f_0:S_0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G_0\) a morphism and \(\chi:G_0(\mathbb{F}_q) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} E_\lambda^\times\).

\[ \tag{1.9.1} \sum_{s\in S_0(\mathbb{F}_q)} \chi f_0(s) = \operatorname{tr}(F^\ast,\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^\bullet(S,\mathscr{F}(\chi,f_0))) \]

and, for the whole \(n\geqslant 1\)

\[ \tag{1.9.2} \sum_{s\in S_0(\mathbb{F}_{q^n})} \chi\circ N_{\mathbb{F}_{q^n}/\mathbb{F}_q} f_0(s) = \operatorname{tr}({F^\ast}^n, \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^\bullet\left(S,\mathscr{F}(\chi,f_0))\right) \text{.} \]

1.9.3 Note

Let's take for \(G_0\) a product. 1.7(d) and 1.8(c) the formula (1.9.2) becomes

\[ \sum_{s\in S_0(\mathbb{F}_{q^n})} \prod_i \chi_i\circ N_{\mathbb{F}_{q^n}/\mathbb{F}_q} (f_0^i(s)) = \operatorname{tr}\Big({F^\ast}^n, \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^\bullet\Big(S,\mathscr{F}\Big(\prod_i \chi_i f_0^i\Big)\Big)\Big) \text{.} \]

1.9.4 Note

Let's take \(G_0=\{e\}\). The formula (1.9.1) becomes

\[ \left|S_0(\mathbb{F}_q)\right| = \sum_{s\in S_0(\mathbb{F}_q)} 1 = \operatorname{tr}(F^\ast,\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^\bullet(S,\mathbb{Q}_\ell)) \text{.} \]

It is rare that one can explicitly calculate the right-hand member of (1.9.1Here is a fun example, with \(G_0=\{e\}\).

1.10 Example

A non-specific projective quadric of odd dimension on \(\mathbb{F}_q\) has the same number of rational points as the projective space on \(\mathbb{F}_q\) of the same dimension.

If, on \(\mathbb{C}\), \(X\) is a quadric hypersurface not singular in the projective space \(\mathbb{P}^{2 N}\), and \(Y\) a hyperplane, we know that for \(i\leqslant 2\dim(X)=2\dim(Y)\), inclusions \(X\hookrightarrow \mathbb{P}^{2 N}\hookleftarrow Y\) induce isomorphisms (in ordinary cohomology)

\[ \xymatrix{ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(X,\mathbb{Q}) & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(\mathbb{P}^{2 N},\mathbb{Q}) \ar[r]^-\sim \ar[l]_-\sim& \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(Y,\mathbb{Q}) . } \]

By specialization, the result is that if \(X_0'\) is a non-specific quadric hypersurface in the projective space \(\mathbb{P}_0^{2 N}\) on \(\mathbb{F}_q\), and \(Y_0'\) a hyperplane, inclusions \(X_0'\hookrightarrow \mathbb{P}_0^{2 N}\hookleftarrow Y_0'\) induce isomorphisms

\[ \xymatrix{ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(X',\mathbb{Q}_\ell) & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(\mathbb{P}^{2 N},\mathbb{Q}_\ell) \ar[r]^-\sim \ar[l]_-\sim& \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(Y',\mathbb{Q}_\ell) . } \]

These isomorphisms switch to \(F^\ast\), and the trace formula is applied.

1.11

The trace formula can also be used to understand the classical formulae giving the number of rational points of the linear groups on the finite fields, and those of certain homogeneous spaces (see paragraph 8).

1.12

A dictionary is available to translate into cohomological terms than various types of classical manipulations on trigonometric sums. This dictionary will be given in paragraph 2. The cohomological statement being more "geometric," it may sometimes apply to situations where the classical argument only applies after an extension of the basic finite field. Here is an example (a particular case of a Kazhdan theorem).

Theorem 1.13 (Kazhdan)

Either \(X_0\) a scheme on \(\mathbb{F}_q\). Suppose there is an action \(\rho\) of \(\mathbb{G}_a\) on \(X\), and a morphism \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) schemes on \(\mathbb{F}\), making \(X\) a \(\mathbb{G}_a\)-torseur on \(Y\). Then the number of rational points of \(X_0\) is divisible by \(q\).

Suppose first that \(\rho\), \(Y\) and \(f\) be defined on \(\mathbb{F}_q\).

Argument classical

The fibres of \(f:X_0(\mathbb{F}_q)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y_0(\mathbb{F}_q)\) all have \(q\) elements: \(|X_0(\mathbb{F}_q)| = q\cdot |Y_0(\mathbb{F}_q)|\), hence the divisibility.

Cohomological translation

The upper direct image sheaves \(\mathsf{R}^i f_!\mathbb{Q}_\ell\) are

\[ \mathsf{R}^i f_!\mathbb{Q}_\ell = \begin{cases} \mathbb{Q}_\ell(-1) & \text{if } i = 2 \\ 0 & \text{for } i\ne 2 \end{cases} \]

The Leray spectral sequence of \(f\) thus degenerates into an isomorphism

\[ \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(X,\mathbb{Q}_\ell) = \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^{i-2}(Y,\mathbb{Q}_\ell)(-1) \text{.} \]

To understand the effect of a Tate twist on Frobenius' eigenvalues, the most convenient is to use the Galoisian perspective ([II]Report 1.8), and note that the substitution of Frobenius \(\varphi\) acts on \(\mathbb{Q}_\ell(1)\) by multiplication by \(q\). The eigenvalues of \(F^\ast\) on \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^p(X,\mathbb{Q}_\ell)\) are therefore produced by \(q\) of the eigenvalues of \(F^\ast\) acting on \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^{i-2}(Y,\mathbb{Q}_\ell)\). It is known that these are algebraic integers [16]; therefore

\[ \tag{*} \substack{\displaystyle\text{the eigenvalues of } F^\ast \text{ acting on } \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(X,\mathbb{Q}_\ell) \text{ are} \\ \displaystyle\text{Algebraic integers divisible by } q \text{.}} \]

Descent

For \(n\) suitable, it can be assumed that \(\rho\), \(Y\) and \(f\) are defined on \(\mathbb{F}_{q^n}\). The morphism of Frobenius relating to \(\mathbb{F}_{q^n}\) being the power \(n\)-th of that relating to \(\mathbb{F}_q\), the statement (*) for the scheme on \(\mathbb{F}_{q^n}\) deducted from \(X_0\) by extension of scalar provides us with:

\[ \tag{**} \substack{\displaystyle\text{Powers } n \text{- the same of the eigenvalues of } F^\ast \text{ acting on} \\ \displaystyle\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(X,\mathbb{Q}_\ell) \text{ are algebraic integers divisible by } q^n \text{.}} \]

This assertion implies (*) The number of rational points of \(X_0\), i.e. \(\sum (-1)^i \operatorname{tr}(F^\ast,\underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(X,\mathbb{Q}_\ell))\), is therefore algebraic integer divisible by \(q\). This implies that it is divisible by \(q\) as a rational integer.

1.14

The formula 1.9.3 control the dependence in \(n\) of the trigonometric sum to the left member. It involves identities between a trigonometric sum and those deduced from it by "extension of the scalar." The most famous of these identities is that of Hasse-Davenport: either \(\chi:\mathbb{F}_q^\times \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbb{C}^\times\) and \(\psi:\mathbb{F}_q\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbb{C}^\times\) of the characters, \(\psi\) not trivial, and let's define the sum of Gauss \(\tau(\chi,\psi)\) by

\[ \tag{1.14.1} \tau(\chi,\psi) = - \sum_{x\in \mathbb{F}_q^\times} \psi(x) \chi^{-1}(x) \text{.} \]
Theorem 1.15 (Hasse-Davenport)

We've got

\[ \tag{1.15.1} \tau\left(\chi\circ N_{\mathbb{F}_{q^n}/\mathbb{F}_q},\psi\circ \operatorname{tr}_{\mathbb{F}_{q^n}/\mathbb{F}_q}\right) = \tau(\chi,\psi)^n \text{.} \]

Is \(E\subset \mathbb{C}\) a field of numbers containing the values of \(\chi\) and \(\psi\), and \(E_\lambda\) completed with \(E\) in a characteristic place \(\ell\ne p\). Identity (1.15.1) is an identity in \(E\). It is the same to prove it in \(\mathbb{C}\), or \(E_\lambda\). We will prove it in \(E_\lambda\), by looking at \(\chi\) and \(\psi\) as in \(E_\lambda^\times\).

We're applying 1.9.3 for \(X_0=\mathbb{G}_m\) on \(\mathbb{F}_q\) and \(G_0=\mathbb{G}_m\times \mathbb{G}_a\). \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(\mathbb{G}_m,\mathscr{F}(\chi^{-1}\psi))\) are void for \(i\ne 1\), and \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^1\) is of size \(1\) (4.2). Based on 1.9.3, \(\tau(\chi\circ N_{\mathbb{F}_{q^n}/\mathbb{F}_q},\psi\circ \operatorname{tr}_{\mathbb{F}_{q^n}/\mathbb{F}_q})\) is the only eigenvalue of \((F^\ast)^n\) on \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^1\), and (1.15.1) as a result.

Similar examples are given in Weil [43].

1.16

A \(E_\lambda\)-sheaf \(\mathscr{F}_0\) on \(X-0\) is said punctually weight \(n\) if for any closed point \(x\in |X_0|\), the eigenvalues of \(F_x^\ast\) ([II]Report 1.2) are algebraic numbers with all complex conjugates of absolute value \(q_x^{n/2}\), where \(q_x\) is the number of elements of \(k(x)\). The following theorem will be shown in [15].

Theorem 1.17

If \(\mathscr{F}_0\) is punctually of weight \(n\), for any eigenvalue \(\alpha\) of endomorphism \(F^\ast\) of \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(X,\mathscr{F})\), there is an integer \(m\leqslant n+i\) such that the complex conjugates of \(\alpha\) are all absolute values \(q^{m/2}\).

The bundles considered in 1.9 are of weight \(0\). The theorem therefore provides for the sum 1.9.1 (it is rather, for all its complex conjugates) the increase

\[ \tag{1.17.1} \left|\sum_{s\in S_0(\mathbb{F}_q)} \prod_i \chi_i(f^i(s)) \right| \leqslant \sum_i \dim \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i\left(S,\mathscr{F}\left(\prod \chi_i f^i\right)\right)\cdot q^{i/2} \text{.} \]

1.18 Remarks

  1. If \(\dim S=n\), for all \(E_\lambda\)-sheaf \(\mathscr{F}\) on \(S\), we have

    \[ \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(S,\mathscr{F}) = 0 \qquad \text{for } i\notin [0,2n] \text{.} \]

  2. The group \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^0(X,\mathscr{F})\) is the group of the global sections of \(\mathscr{F}\) on \(S\) with its compact support. If \(S\) is related, not complete and \(\mathscr{F}\) smooth, well if \(S\) is related, \(\mathscr{F}\) smooth of rank one, and not constant, this group is null.

  3. If \(S\) is purely smooth in size \(n\), and \(\mathscr{F}\) smooth, Poincaré's duality says that the vector spaces \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(X,\mathscr{F})\) and \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^{2n-i}(X,\mathscr{F}^\vee)(2n)\) are due to each other (for the effect of a tate twist on Frobenius' eigenvalues, see \(2\) part of the proof of 1.13).

  4. If \(\dim S=n\), Poincaré's duality makes it possible to calculate as a result \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^{2n}(S,\mathscr{F})\): we take an open \(U\) of \(S_\text{red}\), whose complement is of dimension \(<n\), pure smooth of dimension \(n\), and on which \(\mathscr{F}\) is smooth. We then have \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^{2n}(U,\mathscr{F}) \xrightarrow{\sim} \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^{2n}(S,\mathscr{F})\), because in the exact long sequence of cohomology, \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(S\setminus U,\mathscr{F}) = 0\) for \(i=2n,2n-1\). By Poincaré, we have therefore

    \[ \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^{2n}(S,\mathscr{F}) \xrightarrow{\sim} \left(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^0(U,\mathscr{F}^\vee)(2n)\right)^\vee \text{.} \]

    Suppose \(U\) related, and be \(u\) a geometric point of \(U\). The "local system" \(\mathscr{F}\) corresponds to a representation of \(\pi_1(U,u)\) on \(\mathscr{F}_u\), and \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^0(U,\mathscr{F}^\vee)\) is \((\mathscr{F}_u^\vee)^{\pi_1(U,u)}=((\mathscr{F}_u)_{\pi_1(U,u)})^\vee\) (dual of variable corners).

    \[ \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^{2n}(S,\mathscr{F}) \simeq (\mathscr{F}_u)_{\pi_1(U,u)}(-2n) \text{.} \]

  5. These remarks often allow the calculation of \(b_0\) and \(b_{2n}\). Calculating the others \(b_i\) can be difficult. If \(b_{2n}=0\) and \(b_{2n-1}=0\), the increase (1.17.1) is an increase to the Lang-Weil, with, for \(q\) large, a gain in \(\sqrt q\) compared to the three-way increase in \(O(q^n)\). However, it should be noted that the increase (1.17.1) does not formally imply the three-way mark-up

    \[ \left|\sum_{s\in S_0(\mathbb{F}_q)} \prod_i \chi_i(f^i(s))\right| \leqslant \left| S_0(\mathbb{F}_q)\right| \text{,} \]

    It may therefore be useful to take into account (cf. the proof of 3.8).

I explain below a method to prove that \(b_i=0\) for \(i\ne n\). When it applies, it provides an estimate in \(O(q^{n/2}\). The constant implicit in \(0\) is \(b_n=(-1)^n \chi(S,\mathscr{F}(\prod \chi_i f^i))\) can be difficult to calculate.

Proposition 1.19

It is assumed that \(\overline{X}\) a proper scheme on an algbrically enclosed field \(k\), \(j:X\hookrightarrow bar X\) an open \(X\) and \(\mathscr{F}\) a \(E_\lambda\)-sheaves on \(X\).

  1. \((j_\ast\mathscr{F})_x = 0\) for \(x\in \overline{X}\setminus X\), i.e. \(j_!\mathscr{F}\xrightarrow{\sim} j_\ast\mathscr{F}\);

  2. \(\mathsf{R}^i j_\ast \mathscr{F} = 0\) for \(i>0\).

So, the applications

\[ \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(X,\mathscr{F}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(X,\mathscr{F}) \]

are isomorphisms.

Assumptions a) b) mean that \(j_! \mathscr{F} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathsf{R} j_\ast \mathscr{F}\), hence

\[ \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(X,\mathscr{F}) = \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(\overline{X},j_!\mathscr{F}) \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathsf{H}^i(\overline{X},\mathsf{R} j_\ast \mathscr{F}) = \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(X,\mathscr{F}) \text{.} \]

In other words, Leray's spectral sequence for \(j\)

\[ E_2^{pq} = \underline{\operatorname{H}}^p(\overline{X},\mathsf{R}^q j_\ast \mathscr{F}) \Rightarrow \underline{\operatorname{H}}^{p+q}(X,\mathscr{F}) \]

degenerates, by (b) in isomorphisms

\[ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(\overline{X},j_\ast\mathscr{F}) \xrightarrow{\sim} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(X,\mathscr{F}) \text{,} \]

while, under (a)

\[ \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(X,\mathscr{F}) = \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(\overline{X},j_!\mathscr{F}) \xrightarrow{\sim} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(\overline{X},j_\ast\mathscr{F}) \text{.} \]

Example

If \(X\) is a curve, or if \(\overline{X}\) is smooth, \(X\) the complement of a divisor with normal crosses and \(\mathscr{F}\) moderately branched, the assumption (a) of 1.19 (no invariants under local monodromy) implies hypothesis b).

Proposition 1.20

Been \(X_0\) a purely dimensional smooth affine scheme \(n\) on \(\mathbb{F}_q\) and \(\mathscr{F}_0\) one \(E_\lambda\)-Smooth, punctually smooth sheaf of weight \(m\) on \(X_0\). It is assumed that applications \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(X,\mathscr{F}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(X,\mathscr{F})\) are isomorphisms (cf. 1.17) So, \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(X,\mathscr{F}) = 0\) for \(i\ne n\), and the complex conjugates of the eigenvalues of \(F^\ast\) on \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^n(X,\mathscr{F})\) are of absolute value \(q^{(m+n)/2}\).

The duality of Poincaré puts into duality \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(X,\mathscr{F})\) (resp. \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(X,\mathscr{F}^\vee)\)) with \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^{2n-i}(X,\mathscr{F}^\vee(n))\) (resp. \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^{2n-i}(X,\mathscr{F}(n))\)). [2], \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^i\) without support is invalid for \(i>n\). By duality, \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i\) are invalid for \(i<n\); since \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(X,\mathscr{F}) \xrightarrow{\sim} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(X,\mathscr{F})\), these groups are invalid for \(i\ne n\). 1.15 to \(\mathscr{F}_0\) and to \(\mathscr{F}_0^\vee(n)\) (of peppers) \(-m-2n\)) It is found that complex conjugates \(\alpha\) of the eigenvalues of \(F^\ast\) on \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^n(X,\mathscr{F}) = \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^n(X,\mathscr{F}^\vee(n))^\vee\) check

\begin{align*} |\alpha| &\leqslant q^{(m+n)/2} && \text{and} \\ |\alpha^{-1}| &\leqslant q^{((-m-2n)+n)/2} = q^{-(m+n)/2} \text{,} \end{align*}

Hence the assertion.

1.21 Note

The last argument shows that if \(X_0\) is a smooth separate pattern on \(\mathbb{F}_q\), that \(\mathscr{F}_0\) is a \(E_\lambda\)-smooth smooth pattern of weight \(m\) on \(X_0\) and that \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(X,\mathscr{F}) \hookrightarrow \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(X,\mathscr{F})\), then the complex conjugates of the eigenvalues of \(F^\ast\) on \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(X,\mathscr{F})\) are of absolute value \(q^{(m+i)/2}\).

Appendix. Lang isogeny in the non-commutative case

1.22

Be \(G-0\) a related algebraic group on \(\mathbb{F}_q\) and \(\mathfrak{L}\) the morphism \(x\mapsto F x\cdot x^{-1}\) of \(G-0\) in itself. It is an orphism of \(G_0\)-homogeneous spaces, if at the source we act \(G-0\) by translation to the left \(x\mapsto (y\mapsto x y)\), for the purpose by \(x\mapsto (y\mapsto F x\cdot y\cdot x^{-1}\)). We have \(\mathfrak{L}(xy) = \mathfrak{L}(x)\) for \(y\in G_0(\mathbb{F}_q)\), and \(\mathfrak{L}\) induces isomorphism \(G_0/G_0(\mathbb{F}_q)\xrightarrow{\sim} G_0\).

As in the commutative case, \(\mathfrak{L}\) makes \(G_0\) a \(G_0(\mathbb{F}_q)\)-torseur on \(G_0\), the Lang torsor \(L\). If \(\rho:G_0(\mathbb{F}_q) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{gl}(n,E_\lambda)\) is a linear representation of \(G_0(\mathbb{F}_q)\), we will note \(\mathscr{F}(\rho)\) the \(E_\lambda\)-sheaf \(\rho(L)\) (1.2).

1.23

Either \(\gamma\in G^F=G_0(\mathbb{F}_q)\), and let's calculate the endomorphism \(F^\ast\) of \(\mathscr{F}(\rho)_\gamma\). Either \(g\in G\) such as \(\mathfrak{L}(g)=\gamma\), hence a landmark \(\rho(g)\in \operatorname{Isom}(E_\lambda^n,\mathscr{F}(\rho)_\gamma)\). For \(e\in E_\lambda^n\), we have

\[ F(\rho(g)(e)) = \rho(F g)(e) = \rho(\gamma g)(e) = \rho(g\cdot g^{-1}\gamma g)(e) \text{.} \]

We will see that \(g^{-1} \gamma g\in G_0(\mathbb{F}_q)\), from which

\[ F(\rho(g)(e)) = \rho(g) \rho(g^{-1}\gamma g)(e) \]

and

\[ \tag{1.23.1} \rho(g)^{-1} (F_\gamma^\ast)^{-1} \rho(g) = \rho(g^{-1} \gamma g) \text{:} \]

\(\rho(g)\) identifies the opposite of \(F^\ast\) en \(\gamma\) to automorphism \(\rho(g^{-1} \gamma g)\) de \(E_\lambda^n\). It remains to be understood what \(g^{-1} \gamma g\) is.

Lemma 1.24

  1. If \(F g\cdot g^{-1} \in G_0(\mathbb{F}_q)\), we have \(g^{-1} (F g\cdot g^{-1}) g = g^{-1} F g\in G_0(\mathbb{F}_q)\).

  2. Be \(\gamma\in G_0(\mathbb{F}_q)\), and \(g\) such as \(F g\cdot g^{-1} = \gamma\). The conjugation class of \(\gamma'=g^{-1} Fg\) in \(G_0(\mathbb{F}_q)\) depends only on that of \(\gamma\).

  3. The application induced by \(\gamma\mapsto \gamma'\), of the set \(G_0(\mathbb{F}_q)^\natural\) of the conjugation classes of \(G_0(\mathbb{F}_q)\) in itself, is bijective. Its inverse is the application for the opposite group.

(i) If \(\gamma= F g\cdot g^{-1}\in G_0(\mathbb{F}_q)\), the identities \(F g=\gamma g\) and \(F\gamma=\gamma\) give \(F(g^{-1} F g) = (F g)^{-1} F F g = g^{-1} \gamma^{-1} F(\gamma g) = g^{-1} \gamma^{-1} F \gamma F g = g^{-1} F g\), so that \(g^{-1} F g \in G_0(\mathbb{F}_q)\).

(ii) For \(\alpha,\gamma\in G_0(\mathbb{F}_q)\), \(g\) such as \(F g\cdot g^{-1} = \gamma\) form a right class under \(G_0(\mathbb{F}_q)\), and if \(F g\cdot g^{-1} = \gamma\), we have \(F(\alpha g \alpha^{-1})(\alpha g \alpha^{-1})^{-1} = \alpha \gamma\alpha^{-1}\). For \(\gamma\) running a conjugation class in \(G_0(\mathbb{F}_q)\), \(g\) such as \(F g\cdot g^{-1} = \gamma\) thus travel a double class under \(G_0(\mathbb{F}_q)\), and \(\gamma'= g^{-1} F g\) travels through a conjugation class.

(iii) Finally, the opposite of \(F g\cdot g^{-1}\mapsto g^{-1} F g\) is \(g^{-1} F g\mapsto F g\cdot g^{-1}\).

1.25

The formula (1.23.1) can be read, briefly: \(F^\ast\) en \(\gamma\) is \(\rho(\gamma')^{-1}\). If \(\gamma\) belongs to the neutral component of its centralizer, we can take \(g\) in \(Z(\gamma)\). We then have \(\gamma=\gamma'\). If this condition is not met, \(\gamma\) and \(\gamma'\) may not be combined.

1.26 An example

For \(G_0=\operatorname{SL}(2)\), \(q\) odd, \(\alpha\in F_q^\times\setminus {\mathbb{F}_q^\times}^2\) and \(\overline{\gamma}\) the conjugation class of \(-1(1+N)\), with \(N^2=0\), \(\overline{\gamma}'\) is the conjugation class of \(-1\cdot (1+\alpha N)\), so \(\overline{\gamma}'\ne \overline{\gamma}\) if \(N\ne 0\).

blocks.html: 1 Principles

Be \(X_0\) a separate scheme of finite type on \(\mathbb{F}_q\), \(E_\lambda\) a finite extension of \(\mathbb{Q}_\ell\) and \(\mathscr{F}_0\) a \(E_\lambda\)-sheaf on \(X-0\). The formula of traces says that
\[ \tag{1.1.1} \sum_{x\in X^F} \operatorname{tr}(F_x^\ast,\mathscr{F}) = \sum (-1)^i \operatorname{tr}\left(F^\ast,\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(X,\mathscr{F})\right) \text{.} \]
With notation 0.3, the right-hand member is simply written \(\operatorname{tr}(F^\ast,\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^\bullet(X,\mathscr{F}))\).

This trace formula for \(E_\lambda\)-sheaves can be deduced from [II]Report 4.10 limit passage (cf. [II]Report 4.11 to 4.13), or be deducted from the trace formula for \(\mathbb{Q}_\ell\)-sheaves ([II]Report 3.2) by the method of ([III]Functions \(L\) mod. \(\ell^n\), 4.3).
We will interpret various trigonometrics as the left-hand member of (1.1.1), for \(X_0\) and \(\mathscr{F}_0\) suitable.

Either \(A\) a finite group. \(A\)-torsor on a diagram \(X\) is a sheaf \(T\) on \(X\), equipped with a right-hand action of \(A\), which, locally (for the live topology) on \(X\), is isomorphic to the constant sheaf \(A\) on which \(A\) acts by translation to the right.

If \(\tau:A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) is a homomorphism, and \(T\) a \(A\)-torseur, there is a unique isomorphism near one and only one \(B\)-torseur \(\tau(T)\), with sheaf morphism \(\tau:T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \tau(T)\) such as
\[ \tag{1.2.1} \tau(t a) = \tau(t) \tau(a)\text{.} \]

If \(T\) is a \(A\)-torsor on \(X\), and \(\rho\) a linear representation of \(A\): \(\rho:A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{gl}(V)\) (\(V\) a vector space of finite dimension on \(E_\lambda\)), there is a single isomorphism near a single \(E_\lambda\)-sheaf \(\mathscr{F}\), smooth row \(\dim(V)\), equipped with sheaf morphism
\[ \tag{1.2.2} \rho:T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{Isom}}(V,\mathscr{F}) \text{.} \]

\(\rho(t a) = \rho(t) \rho(a)\). It is noted \(\rho(T)\). For \(\tau:A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) and \(\rho\) a representation of \(B\), one has canonical isomorphism \(\rho(\tau(T)) = (\rho\tau)(T)\).

This paragraph (except the appendix) only considers the case where \(A\) is commutative and \(V=E_\lambda\): \(\rho\) is a character \(A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} E_\lambda^\times\), and \(\rho(T)\) a \(E_\lambda\)-smoothed sheaf of rank one. Morphism (1.2.2) interprets itself as a morphism everywhere non-zero
\[ \tag{1.2.3} \rho:T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \rho(T) \]
such as \(\rho(t a) = \rho(a)\rho(t)\).
Be \(S\) a scheme, \(G\) a scheme in a commutative group on \(S\), and \(G'\) an extension of scheme in commutative groups on \(S\)
\[ \xymatrix{ 0 \ar[r] & A \ar[r] & G' \ar[r]^-\pi & G \ar[r] & 0 . } \]
The sheaf \(T\) of the local sections of \(\pi\) is then a \(A\)-torse on \(G\).
If \(X\) is a scheme on \(S\), and \(f,g\) two \(S\)-morphisms of \(X\) in \(G\), because \(T\) is defined by an extension, one has canonical isomorphism
\[ \tag{1.3.1} (f+g)^\ast T = f^\ast T + g^\ast T \text{.} \]
On the left, \(f+g\) is the sum of \(f\) and \(g\), within the meaning of group law \(G\): on the right, \(+\) means a sum of torsors.

If \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G\) is factored by \(G'\), \(f^\ast T\) is trivial (and a factorization gives a trivialization); to isomorphism (not unique) near \(A\)-torseur \(f^\ast T\) depends only on the image of \(f\) in \(\hom_S(X,G)/\pi\hom_S(X,G')\): it is given by morphism \(\partial\) in the exact sequence
\[ \xymatrix{ \hom(X,G') \ar[r] & \hom(X,G) \ar[r]^-\partial & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(X,A) . } \]

We will apply these constructions to Kummer's exact suites \(0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \boldsymbol{\mu}_n \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbb{G}_m \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbb{G}_m \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0\), and Lang's torsors.
1.4. The Lang torsor
Either \(G_0\) a related commutative algebraic group on \(\mathbb{F}_q\) (for non- commutative cases, see Appendix). The group law is noted multiple times. Lang's isogeny
\[ \mathfrak{L}:G_0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G_0: x\mapsto F x\cdot x^{-1} \]

is spread; its image, an open sub-group of \(G_0\), can only be \(G_0\) itself; its kernel is the finite group \(G_0(\mathbb{F}_q)\). Lang torsor \(L\) is the \(G_0(\mathbb{F}_q)\)-torsor on \(G_0\) defined by the exact later
\[ \tag{1.4.1} \xymatrix{ 0 \ar[r] & G_0(\mathbb{F}_q) \ar[r] & G_0 \ar[r]^-{\mathfrak{L}} & G_0 \ar[r] & 0 . } \]
Rating

Note again \(L\) and \(\mathfrak{L}\) (or \(L_{(q)}\) and \(\mathfrak{L}_{(q)}\)) the items deducted from \(L\) and \(\mathfrak{L}\) by extension of the base field. If necessary, an index shall be given \(0\) or \(1\).
Examples
For \(G_0=\mathbb{G}_a\) or \(\mathbb{G}_m\), the suite(1.4.1) write

\begin{align*} \tag{1.4.2} &\xymatrix{ 0 \ar[r] & \mathbb{F}_q \ar[r] & \mathbb{G}_a \ar[r]^-{x^q-x} & \mathbb{G}_a \ar[r] & 0 } \\ \tag{1.4.3} &\xymatrix{ 0 \ar[r] & \boldsymbol{\mu}_{q-1} \ar[r] & \mathbb{G}_m \ar[r]^-{x^{q-1}} & \mathbb{G}_m \ar[r] & 0 } \end{align*}

Let's calculate the endomorphism \(F^\ast\) of the Lang torsor fiber in \(\gamma\in G^F=G_0(\mathbb{F}_q)\). If \(g\in \mathfrak{L}^{-1}(\gamma)\subset G\), we have \(F g = F g\cdot g^{-1}\cdot g = \gamma g\). From then on ([II]Report, 1.2)
\[ \tag{1.5.1} \text{on } L_0(G_0)_\gamma\simeq \mathfrak{L}^{-1}(\gamma) \text{, } F^\ast \text{ is } g\mapsto g\gamma^{-1} \text{.} \]

On \(\mathbb{F}_{q^n}\), the identity \(F_{(q^n)}=F_{(q)}^n\) between \(G_1\) implies that \(\mathfrak{L}_{(q^n)}=\mathfrak{L}_{(q)}\circ \prod_{i=0}^{n-1} F_{(q)}^i\). On \(G_0(\mathbb{F}_{q^n})\), \(F_{(q)}^i\) acts as the element \(x\mapsto x^{q^i}\) of \(\operatorname{Gal}(\mathbb{F}_{q^n}/\mathbb{F}_q)\). On \(G_0(\mathbb{F}_{q^n})\), \(\prod F_{(q)}^i\) is therefore composed of the standard \(G_0(\mathbb{F}_{q^n})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G_0(\mathbb{F}_q)\) and inclusion \(G_0(\mathbb{F}_q)\subset G_0(\mathbb{F}_{q^n})\): the diagram

\[ \tag{1.6.1} \xymatrix{ 0 \ar[r] & G_0(\mathbb{F}_{q^n}) \ar[r] \ar[d]^-N & G_1 \ar[r]^-{\mathfrak{L}_{(q^n)}} \ar[d]^-{\prod_{i=0}^{n-1} F_{(q)}^i} & G_1 \ar[r] \ar@{=}[d] & 0 \\ 0 \ar[r] & G_0(\mathbb{F}_q) \ar[r] & G_q \ar[r]^-{\mathfrak{L}_{(q)}} & G_1 \ar[r] & 0 } \]
In the language of the torsors, this provides canonical isomorphism between \(G_0(\mathbb{F}_q)\)-torsors on \(G_1\)
\[ \tag{1.6.2} N L_{(q^n)} = L_{(q)} \text{.} \]

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1.7

Be \(f_0:X_0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G_0\) a morphism and \(\chi:G_0(\mathbb{F}_q)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} E_\lambda^\times\) a character. We pose \(\mathscr{F}(\chi,f_0) = \chi^{-1}(f_0^\ast (L_0(G_0))) = f_0^\ast\chi^{-1}(L_0(G_0))\).
We have the following functional properties:

1. \(\mathscr{F}(\chi,f_0)\) is bimultiplicative in \(\chi\) and \(f_0\):

    \begin{align*} \tag{1.7.1} \mathscr{F}(\chi,f_0'\cdot f_0'') &= \mathscr{F}(\chi,f_0')\otimes \mathscr{F}(\chi,f_0'') \text{,} \\ \tag{1.7.2} \mathscr{F}(\chi'\chi'',\mathscr{F}_0) &= \mathscr{F}(\chi',f_0)\otimes \mathscr{F}(\chi'',f_0) \text{.} \end{align*}

    This results from the definition of \(\chi(T)\), for \(T\) a torsor, attached to (1.3.1) for (1.7.1).

  2. For \(g_0:Y_0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X_0\) a morphism, we have

    \[ \tag{1.7.3} \mathscr{F}(\chi,f_0\circ g_0) = g_0^\ast \mathscr{F}(\chi,f_0) \text{.} \]

    In particular, \(\mathscr{F}(\chi,f_0) = f_0^\ast \mathscr{F}(\chi)\), where \(\mathscr{F}(\chi)\) designates \(\mathscr{F}(\chi,\operatorname{id}_{G_0})\),

  3. For \(u_0:G_0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H_0\) a morphism, and \(\chi:H_0(\mathbb{F}_q) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} E_\lambda^\times\), we have

    \[ \tag{1.7.4} \mathscr{F}(\chi,u_0 f_0) = \mathscr{F}(\chi u_0,f_0) \text{.} \]

  4. For \(G_0=\prod_{i\in I} G_0^i\), \(\chi\) of coordinates \(\chi_i\) (\(i\in I\)) and \(f_0\) of coordinates \(f_0^i\), it results from (1.7.1) to (1.7.4) that

    \[ \tag{1.7.5} \mathscr{F}(\chi,f_0) = \bigotimes_{i\in I} \mathscr{F}(\chi_i,f_0^i) \text{.} \]
Note \(\chi^{-1}\) in the definition 1.7. It ensures that, for \(x\in X^F\), we have on \(\mathscr{F}(\chi,f_0)_x\)
\[ \tag{1.7.6} F_x^\ast = \chi f_0(x) \]
(use only the fiber in \(x\) of morphism (1.2.2), for \(\rho=\chi^{-1}\), switch to \(F_x^\ast\), and (1.5.1)).
If \(\mathscr{F}(\chi,f_0)_1\) is the sheaf deduced from \(\mathscr{F}(\chi,f_0)\) by extension of the base field to \(\mathbb{F}_{q^n}\), it is deduced from (1.6.2) that
\[ \tag{1.7.7} \mathscr{F}(\chi,f_0)_1 = \mathscr{F}(\chi\circ N,f_1) \text{.} \]
1.8 Abuse of ratings

(i) If \(\Xi\) is a notation for the compound application \(\chi f_0:X_0(\mathbb{F}_q) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} E_\lambda^\times\), we will sometimes write \(\mathscr{F}(\Xi)\) instead of \(\mathscr{F}(\chi,f_0)\).1.7.1) to (1.7.5There is little risk of ambiguity, e.g.:

1. \(\mathscr{F}(\chi)\) for \(\mathscr{F}(\chi,\operatorname{id}_{G_0})\) (notations already used in 1.7(b));

  2. in writing \(\mathscr{F}(\chi f_0)\) for \(\mathscr{F}(\chi,f_0)\);

  3. with the ratings of 1.7d), we write \(\mathscr{F}(\prod \chi_i f_0^i)\) for \(\mathscr{F}(\chi,f))\).

With this notation, (1.7.4) expresses that the writing \(\mathscr{F}(\chi u_0 f_0)\) is not ambiguous, (1.7.1) (1.7.2) (1.7.5) express a multiplicity of \(\mathscr{F}(\Xi)\) in \(\Xi\), and (1.7.6) rewrites \(F_x^\ast = \Xi(x)\) on \(\mathscr{F}(\Xi)_x\).

(ii) If \(X\) is a scheme on an extension \(k\) of the \(\mathbb{F}_q\), and that \(f\) is a morphism of \(X\) in \(G_0\otimes_{\mathbb{F}_q} k\), we will still note \(\mathscr{F}(\chi,f)\), \(\mathscr{F}(\chi f)\), or \(\mathscr{F}(\chi)\) (for \(f\) inclusion) the reciprocal image by \(f\) of the \(E_\lambda\)-sheaf deducted from \(\chi^{-1}(L_0(G))\) by extension of scalar \(\mathbb{F}_q\) to \(k\). If \(f_0\) is the compound \(X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G_0\otimes_{\mathbb{F}_q} k \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G_0\), it's still the \(\mathscr{F}(\chi,f_0)\) of the 1.7. For \(k\) is a finite extension of \(\mathbb{F}_q\), we have \(\mathscr{F}(\chi f) = \chi(\chi N_{k/\mathbb{F}_q} f)\).
Applicant (1.1.1) to (1.7.6) and (1.7.7), there are:

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 1.9

Be \(S_0\) a separate scheme of finite type on \(\mathbb{F}_q\), \(G_0\) a related commutative algebraic group on \(\mathbb{F}_q\), \(f_0:S_0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G_0\) a morphism and \(\chi:G_0(\mathbb{F}_q) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} E_\lambda^\times\).

\[ \tag{1.9.1} \sum_{s\in S_0(\mathbb{F}_q)} \chi f_0(s) = \operatorname{tr}(F^\ast,\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^\bullet(S,\mathscr{F}(\chi,f_0))) \]

and, for the whole \(n\geqslant 1\)

\[ \tag{1.9.2} \sum_{s\in S_0(\mathbb{F}_{q^n})} \chi\circ N_{\mathbb{F}_{q^n}/\mathbb{F}_q} f_0(s) = \operatorname{tr}({F^\ast}^n, \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^\bullet\left(S,\mathscr{F}(\chi,f_0))\right) \text{.} \]
Let's take for \(G_0\) a product. 1.7(d) and 1.8(c) the formula (1.9.2) becomes

\[ \sum_{s\in S_0(\mathbb{F}_{q^n})} \prod_i \chi_i\circ N_{\mathbb{F}_{q^n}/\mathbb{F}_q} (f_0^i(s)) = \operatorname{tr}\Big({F^\ast}^n, \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^\bullet\Big(S,\mathscr{F}\Big(\prod_i \chi_i f_0^i\Big)\Big)\Big) \text{.} \]
Let's take \(G_0=\{e\}\). The formula (1.9.1) becomes
\[ \left|S_0(\mathbb{F}_q)\right| = \sum_{s\in S_0(\mathbb{F}_q)} 1 = \operatorname{tr}(F^\ast,\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^\bullet(S,\mathbb{Q}_\ell)) \text{.} \]
It is rare that one can explicitly calculate the right-hand member of (1.9.1Here is a fun example, with \(G_0=\{e\}\).
1.10 Example
A non-specific projective quadric of odd dimension on \(\mathbb{F}_q\) has the same number of rational points as the projective space on \(\mathbb{F}_q\) of the same dimension.

If, on \(\mathbb{C}\), \(X\) is a quadric hypersurface not singular in the projective space \(\mathbb{P}^{2 N}\), and \(Y\) a hyperplane, we know that for \(i\leqslant 2\dim(X)=2\dim(Y)\), inclusions \(X\hookrightarrow \mathbb{P}^{2 N}\hookleftarrow Y\) induce isomorphisms (in ordinary cohomology)
\[ \xymatrix{ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(X,\mathbb{Q}) & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(\mathbb{P}^{2 N},\mathbb{Q}) \ar[r]^-\sim \ar[l]_-\sim& \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(Y,\mathbb{Q}) . } \]

By specialization, the result is that if \(X_0'\) is a non-specific quadric hypersurface in the projective space \(\mathbb{P}_0^{2 N}\) on \(\mathbb{F}_q\), and \(Y_0'\) a hyperplane, inclusions \(X_0'\hookrightarrow \mathbb{P}_0^{2 N}\hookleftarrow Y_0'\) induce isomorphisms

\[ \xymatrix{ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(X',\mathbb{Q}_\ell) & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(\mathbb{P}^{2 N},\mathbb{Q}_\ell) \ar[r]^-\sim \ar[l]_-\sim& \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(Y',\mathbb{Q}_\ell) . } \]
These isomorphisms switch to \(F^\ast\), and the trace formula is applied.

The trace formula can also be used to understand the classical formulae giving the number of rational points of the linear groups on the finite fields, and those of certain homogeneous spaces (see paragraph 8).

A dictionary is available to translate into cohomological terms than various types of classical manipulations on trigonometric sums. This dictionary will be given in paragraph 2. The cohomological statement being more "geometric," it may sometimes apply to situations where the classical argument only applies after an extension of the basic finite field. Here is an example (a particular case of a Kazhdan theorem).

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 1.13 (Kazhdan)

Either \(X_0\) a scheme on \(\mathbb{F}_q\). Suppose there is an action \(\rho\) of \(\mathbb{G}_a\) on \(X\), and a morphism \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) schemes on \(\mathbb{F}\), making \(X\) a \(\mathbb{G}_a\)-torseur on \(Y\). Then the number of rational points of \(X_0\) is divisible by \(q\).
Suppose first that \(\rho\), \(Y\) and \(f\) be defined on \(\mathbb{F}_q\).
Argument classical

The fibres of \(f:X_0(\mathbb{F}_q)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y_0(\mathbb{F}_q)\) all have \(q\) elements: \(|X_0(\mathbb{F}_q)| = q\cdot |Y_0(\mathbb{F}_q)|\), hence the divisibility.
Cohomological translation
The upper direct image sheaves \(\mathsf{R}^i f_!\mathbb{Q}_\ell\) are
\[ \mathsf{R}^i f_!\mathbb{Q}_\ell = \begin{cases} \mathbb{Q}_\ell(-1) & \text{if } i = 2 \\ 0 & \text{for } i\ne 2 \end{cases} \]
The Leray spectral sequence of \(f\) thus degenerates into an isomorphism
\[ \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(X,\mathbb{Q}_\ell) = \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^{i-2}(Y,\mathbb{Q}_\ell)(-1) \text{.} \]

To understand the effect of a Tate twist on Frobenius' eigenvalues, the most convenient is to use the Galoisian perspective ([II]Report 1.8), and note that the substitution of Frobenius \(\varphi\) acts on \(\mathbb{Q}_\ell(1)\) by multiplication by \(q\). The eigenvalues of \(F^\ast\) on \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^p(X,\mathbb{Q}_\ell)\) are therefore produced by \(q\) of the eigenvalues of \(F^\ast\) acting on \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^{i-2}(Y,\mathbb{Q}_\ell)\). It is known that these are algebraic integers [16]; therefore

\[ \tag{*} \substack{\displaystyle\text{the eigenvalues of } F^\ast \text{ acting on } \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(X,\mathbb{Q}_\ell) \text{ are} \\ \displaystyle\text{Algebraic integers divisible by } q \text{.}} \]
Descent

For \(n\) suitable, it can be assumed that \(\rho\), \(Y\) and \(f\) are defined on \(\mathbb{F}_{q^n}\). The morphism of Frobenius relating to \(\mathbb{F}_{q^n}\) being the power \(n\)-th of that relating to \(\mathbb{F}_q\), the statement (*) for the scheme on \(\mathbb{F}_{q^n}\) deducted from \(X_0\) by extension of scalar provides us with:

\[ \tag{**} \substack{\displaystyle\text{Powers } n \text{- the same of the eigenvalues of } F^\ast \text{ acting on} \\ \displaystyle\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(X,\mathbb{Q}_\ell) \text{ are algebraic integers divisible by } q^n \text{.}} \]

This assertion implies (*) The number of rational points of \(X_0\), i.e. \(\sum (-1)^i \operatorname{tr}(F^\ast,\underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(X,\mathbb{Q}_\ell))\), is therefore algebraic integer divisible by \(q\). This implies that it is divisible by \(q\) as a rational integer.

The formula 1.9.3 control the dependence in \(n\) of the trigonometric sum to the left member. It involves identities between a trigonometric sum and those deduced from it by "extension of the scalar." The most famous of these identities is that of Hasse-Davenport: either \(\chi:\mathbb{F}_q^\times \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbb{C}^\times\) and \(\psi:\mathbb{F}_q\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbb{C}^\times\) of the characters, \(\psi\) not trivial, and let's define the sum of Gauss \(\tau(\chi,\psi)\) by
\[ \tag{1.14.1} \tau(\chi,\psi) = - \sum_{x\in \mathbb{F}_q^\times} \psi(x) \chi^{-1}(x) \text{.} \]

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 1.15 (Hasse-Davenport)

We've got

\[ \tag{1.15.1} \tau\left(\chi\circ N_{\mathbb{F}_{q^n}/\mathbb{F}_q},\psi\circ \operatorname{tr}_{\mathbb{F}_{q^n}/\mathbb{F}_q}\right) = \tau(\chi,\psi)^n \text{.} \]

Is \(E\subset \mathbb{C}\) a field of numbers containing the values of \(\chi\) and \(\psi\), and \(E_\lambda\) completed with \(E\) in a characteristic place \(\ell\ne p\). Identity (1.15.1) is an identity in \(E\). It is the same to prove it in \(\mathbb{C}\), or \(E_\lambda\). We will prove it in \(E_\lambda\), by looking at \(\chi\) and \(\psi\) as in \(E_\lambda^\times\).

We're applying 1.9.3 for \(X_0=\mathbb{G}_m\) on \(\mathbb{F}_q\) and \(G_0=\mathbb{G}_m\times \mathbb{G}_a\). \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(\mathbb{G}_m,\mathscr{F}(\chi^{-1}\psi))\) are void for \(i\ne 1\), and \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^1\) is of size \(1\) (4.2). Based on 1.9.3, \(\tau(\chi\circ N_{\mathbb{F}_{q^n}/\mathbb{F}_q},\psi\circ \operatorname{tr}_{\mathbb{F}_{q^n}/\mathbb{F}_q})\) is the only eigenvalue of \((F^\ast)^n\) on \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^1\), and (1.15.1) as a result.
Similar examples are given in Weil [43].

A \(E_\lambda\)-sheaf \(\mathscr{F}_0\) on \(X-0\) is said punctually weight \(n\) if for any closed point \(x\in |X_0|\), the eigenvalues of \(F_x^\ast\) ([II]Report 1.2) are algebraic numbers with all complex conjugates of absolute value \(q_x^{n/2}\), where \(q_x\) is the number of elements of \(k(x)\). The following theorem will be shown in [15].

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 1.17

If \(\mathscr{F}_0\) is punctually of weight \(n\), for any eigenvalue \(\alpha\) of endomorphism \(F^\ast\) of \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(X,\mathscr{F})\), there is an integer \(m\leqslant n+i\) such that the complex conjugates of \(\alpha\) are all absolute values \(q^{m/2}\).
The bundles considered in 1.9 are of weight \(0\). The theorem therefore provides for the sum 1.9.1 (it is rather, for all its complex conjugates) the increase

\[ \tag{1.17.1} \left|\sum_{s\in S_0(\mathbb{F}_q)} \prod_i \chi_i(f^i(s)) \right| \leqslant \sum_i \dim \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i\left(S,\mathscr{F}\left(\prod \chi_i f^i\right)\right)\cdot q^{i/2} \text{.} \]
1.18 Remarks

1. If \(\dim S=n\), for all \(E_\lambda\)-sheaf \(\mathscr{F}\) on \(S\), we have

    \[ \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(S,\mathscr{F}) = 0 \qquad \text{for } i\notin [0,2n] \text{.} \]

  2. The group \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^0(X,\mathscr{F})\) is the group of the global sections of \(\mathscr{F}\) on \(S\) with its compact support. If \(S\) is related, not complete and \(\mathscr{F}\) smooth, well if \(S\) is related, \(\mathscr{F}\) smooth of rank one, and not constant, this group is null.

  3. If \(S\) is purely smooth in size \(n\), and \(\mathscr{F}\) smooth, Poincaré's duality says that the vector spaces \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(X,\mathscr{F})\) and \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^{2n-i}(X,\mathscr{F}^\vee)(2n)\) are due to each other (for the effect of a tate twist on Frobenius' eigenvalues, see \(2\) part of the proof of 1.13).

  4. If \(\dim S=n\), Poincaré's duality makes it possible to calculate as a result \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^{2n}(S,\mathscr{F})\): we take an open \(U\) of \(S_\text{red}\), whose complement is of dimension \(<n\), pure smooth of dimension \(n\), and on which \(\mathscr{F}\) is smooth. We then have \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^{2n}(U,\mathscr{F}) \xrightarrow{\sim} \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^{2n}(S,\mathscr{F})\), because in the exact long sequence of cohomology, \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(S\setminus U,\mathscr{F}) = 0\) for \(i=2n,2n-1\). By Poincaré, we have therefore

    \[ \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^{2n}(S,\mathscr{F}) \xrightarrow{\sim} \left(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^0(U,\mathscr{F}^\vee)(2n)\right)^\vee \text{.} \]

    Suppose \(U\) related, and be \(u\) a geometric point of \(U\). The "local system" \(\mathscr{F}\) corresponds to a representation of \(\pi_1(U,u)\) on \(\mathscr{F}_u\), and \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^0(U,\mathscr{F}^\vee)\) is \((\mathscr{F}_u^\vee)^{\pi_1(U,u)}=((\mathscr{F}_u)_{\pi_1(U,u)})^\vee\) (dual of variable corners).

    \[ \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^{2n}(S,\mathscr{F}) \simeq (\mathscr{F}_u)_{\pi_1(U,u)}(-2n) \text{.} \]

  5. These remarks often allow the calculation of \(b_0\) and \(b_{2n}\). Calculating the others \(b_i\) can be difficult. If \(b_{2n}=0\) and \(b_{2n-1}=0\), the increase (1.17.1) is an increase to the Lang-Weil, with, for \(q\) large, a gain in \(\sqrt q\) compared to the three-way increase in \(O(q^n)\). However, it should be noted that the increase (1.17.1) does not formally imply the three-way mark-up

    \[ \left|\sum_{s\in S_0(\mathbb{F}_q)} \prod_i \chi_i(f^i(s))\right| \leqslant \left| S_0(\mathbb{F}_q)\right| \text{,} \]

    It may therefore be useful to take into account (cf. the proof of 3.8).

I explain below a method to prove that \(b_i=0\) for \(i\ne n\). When it applies, it provides an estimate in \(O(q^{n/2}\). The constant implicit in \(0\) is \(b_n=(-1)^n \chi(S,\mathscr{F}(\prod \chi_i f^i))\) can be difficult to calculate.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.19

It is assumed that \(\overline{X}\) a proper scheme on an algbrically enclosed field \(k\), \(j:X\hookrightarrow bar X\) an open \(X\) and \(\mathscr{F}\) a \(E_\lambda\)-sheaves on \(X\).

  1. \((j_\ast\mathscr{F})_x = 0\) for \(x\in \overline{X}\setminus X\), i.e. \(j_!\mathscr{F}\xrightarrow{\sim} j_\ast\mathscr{F}\);

  2. \(\mathsf{R}^i j_\ast \mathscr{F} = 0\) for \(i>0\).

So, the applications

\[ \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(X,\mathscr{F}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(X,\mathscr{F}) \]

are isomorphisms.
Assumptions a) b) mean that \(j_! \mathscr{F} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathsf{R} j_\ast \mathscr{F}\), hence

\[ \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(X,\mathscr{F}) = \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(\overline{X},j_!\mathscr{F}) \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathsf{H}^i(\overline{X},\mathsf{R} j_\ast \mathscr{F}) = \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(X,\mathscr{F}) \text{.} \]
In other words, Leray's spectral sequence for \(j\)
\[ E_2^{pq} = \underline{\operatorname{H}}^p(\overline{X},\mathsf{R}^q j_\ast \mathscr{F}) \Rightarrow \underline{\operatorname{H}}^{p+q}(X,\mathscr{F}) \]
degenerates, by (b) in isomorphisms
\[ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(\overline{X},j_\ast\mathscr{F}) \xrightarrow{\sim} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(X,\mathscr{F}) \text{,} \]
while, under (a)

\[ \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(X,\mathscr{F}) = \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(\overline{X},j_!\mathscr{F}) \xrightarrow{\sim} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(\overline{X},j_\ast\mathscr{F}) \text{.} \]
Example

If \(X\) is a curve, or if \(\overline{X}\) is smooth, \(X\) the complement of a divisor with normal crosses and \(\mathscr{F}\) moderately branched, the assumption (a) of 1.19 (no invariants under local monodromy) implies hypothesis b).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.20

Been \(X_0\) a purely dimensional smooth affine scheme \(n\) on \(\mathbb{F}_q\) and \(\mathscr{F}_0\) one \(E_\lambda\)-Smooth, punctually smooth sheaf of weight \(m\) on \(X_0\). It is assumed that applications \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(X,\mathscr{F}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(X,\mathscr{F})\) are isomorphisms (cf. 1.17) So, \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(X,\mathscr{F}) = 0\) for \(i\ne n\), and the complex conjugates of the eigenvalues of \(F^\ast\) on \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^n(X,\mathscr{F})\) are of absolute value \(q^{(m+n)/2}\).

The duality of Poincaré puts into duality \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(X,\mathscr{F})\) (resp. \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(X,\mathscr{F}^\vee)\)) with \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^{2n-i}(X,\mathscr{F}^\vee(n))\) (resp. \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^{2n-i}(X,\mathscr{F}(n))\)). [2], \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^i\) without support is invalid for \(i>n\). By duality, \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i\) are invalid for \(i<n\); since \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(X,\mathscr{F}) \xrightarrow{\sim} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(X,\mathscr{F})\), these groups are invalid for \(i\ne n\). 1.15 to \(\mathscr{F}_0\) and to \(\mathscr{F}_0^\vee(n)\) (of peppers) \(-m-2n\)) It is found that complex conjugates \(\alpha\) of the eigenvalues of \(F^\ast\) on \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^n(X,\mathscr{F}) = \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^n(X,\mathscr{F}^\vee(n))^\vee\) check
\begin{align*} |\alpha| &\leqslant q^{(m+n)/2} && \text{and} \\ |\alpha^{-1}| &\leqslant q^{((-m-2n)+n)/2} = q^{-(m+n)/2} \text{,} \end{align*}
Hence the assertion.

The last argument shows that if \(X_0\) is a smooth separate pattern on \(\mathbb{F}_q\), that \(\mathscr{F}_0\) is a \(E_\lambda\)-smooth smooth pattern of weight \(m\) on \(X_0\) and that \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(X,\mathscr{F}) \hookrightarrow \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(X,\mathscr{F})\), then the complex conjugates of the eigenvalues of \(F^\ast\) on \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(X,\mathscr{F})\) are of absolute value \(q^{(m+i)/2}\).
Appendix. Lang isogeny in the non-commutative case

Be \(G-0\) a related algebraic group on \(\mathbb{F}_q\) and \(\mathfrak{L}\) the morphism \(x\mapsto F x\cdot x^{-1}\) of \(G-0\) in itself. It is an orphism of \(G_0\)-homogeneous spaces, if at the source we act \(G-0\) by translation to the left \(x\mapsto (y\mapsto x y)\), for the purpose by \(x\mapsto (y\mapsto F x\cdot y\cdot x^{-1}\)). We have \(\mathfrak{L}(xy) = \mathfrak{L}(x)\) for \(y\in G_0(\mathbb{F}_q)\), and \(\mathfrak{L}\) induces isomorphism \(G_0/G_0(\mathbb{F}_q)\xrightarrow{\sim} G_0\).

As in the commutative case, \(\mathfrak{L}\) makes \(G_0\) a \(G_0(\mathbb{F}_q)\)-torseur on \(G_0\), the Lang torsor \(L\). If \(\rho:G_0(\mathbb{F}_q) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{gl}(n,E_\lambda)\) is a linear representation of \(G_0(\mathbb{F}_q)\), we will note \(\mathscr{F}(\rho)\) the \(E_\lambda\)-sheaf \(\rho(L)\) (1.2).

Either \(\gamma\in G^F=G_0(\mathbb{F}_q)\), and let's calculate the endomorphism \(F^\ast\) of \(\mathscr{F}(\rho)_\gamma\). Either \(g\in G\) such as \(\mathfrak{L}(g)=\gamma\), hence a landmark \(\rho(g)\in \operatorname{Isom}(E_\lambda^n,\mathscr{F}(\rho)_\gamma)\). For \(e\in E_\lambda^n\), we have
\[ F(\rho(g)(e)) = \rho(F g)(e) = \rho(\gamma g)(e) = \rho(g\cdot g^{-1}\gamma g)(e) \text{.} \]
We will see that \(g^{-1} \gamma g\in G_0(\mathbb{F}_q)\), from which
\[ F(\rho(g)(e)) = \rho(g) \rho(g^{-1}\gamma g)(e) \]
and
\[ \tag{1.23.1} \rho(g)^{-1} (F_\gamma^\ast)^{-1} \rho(g) = \rho(g^{-1} \gamma g) \text{:} \]
\(\rho(g)\) identifies the opposite of \(F^\ast\) en \(\gamma\) to automorphism \(\rho(g^{-1} \gamma g)\) de \(E_\lambda^n\). It remains to be understood what \(g^{-1} \gamma g\) is.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.24

  1. If \(F g\cdot g^{-1} \in G_0(\mathbb{F}_q)\), we have \(g^{-1} (F g\cdot g^{-1}) g = g^{-1} F g\in G_0(\mathbb{F}_q)\).

  2. Be \(\gamma\in G_0(\mathbb{F}_q)\), and \(g\) such as \(F g\cdot g^{-1} = \gamma\). The conjugation class of \(\gamma'=g^{-1} Fg\) in \(G_0(\mathbb{F}_q)\) depends only on that of \(\gamma\).

  3. The application induced by \(\gamma\mapsto \gamma'\), of the set \(G_0(\mathbb{F}_q)^\natural\) of the conjugation classes of \(G_0(\mathbb{F}_q)\) in itself, is bijective. Its inverse is the application for the opposite group.

(i) If \(\gamma= F g\cdot g^{-1}\in G_0(\mathbb{F}_q)\), the identities \(F g=\gamma g\) and \(F\gamma=\gamma\) give \(F(g^{-1} F g) = (F g)^{-1} F F g = g^{-1} \gamma^{-1} F(\gamma g) = g^{-1} \gamma^{-1} F \gamma F g = g^{-1} F g\), so that \(g^{-1} F g \in G_0(\mathbb{F}_q)\).

(ii) For \(\alpha,\gamma\in G_0(\mathbb{F}_q)\), \(g\) such as \(F g\cdot g^{-1} = \gamma\) form a right class under \(G_0(\mathbb{F}_q)\), and if \(F g\cdot g^{-1} = \gamma\), we have \(F(\alpha g \alpha^{-1})(\alpha g \alpha^{-1})^{-1} = \alpha \gamma\alpha^{-1}\). For \(\gamma\) running a conjugation class in \(G_0(\mathbb{F}_q)\), \(g\) such as \(F g\cdot g^{-1} = \gamma\) thus travel a double class under \(G_0(\mathbb{F}_q)\), and \(\gamma'= g^{-1} F g\) travels through a conjugation class.
(iii) Finally, the opposite of \(F g\cdot g^{-1}\mapsto g^{-1} F g\) is \(g^{-1} F g\mapsto F g\cdot g^{-1}\).

The formula (1.23.1) can be read, briefly: \(F^\ast\) en \(\gamma\) is \(\rho(\gamma')^{-1}\). If \(\gamma\) belongs to the neutral component of its centralizer, we can take \(g\) in \(Z(\gamma)\). We then have \(\gamma=\gamma'\). If this condition is not met, \(\gamma\) and \(\gamma'\) may not be combined.
1.26 An example

For \(G_0=\operatorname{SL}(2)\), \(q\) odd, \(\alpha\in F_q^\times\setminus {\mathbb{F}_q^\times}^2\) and \(\overline{\gamma}\) the conjugation class of \(-1(1+N)\), with \(N^2=0\), \(\overline{\gamma}'\) is the conjugation class of \(-1\cdot (1+\alpha N)\), so \(\overline{\gamma}'\ne \overline{\gamma}\) if \(N\ne 0\).

pages.title: Dictionary
pages.html:
2 Dictionary

An elementary manipulation of trigonometric sums can often be seen as the reflection, through the formula of traces, of a cohomological statement. In this paragraph I enumerate such statements, and their reflection; they bear the same number, plus a \(\ast\) for the cohomological statement.

2.1

"A sum of algebraic integers is an algebraic integer" admits for cohomological analogue the following theorem, proven in [16] (cf. the use of 2.1\(^\ast\) in 1.13).

Theorem 2.1 (2.1*)

Be \(X_0\) a separate scheme of finite type on \(\mathbb{F}_q\) and \(\mathscr{F}_0\) a \(E_\lambda\)-sheaf on \(X_0\). If, for all \(x\in |X_0|\), the eigenvalues of \(F_x^\ast\) on \(\mathscr{F}_0\) are algebraic integers, then the eigenvalues of \(F^\ast\) on \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(X,\mathscr{F})\) are algebraic integers.

2.2

Other results of integers are proven in [16]: if \(\dim(x_0)\leqslant n\), and \(\alpha\) is a proper value of \(F^\ast\) on \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(X,\mathscr{F})\), we have:

  1. under the assumptions of 2.1\(^\ast\), and if \(i>n\), then \(q^{i-n}\) divides \(\alpha\);

  2. If the inverses of the eigenvalues of \(F_x^\ast\) are algebraic integers, then \(\alpha^{-1}\) is integer, except in \(p\). Specifically, \(q^{\inf(n,i)}\alpha^{-1}\) is an algebraic integer.

For the bundles considered in 1.9, the eigenvalues of \(F_x^\ast\) are roots of the unit, so that the assumptions of the theorem and that of (b) above are verified.

2.3

Be \(f:A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) an application of one finite set in another, and \(\varepsilon\) a function on \(A\).

\[ \tag{2.3.1} \sum_{a\in A} \varepsilon(a) = \sum_{b\in B} \sum_{f(a)=b} \varepsilon(a) \text{.} \]

2.3 2.3*

Be \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) a morphism of separate diagrams of finite type on \(k\) algebraically closed, and \(\mathscr{F}\) a \(E_\lambda\)-sheaf on \(X\). The spectral sequence of Leray from \(f\) in compact support cohomology is written

\begin{align*} \tag{2.3.1*} E_2^{p q} = \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^p(Y,\mathsf{R}^q f_! \mathscr{F}) \Rightarrow \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^{p+q}(X,\mathscr{F}) \text{.} \end{align*}

We have \(f_0:X_0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y_0\) a morphism of separate diagrams of the finite type on \(\mathbb{F}_q\) and \(\mathscr{F}_0\) a \(E_\lambda\)-sheaf on \(X_0\).2.3.1) and (2.3.1*) the following compatibility.

  1. The sheaf \(\mathsf{R}^q f_! \mathscr{F}\) is deduced by extension of the scalar from \(\mathbb{F}_q\) to \(\mathbb{F}\) of the sheaf \(\mathsf{R}^q f_{0!} \mathscr{F}_0\) on \(Y_0\). As such, it is equipped with a correspondence of Frobenius \(F^\ast:F^\ast\mathsf{R}^q f_! \mathscr{F} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathsf{R}^q f_! \mathscr{F}\). It is deduced by function of \(\mathsf{R} f_!\) of the correspondence of Frobenius of \(\mathscr{F}\): it is the compound

    \[ \xymatrix{ F^\ast \mathsf{R}^q f_! \mathscr{F} \ar[r]^-\sim & \mathsf{R}^q f_! F^\ast \mathscr{F} \ar[r]^-\sim & \mathsf{R}^q f_! \mathscr{F} . } \]

  2. The pattern formula for \(\mathscr{F}_q\) is written

    \begin{align*} \tag{1} \sum_{x\in X_0(\mathbb{F}_q)} \operatorname{tr}(F_x^\ast,\mathscr{F}_0) = \operatorname{tr}(F^\ast,\underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(X,\mathscr{F})) \text{;} \end{align*}

    the one for \(\mathsf{R}^q f_! \mathscr{F}\) is written

    \begin{align*} \tag{2$_q$} \sum_{y\in Y_0(\mathbb{F}_q)} \operatorname{tr}(F_y^\ast,\mathsf{R}^q f_! \mathscr{F}) = \operatorname{tr}(F^\ast, \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(Y,\mathsf{R}^q f_! \mathscr{F})) \text{.} \end{align*}

For \(y\in Y(\mathbb{F})\), \((\mathsf{R}^q f_! \mathscr{F})_y = \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^q(f^{-1}(y),\mathscr{F})\), and if \(y\in Y_0(\mathbb{F}_q)\),

\[ \operatorname{tr}(F_y^\ast,\mathsf{R}^q f_! \mathscr{F}) = \operatorname{tr}(F^\ast,\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^q(f^{-1}(y),\mathscr{F})) \text{,} \]

from where by the formula traces on the fiber

\[ \sum_{\substack{x\in X_0(\mathbb{F}_q) \\ f(x) = y}} \operatorname{tr}(F_x^\ast,\mathscr{F}_0) = \sum (-1)^q \operatorname{tr}(F_y^\ast,\mathsf{R}^q f_! \mathscr{F}) \text{.} \]

Taking the alternate sum of identities (\(2_q\)), so we find

\begin{align*} \tag{2} \sum_{y\in Y_0(\mathbb{F}_q)} \sum_{\substack{x \in X_0(\mathbb{F}_q) \\ f(x) = y}} \operatorname{tr}(F_x^\ast,\mathscr{F}_0) = \operatorname{tr}(F^\ast,\underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(Y,\mathsf{R}^\bullet f_! \mathscr{F})) \text{.} \end{align*}

The equality of the members of the left (1) and (2) is the result of (2.3.1), that of the right-wing members of (2.3.1*) (taking into account that \(F^\ast\) is an endomorphism of the entire spectral sequence).

2.4

Be \((A_i)_{i\in I}\) a finite family of finished sets, \(A=\prod_{i\in I} A_i\), \(\varepsilon_i\) a function on \(A_i\), and \(\varepsilon\) the function \(a\mapsto \prod \varepsilon_i(a_i)\) on \(A\).

\begin{align*} \tag{2.4.1} \sum_{a\in A} \varepsilon(a) = \prod_{i\in I} \sum_{a_i\in A_i} \varepsilon_i(a_i) \text{.} \end{align*}

2.4 2.4*

We have the formula of Künneth \((X_i)_{i\in I}\) a finished family of separate schemes of the finite type \(k\) algebraically closed, \(X=\prod_{i\in I} X_i\), \(\mathscr{F}_i\) a \(E_\lambda\)-sheaf on \(X_i\) and \(\mathscr{F}\) the external tensor product \(\boxtimes_{i\in I}\mathscr{F}_i = \bigotimes_{i\in I} \mathrm{pr}_i^\ast(\mathscr{F}_i)\) of \(\mathscr{F}_i\).

\begin{align*} \tag{2.4.1*} \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^\bullet(X,\mathscr{F}) = \bigotimes_{i\in I} \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^\bullet(X_i,\mathscr{F}_i) \text{.} \end{align*}

If you want isomorphism (2.4.1*) canonical, and independent of the choice of a total order on \(I\), it is necessary to take to the right limb the graded tensorial product within the meaning of the Koszul rule ([IV]Cycle, 1.3).

2.5

Be \(A\) a finished set, \(B\) a part of \(A\) and \(\varepsilon\) a function on \(A\).

\begin{align*} \tag{2.5.1} \sum_{a\in A} \varepsilon(a) = \sum_{a\in A\setminus B}\varepsilon(a) + \sum_{a\in B} \varepsilon(a) \text{.} \end{align*}

2.5 2.5*

We have \(x\) a separate scheme of finite type on \(k\) algebraically closed, \(Y\) a closed sub-scheme and \(\mathscr{F}\) a \(E_\lambda\)-sheaves on \(X\). We have an exact long sequence of cohomology

\begin{align*} \tag{2.5.1*} \xymatrix{ \cdots \ar[r]^-\partial & \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(X\setminus Y,\mathscr{F}) \ar[r] & \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(X,\mathscr{F}) \ar[r] & \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(Y,\mathscr{F}) \ar[r]^-\partial & \cdots } \end{align*}

More generally, if one starts from a finished filtration of \(X\) by closed parts \(X_p\) (\(p\in \mathbb{Z}\); it is assumed that \(X_p\supset X_{p+1}\), that \(X_p=X\) for \(p\) rather small, and that \(X_p=\varnothing\) for \(p\) rather large), one has a spectral sequence

\begin{align*} \tag{2.5.2*} E_1^{p q} = \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^{p+1}(X_p\setminus X_{p+1},\mathscr{F}) \Rightarrow \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^{p+q}(X,\mathscr{F}) \text{.} \end{align*}

2.6

We have \(A\) a finished set, \((A_i)_{i\in I}\) a finished recovery of \(A\) and \(\varepsilon\) a function on \(A\). For \(J\subset I\), i.e. \(A_J\) the intersection of \(A_j\) (\(j\in J\)).

\begin{align*} \tag{2.6.1} \sum_{J\subset I} (-1)^{|J|} \sum_{a\in A_J} \varepsilon(a) = 0 \text{.} \end{align*}

2.6 2.6*

Be \(X\) a separate scheme of finite type on \(k\) algebraically closed, \((X_i)_{i\in I}\) a closed cover (open) finished on \(X\) by sub-schemes, and \(\mathscr{F}\) a \(E_\lambda\)-sheaf on \(X\). For \(J\subset I\), i.e. \(X_J\) the intersection of \(X_j\) (\(j\in J\)).

\begin{align*} \tag{2.6.1*} E_1^{p q} &= \bigoplus_{|J|=p+1>0} \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^q(X_J,\mathscr{F}) \otimes_\mathbb{Z} \textstyle\bigwedge^{|J|} \mathbb{Z}^J \Rightarrow \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^{p+q}(X,\mathscr{F}) \\ \tag{2.6.2*} E_1^{p q} &= \bigoplus_{|J|=1-p>0} \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^q(X_J,\mathscr{F}) \otimes_\mathbb{Z} \textstyle\bigwedge^{|J|} \mathbb{Z}^J \Rightarrow \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^{p+q}(X,\mathscr{F}) \end{align*}

If \(J=\varnothing\) were not excluded, we would also have spectral sequences converging to \(0\). The factors \(\bigwedge^{|J|} \mathbb{Z}^J\) are there to exempt us from choosing a total order on \(I\).

2.7

Be \(A\) a finite commutative group, and \(\chi:A \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} E_\lambda^\times\) a non-trivial character.

\begin{align*} \tag{2.7.1} \sum_{a\in A} \chi(a) = 0 \text{.} \end{align*}

The cohomological analogue of (2.7.1) by transposing the following evidence: if \(x\in A\), \(a\mapsto x a\) is a permutation of \(A\), and

\begin{align*} \sum_{a\in A}\chi(a) = \sum_{a\in A} \chi(x a) &= \chi(x) \sum_{a\in A} \chi(a) && \text{, from which} \\ (\chi(x)-1)\sum_{a\in A} &= 0 \text{,} \end{align*}

and it exists by hypothesis \(x\) such as \(\chi(x)-1\ne 0\).

Theorem 2.7 (2.7*)

Be \(G_0\) a related commutative algebraic group on \(\mathbb{F}_q\) and \(\chi:G_0(\mathbb{F}_q) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} E_\lambda^\times\) a nontrivial character.

\begin{align*} \tag{2.7.1*} \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^\bullet(G,\mathscr{F}(\chi)) = 0 \text{.} \end{align*}

For \(x\) a rational point of \(G\), let's note \(t_x\) the translation \(t_x(g) = x g\) of \(G\). The formula \(\mathfrak{L} t_x = t_{\mathfrak{L}(x)} \mathfrak{L}\) expresses that \((t_x,t_{\mathfrak{L}(x)})\) is an automorphism of the diagram \(G\xrightarrow{\mathfrak{L}} G\) (\(G\) equipped with the Lang torsor). Either \(\rho(g)\) the automorphism of \((G,\mathscr{F}(\chi))\) which is derived from it. For \(g\in G_0(\mathbb{F}_q)\), i.e. for \(\mathfrak{L}(g) = e\), it is the identity on \(G\), and the multiplication by \(\chi(g)^{-1}\) on \(\mathscr{F}(\chi)\).

Note \(\rho_H(g)\) the automorphism of \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^\bullet(G,\mathscr{F}(\chi))\) deduced from \(\rho(g)\). An argument of homotopy (Lamma 2.8 below) shows that \(\rho_H(g) = \rho_H(e)\), therefore is the identity. On the other hand, for \(g\in G_0(\mathbb{F}_q)\), \(\rho_H(g)\) is the multiplication by \((\chi^{-1}(g):\text{ on }\underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(G,\mathscr{F}(\chi))\), the multiplication by \((\chi^{-1}(g)-1)\) is null. Taking \(g\) such as \(\chi(g)\ne 1\), we obtain 2.7.

Lemma 2.8

Be \(X\) and \(Y\) two diagrams on \(k\) algebraically closed, with \(X\) separate finite type and \(Y\) related. Be \(\mathscr{F}\) a sheaf on \(X\) and \((\rho,\varepsilon)\) a family of endomorphisms of \((X,\mathscr{F})\) set by \(Y\):

\begin{align*} \rho:Y\times_k X &\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\times_k X && \text{is a } Y \text{-morphism, and} \\ \varepsilon:\rho^\ast \mathrm{pr}_2^\ast \mathscr{F} &\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{pr}_2^\ast\mathscr{F} && \text{A sheaf morphism.} \end{align*}

It is assumed that \(\rho\) is clean. For \(y\in Y(k)\), that is \(\rho_H(y)^\ast\) the endomorphism of \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^\bullet(X,\mathscr{F})\) induced by \(\rho_y:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) and \(\varepsilon_y:\rho_y^\ast\mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{F}\). So, \(\rho_H(y)^\ast\) is independent of \(y\).

Indeed, \(\mathsf{R}^p \mathrm{pr}_{1!} \mathrm{pr}_2^\ast\mathscr{F}\) is the constant sheaf on \(Y\) of value \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^p(X,\mathscr{F})\), and \(\rho_H(y)^\ast\) is the fiber in \(y\) of endomorphism

\[ \xymatrix{ \mathsf{R}^p \mathrm{pr}_{1!} \mathrm{pr}_2^\ast \mathscr{F} \ar[r]^-{\rho^\ast} & \mathsf{R}^p \mathrm{pr}_{1!} \rho^\ast \mathrm{pr}_2^\ast \mathscr{F} \ar[r]^-\varepsilon & \mathsf{R}^p \mathrm{pr}_{1!} \mathrm{pr}_2^\ast \mathscr{F} } \]

of this sheaf.

2.9. Note

Be \(G_0\) a related algebraic group on \(\mathbb{F}_q\) and \(\rho:G_0(\mathbb{F}_q) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{GL}(V)\) a linear representation such as \(V^{G_0(\mathbb{F}_q)}=0\). We still have \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^\bullet(G,\rho(L))=0\).

blocks.html: 2 Dictionary

An elementary manipulation of trigonometric sums can often be seen as the reflection, through the formula of traces, of a cohomological statement. In this paragraph I enumerate such statements, and their reflection; they bear the same number, plus a \(\ast\) for the cohomological statement.
"A sum of algebraic integers is an algebraic integer" admits for cohomological analogue the following theorem, proven in [16] (cf. the use of 2.1\(^\ast\) in 1.13).

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 2.1 (2.1*)

Be \(X_0\) a separate scheme of finite type on \(\mathbb{F}_q\) and \(\mathscr{F}_0\) a \(E_\lambda\)-sheaf on \(X_0\). If, for all \(x\in |X_0|\), the eigenvalues of \(F_x^\ast\) on \(\mathscr{F}_0\) are algebraic integers, then the eigenvalues of \(F^\ast\) on \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(X,\mathscr{F})\) are algebraic integers.
Other results of integers are proven in [16]: if \(\dim(x_0)\leqslant n\), and \(\alpha\) is a proper value of \(F^\ast\) on \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(X,\mathscr{F})\), we have:

1. under the assumptions of 2.1\(^\ast\), and if \(i>n\), then \(q^{i-n}\) divides \(\alpha\);

  2. If the inverses of the eigenvalues of \(F_x^\ast\) are algebraic integers, then \(\alpha^{-1}\) is integer, except in \(p\). Specifically, \(q^{\inf(n,i)}\alpha^{-1}\) is an algebraic integer.
For the bundles considered in 1.9, the eigenvalues of \(F_x^\ast\) are roots of the unit, so that the assumptions of the theorem and that of (b) above are verified.
Be \(f:A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) an application of one finite set in another, and \(\varepsilon\) a function on \(A\).
\[ \tag{2.3.1} \sum_{a\in A} \varepsilon(a) = \sum_{b\in B} \sum_{f(a)=b} \varepsilon(a) \text{.} \]

  2. The pattern formula for \(\mathscr{F}_q\) is written

    the one for \(\mathsf{R}^q f_! \mathscr{F}\) is written

Be \((A_i)_{i\in I}\) a finite family of finished sets, \(A=\prod_{i\in I} A_i\), \(\varepsilon_i\) a function on \(A_i\), and \(\varepsilon\) the function \(a\mapsto \prod \varepsilon_i(a_i)\) on \(A\).

blocks.html:
