blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.8

Be \(X\) and \(Y\) two diagrams on \(k\) algebraically closed, with \(X\) separate finite type and \(Y\) related. Be \(\mathscr{F}\) a sheaf on \(X\) and \((\rho,\varepsilon)\) a family of endomorphisms of \((X,\mathscr{F})\) set by \(Y\):

\begin{align*} \rho:Y\times_k X &\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\times_k X && \text{is a } Y \text{-morphism, and} \\ \varepsilon:\rho^\ast \mathrm{pr}_2^\ast \mathscr{F} &\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{pr}_2^\ast\mathscr{F} && \text{A sheaf morphism.} \end{align*}

It is assumed that \(\rho\) is clean. For \(y\in Y(k)\), that is \(\rho_H(y)^\ast\) the endomorphism of \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^\bullet(X,\mathscr{F})\) induced by \(\rho_y:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) and \(\varepsilon_y:\rho_y^\ast\mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{F}\). So, \(\rho_H(y)^\ast\) is independent of \(y\).

Indeed, \(\mathsf{R}^p \mathrm{pr}_{1!} \mathrm{pr}_2^\ast\mathscr{F}\) is the constant sheaf on \(Y\) of value \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^p(X,\mathscr{F})\), and \(\rho_H(y)^\ast\) is the fiber in \(y\) of endomorphism

\[ \xymatrix{ \mathsf{R}^p \mathrm{pr}_{1!} \mathrm{pr}_2^\ast \mathscr{F} \ar[r]^-{\rho^\ast} & \mathsf{R}^p \mathrm{pr}_{1!} \rho^\ast \mathrm{pr}_2^\ast \mathscr{F} \ar[r]^-\varepsilon & \mathsf{R}^p \mathrm{pr}_{1!} \mathrm{pr}_2^\ast \mathscr{F} } \]
of this sheaf.

Be \(G_0\) a related algebraic group on \(\mathbb{F}_q\) and \(\rho:G_0(\mathbb{F}_q) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{GL}(V)\) a linear representation such as \(V^{G_0(\mathbb{F}_q)}=0\). We still have \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^\bullet(G,\rho(L))=0\).

pages.title: Amounts with a variable
pages.html:
3 Amounts with a variable

3.1

Weil was the first to have applied "cohomological" methods to the study of trigonometric sums to a variable; since he had proved the analogy of Riemann's hypothesis for Artin's \(L\) functions on function fields, these methods provided him with excellent estimates (in \(O(\text{square root of the number of terms})\)), and the behavior of these sums by "extension of the base field."

The essential part of this paragraph is a presentation, in a cohomological language, of its results.

3.2

Cohomological methods lead here to calculate groups \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(X,\mathscr{F})\), for \(\mathscr{F}_0\) a \(E_\lambda\)-sheaf on a curve \(X-0\) on \(\mathbb{F}_q\). These groups are zero for \(i\ne 0,1,2\) and for \(i=0,2\), they have a simple interpretation (1.8 a,b,c). In addition, the Euler-Poincaré characteristic \(\chi_c(\mathscr{F}) = \sum (-1)^i \dim \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(X,\mathscr{F})\) (otherwise equal to \(\chi(\mathscr{F}) = \sum (-1)^i \dim \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(X,\mathscr{F})\) can be calculated in terms of \(x\), \(\mathscr{F}\) rank at the generic points of \(X\), and \(\mathscr{F}\) branching properties. The essential result is the following.

Been \(\overline{X}\) a smooth and related projective curve, of a kind \(g\), on an algebraically closed field \(k\), \(X\) a dense open \(\overline{X}\), the complement of a finished set \(S\) points and \(\mathscr{F}\) one \(E_\lambda\)-Smooth sheaf on \(X\). Be \(\chi(X)=2-2 g-\# S\) the characteristic of Euler-Poincaré \(X\), and \(\operatorname{rg}(\mathscr{F})\) the rank of \(\mathscr{F}\). For each point \(s\in S\), we define an integer \(\operatorname{Sw}_s(\mathscr{F})\), the driver of Swan, measuring the wild ramification of \(\mathscr{F}\), and

\begin{align*} \tag{3.2.1} \chi_c(\mathscr{F}) = \operatorname{rg}(\mathscr{F}) \cdot \chi(X) - \sum_{s\in S} \operatorname{Sw}_s(\mathscr{F}) \end{align*}

[29].

3.3

Be \(\overline{X}\), \(j:X\hookrightarrow\overline{X}\) and \(\mathscr{F}\) as in 3.2. Poincaré's theorem of duality admits the very handy form: \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(\overline{X},j_\ast \mathscr{F})\) and \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^{2-i}(\overline{X},j_\ast(\mathscr{F}^\vee(1)))\) are due to each other ([V]Duality, 1.3).

3.4

Let's \(k=\mathbb{F}\), and suppose that \(X\), \(\overline{X}\), and \(\mathscr{F}\) come from \(X_0\), \(\overline{X}_0\) and \(\mathscr{F}_0\) on \(\mathbb{F}_q\). If \(\mathscr{F}_0\) becomes trivial on a finished coating of \(X_0\), Weil has proven that the complex conjugates of the eigenvalues of \(F^\ast\) on \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(\overline{X},j_\ast\mathscr{F})\) are of absolute value \(q^{i/2}\). Such sheaves \(\mathscr{F}_0\) correspond to the functions \(L\) of Artin on the function field of \(\overline{X}_0\).

3.4 3.5

Under the same assumptions, or more generally if \(E_\lambda\) is completed in one place \(\lambda\) of a field of numbers \(E\) and \(\mathscr{F}_0\) belongs to an infinitely compatible system of representations \(v\)-adics (\(v\) place of \(E\)), one can calculate

\[ \prod_i \det(-F^\ast,\underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(X,\mathscr{F}))^{(-1)^i} \]

from information Local on \(\mathscr{F}_0\). This is explained in [13]. For \(\mathscr{F}_0\) trivial on a finished coating of \(X_0\), this is the expression of the constant of the functional equation of a function \(L\) of Artin as a product of local constants.

3.5 Example

Be \(\psi\) the character \(\exp\left(\frac{2\pi i}{p} \operatorname{tr}_{\mathbb{F}_q/\mathbb{F}_p}\right)\) of \(\mathbb{F}_q\); we have \(\psi(a^p-a)=0\). Be \(X_0\) a smooth projective curve absolutely irreducible of genus \(g\) on \(\mathbb{F}_q\), and \(f\) a rational function on \(X_0\), i.e. a morphism \(f:X_0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbb{P}^1\), not identically equal to \(\infty\). We are interested in the sum

\[ S_f' = \sum_{\substack{x\in X_0(\mathbb{F}_q) \\ f(x)\ne \infty}} \psi(f(x)) \text{.} \]

This sum is zero for \(f\) of the form \(g^p-g\). This suggests changing the sum \(S_f'\) as follows:

  1. for any closed point \(x\) of the \(X_0\), we're laying down \(v_x(f) = \) order of the pole of \(f\) in \(x\) if \(f(x)=\infty\), \(v_x(f)=0\) If not, and \(v_x^\ast(f) = \inf v_x(f+g^p-g)\) (lower bound on \(g\)).

  2. If \(v_x^\ast(f)=0\), \(x\in X_0(\mathbb{F}_q)\), and \(f+g^p-g\) is regular in \(x\), then place \(\psi(f(x)) = \psi((f+g^p-g)(x))\).

    \[ \tag{3.5.1} S_f = \sum'_{x\in X_0(\mathbb{F}_q)} \psi(f(x)) \text{,} \]

    where \((-)'\) indicates that the sum is extended to \(x\) such as \(v_x^\ast(f)=0\).

We have \(S_f=S_{f+g^p-g}\). We propose to check that if \(f\) is not the form \(g^p-g+c^{t e}\), then

\[ \tag{3.5.2} |S_f|\leqslant \left(2 g-2+\sum_{v_x^\ast(f)\ne 0} [k(x):\mathbb{F}_q] (1+v_x^\ast(f))\right) q^{1/2} \text{.} \]

We start by passing from the complex to \(\ell\)-adic, replacing \(\psi\) with a character of the form \(\psi_0\circ \operatorname{tr}_{\mathbb{F}_q/\mathbb{F}_p}\), with \(\psi_0:\mathbb{F}_p\hookrightarrow E_\lambda^\times\). For \(j:U_0\hookrightarrow X_0\) the inclusion of the open where \(f\ne\infty\), we prove that

\[ \tag{3.5.3} S_f = \sum (-1)^i \operatorname{tr}(F^\ast,\underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(X,j_\ast \mathscr{F}(\psi f))) \text{.} \]

The trades formula returns this statement to the following (for \(x\in X_0(\mathbb{F}_q)\)):

  1. If \(v_x^\ast(f)=0\), then \(F_x^\ast\) on \(j_\ast \mathscr{F}(\psi f)\) is \(\psi(f(x))\);

  2. If \(v_x^\ast(f)\ne 0\), then \(j_\ast \mathscr{F}(\psi f)\) becomes \(x\): \((j_\ast \mathscr{F}(\psi f))_{\overline{x}}=0\).

Formula (a) results from (1.7.6) if \(v_x(f)=0\), and we return to this case by replacing \(f\) with \(f+g^p-g\): with isomorphism, this does not change \(\mathscr{F}(\psi f)_0\) on the open where \(f\) and \(g\) are regular (1.3).

Formula (b) results from:

\[ \tag{3.5.4} \operatorname{Sw}_x \mathscr{F}(f,\psi) = v_x^\ast(f) \text{.} \]

After reduction in case \(v_x(f)=v_x^\ast(f)\) (hence \(p\nmid v_x(f)\)) and extension of scalar to \(\mathbb{F}\), this formula is in [34].

Finally, we check that the sheaf \(\mathscr{F}(\psi f)\) on \(U\) is not constant if \(f\) is not of the form \(g^p-g+c^{t e}\): since \(\psi_0\) isjective, the triviality of \(\mathscr{F}(\psi f)=\mathscr{F}(\psi_0 f)\) (1.8.ii) is equivalent to that of \(\mathbb{F}_q\)-torsor of equation would be the reciprocal image of a \(\mathbb{F}_q\)-torsor on \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathbb{F}_q)\), equation \(T^p-T-\lambda=0\), \(\mathbb{F}_p\)-torsor of equation \(T^p-T-(f-\lambda)=0\) on \(U_0\) would be trivial and \(f\) would therefore be of the form \(g^p-g+\lambda\). The \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(\overline{X},j_\ast \mathscr{F}(\psi f))\) are therefore null for \(i\ne 1\), the formula (3.5.3) is reduced to

\[ \tag{3.5.5} S_f = -\operatorname{tr}(F^\ast,\underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(\overline{X},j_\ast \mathscr{F}(\psi f))) \]

and, according to (3.2.1) and (3.5.4), of \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^1\) is of size \(2 g-2+\sum_{v_x^\ast(f)>0} [k(x):\mathbb{F}_q](1+v_x^\ast(f))\) and \(-S_f\) is the sum of this number of eigenvalues of \(F^\ast\), each of complex absolute values \(q^{1/2}\).

3.6

Suppose that for an automorphism \(\sigma\) of \(X_0\), we have

\[ f(\sigma x) = - f(x) \text{.} \]

The sum \(S_f\) is then real. If \(\sigma\) is involutive, the duality of Poincaré allows us to say a little more: if we pose \(\mathscr{F}_0=j_\ast \mathscr{F}(\psi f)\), \(\mathscr{G}_0 = j_\ast \mathscr{F}(\psi(-f))\), we have

  1. \(\mathscr{F}_0\) and \(\mathscr{G}_0\) are in duality,

  2. \(\sigma^\ast \mathscr{F}_0 \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathscr{G}_0\) and \(\sigma^\ast \mathscr{G}_0 \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathscr{F}_0\), for natural isomorphisms such as the compound \(\mathscr{F}_0=(\sigma^2)^\ast \mathscr{F}_0 \xrightarrow{\sim} \sigma^\ast \mathscr{G}_0 \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathscr{F}_0\) is the identity.

  3. The mating \(\mathscr{F}_0\otimes \mathscr{G}_0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} E_\lambda\) checks \(\sigma^\ast(f\cdot g) = \sigma^\ast(f) \cdot \sigma^\ast (g)\).

Moving to cohomology, we find that \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(X,\mathscr{F})\) and \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(X,\mathscr{F})\) are in perfect duality at value in \(E_\lambda(1)\), and that the bilinear form \(\alpha\cdot \sigma^\ast\beta\) on \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(X,\mathscr{F})\) is alternating: \(\sigma^\ast\) acts trivialy on \(E_\lambda(1)\), and

\[ \alpha\cdot \sigma^\ast \beta = \sigma^\ast(\alpha\cdot \sigma^\ast \beta) = \sigma^\ast \alpha\cdot \beta = -\beta\cdot \sigma^\ast \alpha \text{.} \]

It is concluded that \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(X,\mathscr{F})\) is of even size (it is also easy to check that each \(v_x^\ast(f)\) not zero is odd) and that the eigenvalues of \(F^\ast\) on \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(X,\mathscr{F})\) are grouped in pairs \(\alpha\) and \(q/\alpha\).

3.7 Example

This applies to Kloosterman's sums \(\sum_{x\in \mathbb{F}_q^\times} \psi\left(x+\frac a x\right)\) (doing \(X_0=\mathbb{P}^1\), \(f=x+\frac a x\), \(\sigma x=-x\); the poles of \(f\) are \(0\) and \(\infty\), and in each of them \(v_x^\ast(f)=1\); the \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^1\) is of size \(-2+2+2=2\)) So we have (for \(a\ne 0\))

\[ \sum_{\substack{x y=a \\ x,y\in \mathbb{F}_{q^n}}} \exp\left(\frac{2\pi i}{p} \operatorname{tr}_{\mathbb{F}_{q^n}/\mathbb{F}_p}(x+y)\right) = (-\alpha^n+\alpha^{-n}) \qquad \text{, }\alpha\overline{\alpha} = q \text{.} \]

This result is due to L. Carlitz [9].

Here is an application of a Lang-Weil method.

Proposition 3.8

Either \(P\in \mathbb{F}_q[X_1,\dots,X_n]\) a polynomial to \(n\) variable, degree \(d\), of which it is assumed that it is not of the form \(Q^p-Q+C^{te}\). Still laying \(\psi(x) = \exp\left(\frac{2\pi i}{p} \operatorname{tr}_{\mathbb{F}_q/\mathbb{F}_p}(x)\right)\) We've got

\[ \left|\sum_{x_i\in \mathbb{F}_q} \psi P(x_1,\dots,x_n)\right| \leqslant (d+1) q^{n-\frac 1 2} \text{.} \]

For the left-hand member, we have the trivial estimate \(q^n\). All we have to do is prove 3.8 when \(d-1<q^{1/2}\); suppose only \(d<q+1\), and be \(P_d\) the homogeneous part of degree \(d\) of \(P\).

Lemma 3.9

A hypersurface of degree \(d\) in \(\mathbb{P}^r\) (\(r\geqslant i\)) cannot go through all rational points on \(\mathbb{F}_q\) which is \(d\geqslant q+1\).

If there is a rational hyperplane not entirely contained in the hypersurface (this is not the case for \(r=1\)), the hypothesis of recurrence is applied to the trace of the hypersurface on this hyperplane. Otherwise, the degree \(d\) is \(\geqslant\) the number of rational hyperplanes, \(\geqslant q+1\).

Now let's distinguish two cases.

Case 1: \(p\nmid d\).

Applying the lemma to \(P_d\), we see that, even if we make a linear change of variables, we can assume that \(P_d(1,0,\ldots,0)\ne 0\), i.e. the coefficient of \(X_1^d\) in \(P\) is not null. A polynomial to \(1\) variable

\[ S(X) = \sum_{i=0}^d a_i x^i \text{,} \]

with \(a_d\ne 0\) (\(p\nmid d\)) is never of the form \(Q^p-Q+C^{t e}\), and estimation (3.5.2) is reduced to

\[ \left| \sum \psi(S(x)) \right| \leqslant (d-1) q^{1/2} \text{.} \]

Applying this estimate to the partial amounts obtained by varying only \(x_1\), we find

\[ \left| \sum \psi P(x_1,\dots,x_n) \right| = \left| \sum_{x_2,\dots,x_n} \sum_{x_1} \psi P(x_1,\dots,x_n)\right| \leqslant q^{n-1} (d-1) q^{1/2} \]

As promised.

Case 2: \(p\mid d\) (\(d>0\)).

If \(P_d\) is a power \(p\)-th, replace \(P\) by \(P-(P_d-P_d^{1/p})\) does not change the sum considered, and lowers the degree: this case is disposed of by repeating \(d\). Otherwise, the differential of \(P_d\) is not identically zero; applying 3.9, it can be assumed, even if a linear change of variables is made, that it is not null at the point \((1,0,\dots,0)\).

\[ P_d = \sum_{i=0}^d X_1^{d-i} S_i(X_2,\dots,X_n) \text{,} \]

This means that the linear form \(S_1\) is not identically null.

The form \(S_0\) is a constant, and replacing \(P\) by \(P-(S_0 X_1^d -S_0^{1/p} X_1^{d/p})\), it can be assumed that it is null. Or finally \(-\lambda\) the coefficient of \(X_1^{d-1}\) in \(P\). For \(x_2,\dots,x_n\) fixed, we have

\[ P(X_1,x_2,\dots,x_n) = (S_1(x_2,\dots,x_n)-\lambda) X_1^{d-1} + \text{lower degree terms in } X_1 \]

and if \(S_1(x_2,\dots,x_n)\ne \lambda\), we have

\[ \left| \sum_{x_1} \psi P(x_1,\dots,x_n)\right| \leqslant (d-2) q^{1/2} \text{.} \]

In total,

\begin{align*} \left|\sum \psi P(x_1,\dots,x_n)\right| &\leqslant \left|\sum_{S=\lambda} \psi P(x_1,\dots,x_n)\right| + \sum_{S(X_2,\dots,X_n)\ne\lambda} \left| \sum_{x_1} \psi P(x_1,\dots,x_n)\right| \\ &\leqslant q^{n-1} + (q^{n-1}-q^{n-2})(d-2) q^{1/2} \\ &< (d-2) q^{n-1/2} + q^{n-1} \\ &< (d-1) q^{n-1/2} \text{.} \end{align*}

Another result of this nature is given by R.A. Smith [37].

\[ \tag{3.5.5} S_f = -\operatorname{tr}(F^\ast,\underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(\overline{X},j_\ast \mathscr{F}(\psi f))) \]

and, according to (3.2.1) and (3.5.4), of \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^1\) is of size \(2 g-2+\sum_{v_x^\ast(f)>0} [k(x):\mathbb{F}_q](1+v_x^\ast(f))\) and \(-S_f\) is the sum of this number of eigenvalues of \(F^\ast\), each of complex absolute values \(q^{1/2}\).

\[ f(\sigma x) = - f(x) \text{.} \]

  2. \(\sigma^\ast \mathscr{F}_0 \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathscr{G}_0\) and \(\sigma^\ast \mathscr{G}_0 \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathscr{F}_0\), for natural isomorphisms such as the compound \(\mathscr{F}_0=(\sigma^2)^\ast \mathscr{F}_0 \xrightarrow{\sim} \sigma^\ast \mathscr{G}_0 \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathscr{F}_0\) is the identity.

  3. The mating \(\mathscr{F}_0\otimes \mathscr{G}_0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} E_\lambda\) checks \(\sigma^\ast(f\cdot g) = \sigma^\ast(f) \cdot \sigma^\ast (g)\).

Moving to cohomology, we find that \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(X,\mathscr{F})\) and \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(X,\mathscr{F})\) are in perfect duality at value in \(E_\lambda(1)\), and that the bilinear form \(\alpha\cdot \sigma^\ast\beta\) on \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(X,\mathscr{F})\) is alternating: \(\sigma^\ast\) acts trivialy on \(E_\lambda(1)\), and
\[ \alpha\cdot \sigma^\ast \beta = \sigma^\ast(\alpha\cdot \sigma^\ast \beta) = \sigma^\ast \alpha\cdot \beta = -\beta\cdot \sigma^\ast \alpha \text{.} \]

It is concluded that \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(X,\mathscr{F})\) is of even size (it is also easy to check that each \(v_x^\ast(f)\) not zero is odd) and that the eigenvalues of \(F^\ast\) on \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(X,\mathscr{F})\) are grouped in pairs \(\alpha\) and \(q/\alpha\).
3.7 Example

This applies to Kloosterman's sums \(\sum_{x\in \mathbb{F}_q^\times} \psi\left(x+\frac a x\right)\) (doing \(X_0=\mathbb{P}^1\), \(f=x+\frac a x\), \(\sigma x=-x\); the poles of \(f\) are \(0\) and \(\infty\), and in each of them \(v_x^\ast(f)=1\); the \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^1\) is of size \(-2+2+2=2\)) So we have (for \(a\ne 0\))

\[ \sum_{\substack{x y=a \\ x,y\in \mathbb{F}_{q^n}}} \exp\left(\frac{2\pi i}{p} \operatorname{tr}_{\mathbb{F}_{q^n}/\mathbb{F}_p}(x+y)\right) = (-\alpha^n+\alpha^{-n}) \qquad \text{, }\alpha\overline{\alpha} = q \text{.} \]
This result is due to L. Carlitz [9].
Here is an application of a Lang-Weil method.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.8

Either \(P\in \mathbb{F}_q[X_1,\dots,X_n]\) a polynomial to \(n\) variable, degree \(d\), of which it is assumed that it is not of the form \(Q^p-Q+C^{te}\). Still laying \(\psi(x) = \exp\left(\frac{2\pi i}{p} \operatorname{tr}_{\mathbb{F}_q/\mathbb{F}_p}(x)\right)\) We've got

\[ \left|\sum_{x_i\in \mathbb{F}_q} \psi P(x_1,\dots,x_n)\right| \leqslant (d+1) q^{n-\frac 1 2} \text{.} \]

For the left-hand member, we have the trivial estimate \(q^n\). All we have to do is prove 3.8 when \(d-1<q^{1/2}\); suppose only \(d<q+1\), and be \(P_d\) the homogeneous part of degree \(d\) of \(P\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.9

A hypersurface of degree \(d\) in \(\mathbb{P}^r\) (\(r\geqslant i\)) cannot go through all rational points on \(\mathbb{F}_q\) which is \(d\geqslant q+1\).

If there is a rational hyperplane not entirely contained in the hypersurface (this is not the case for \(r=1\)), the hypothesis of recurrence is applied to the trace of the hypersurface on this hyperplane. Otherwise, the degree \(d\) is \(\geqslant\) the number of rational hyperplanes, \(\geqslant q+1\).
Now let's distinguish two cases.
Case 1: \(p\nmid d\).

Applying the lemma to \(P_d\), we see that, even if we make a linear change of variables, we can assume that \(P_d(1,0,\ldots,0)\ne 0\), i.e. the coefficient of \(X_1^d\) in \(P\) is not null. A polynomial to \(1\) variable
\[ S(X) = \sum_{i=0}^d a_i x^i \text{,} \]
with \(a_d\ne 0\) (\(p\nmid d\)) is never of the form \(Q^p-Q+C^{t e}\), and estimation (3.5.2) is reduced to
\[ \left| \sum \psi(S(x)) \right| \leqslant (d-1) q^{1/2} \text{.} \]
Applying this estimate to the partial amounts obtained by varying only \(x_1\), we find
\[ \left| \sum \psi P(x_1,\dots,x_n) \right| = \left| \sum_{x_2,\dots,x_n} \sum_{x_1} \psi P(x_1,\dots,x_n)\right| \leqslant q^{n-1} (d-1) q^{1/2} \]
As promised.

If \(P_d\) is a power \(p\)-th, replace \(P\) by \(P-(P_d-P_d^{1/p})\) does not change the sum considered, and lowers the degree: this case is disposed of by repeating \(d\). Otherwise, the differential of \(P_d\) is not identically zero; applying 3.9, it can be assumed, even if a linear change of variables is made, that it is not null at the point \((1,0,\dots,0)\).
\[ P_d = \sum_{i=0}^d X_1^{d-i} S_i(X_2,\dots,X_n) \text{,} \]
This means that the linear form \(S_1\) is not identically null.

The form \(S_0\) is a constant, and replacing \(P\) by \(P-(S_0 X_1^d -S_0^{1/p} X_1^{d/p})\), it can be assumed that it is null. Or finally \(-\lambda\) the coefficient of \(X_1^{d-1}\) in \(P\). For \(x_2,\dots,x_n\) fixed, we have
\[ P(X_1,x_2,\dots,x_n) = (S_1(x_2,\dots,x_n)-\lambda) X_1^{d-1} + \text{lower degree terms in } X_1 \]
and if \(S_1(x_2,\dots,x_n)\ne \lambda\), we have
\[ \left| \sum_{x_1} \psi P(x_1,\dots,x_n)\right| \leqslant (d-2) q^{1/2} \text{.} \]
In total,

\begin{align*} \left|\sum \psi P(x_1,\dots,x_n)\right| &\leqslant \left|\sum_{S=\lambda} \psi P(x_1,\dots,x_n)\right| + \sum_{S(X_2,\dots,X_n)\ne\lambda} \left| \sum_{x_1} \psi P(x_1,\dots,x_n)\right| \\ &\leqslant q^{n-1} + (q^{n-1}-q^{n-2})(d-2) q^{1/2} \\ &< (d-2) q^{n-1/2} + q^{n-1} \\ &< (d-1) q^{n-1/2} \text{.} \end{align*}
Another result of this nature is given by R.A. Smith [37].

pages.title: Gauss and Jacob's sums
pages.html:
4 The sums of Gauss and the sums of Jacob

4.1

Be \(k\) a finite field of characteristic \(p\), \(\chi\) a character of \(k^\times\), and \(\psi\) a nontrivial character of the additive group of \(k\). We will take for definition the sums of Gauss:

\[ \tag{4.1.1} \tau(\chi,\psi) = -\sum_{x\in k^\times} \psi(x) \chi^{-1}(x) \]

(note the sign). Classically, \(\chi\) and \(\psi\) are complex values. We will take them to values in \(E_\lambda^\times\) (see proof of 1.15) Let's look at \(-\tau(\chi,\psi)\) as a sum on the rational points of the diagram \(\mathbb{G}_m\) on \(k\). 1, we have \(-\tau(\chi,\psi) = \operatorname{tr}(F^\ast,\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^\ast(\mathbb{G}_m,\mathscr{F}(\psi \chi^{-1}))\). More

Proposition 4.2

The cohomology of \(\mathbb{G}_m\) with coefficient in \(\mathscr{F}(\psi\chi^{-1})\) checks

  1. \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i=0\) for \(i\ne 1\), and \(\dim \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^1=1\).

  2. \(F^\ast\), acting on \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^1\), is the multiplication by \(\tau(\chi,\psi)\).

  3. If \(\chi\) is not trivial, we have \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^\bullet \xrightarrow{\sim} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet\).

Proof▾

For everything \(n\) first to \(p\), let's note \(K_n\) the \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_n\)-torseur on \(\mathbb{G}_m\) defined by Kümmer \(0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \boldsymbol{\mu}_n \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbb{G}_m \xrightarrow{x^n} \mathbb{G}_m \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0\). If \(k\) a \(q\) elements, we have on \(k\): \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_{q-1}=k^\times\), and the torsor of Lang on \(\mathbb{G}_m/k\) is \(K_{q-1}\). From then on, \(\mathscr{F}(\chi^{-1})=\chi(K_{q-1})\). The assertion 4.2(ii) results from (i,iii), themselves contained in the following geometric statement, where \(\mathscr{F}(\psi)\) designates the sheaf on \(\mathbb{G}_m/\mathbb{F}\) deduced by extension from \(k\) to \(\mathbb{F}\) from \(\mathscr{F}(\psi)\) on \(\mathbb{G}_m/k\) (1.8.b).

C.Q.F.D.
Proposition 4.3

Either \(\chi:\boldsymbol{\mu}_n \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} E_\lambda^\times\). The cohomology of \(\mathbb{G}_m\) with coefficient in \(\mathscr{F}(\psi)\otimes \chi(K_n)\) checks

  1. \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i=0\) for \(i\ne 1\), and \(\dim \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^1{} = 1\).

  2. If \(\chi\) is not trivial, we have \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^\bullet{}\xrightarrow{\sim} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet{}\).

Proof▾

The sheaf \(\mathscr{F}(\psi)\) is the restriction to \(\mathbb{G}_m\) of a locally constant sheaf on \(\mathbb{G}_a\), wildly branched to infinity, of Swan conductor \(1\). The sheaf \(\chi(K_n)\) is constant if \(\chi=1\); if \(\chi\ne 1\), it is branched into \(0\) and \(\infty\), moderately.

The sheaf \(\mathscr{F}(\psi)\otimes\chi(K_n)\) is therefore branched to \(\infty\); applying 1.18(b,c), it is found that \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(\mathbb{G}_m,\mathscr{F}(\psi)\otimes \chi(K_n))=0\) for \(i\ne 1\). If \(\chi\ne 1\), it is branched into \(0\) and \(\infty\) and (ii) results from 1.19.a.

Swan's drivers are \(0\) in \(0\) and \(1\) in \(\infty\).3.2.1), the Euler-Poincaré feature is therefore \(-1\) and this completes the demonstration.

C.Q.F.D.

4.4 Note

If \(\chi\) is not trivial, 4.2(iii) and the duality of Poincaré show that \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^1(\mathbb{G}_m,\mathscr{F}(\psi\chi^{-1}))\) and \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^1(\mathbb{G}_m,\mathscr{F}(\psi^{-1}\chi))\) are in duality (valued duality in \(E_\lambda(-1)\)). So we have \(\tau(\chi,\psi)\cdot \tau(\chi^{-1},\psi^{-1}) = q\), i.e. \(|\tau(\chi,\psi)|=1\).

4.5

If \(k\) is a degree extension \(N\) of \(\mathbb{F}_q\), you can also look at (4.1.1) as a sum to \(N\) variables on \(\mathbb{F}_q\). Or more generally \(k\) an algebra spread over \(\mathbb{F}_q\), degree \(N\) on \(\mathbb{F}_q\). It is a field product \(k_i\), degree \(N_i\), and is installed

\[ \tag{4.5.1} \varepsilon(k) = (-1)^{\sum (N_i+1)} \text{.} \]

This is the signature of the permutation of \(S=\hom_{\mathbb{F}_q}(k,\mathbb{F})\) induced by the substitution of Frobenius \(\varphi\in \operatorname{Gal}(\mathbb{F}/\mathbb{F}_q)\).

Be \(\psi:\mathbb{F}_q\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} E_\lambda^\times\) a non-trivial character, and \(\chi:k^\times \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} E_\lambda^\times\).

\[ \tag{4.5.2} \tau_{\mathbb{F}_q}(\chi,\psi) = (-1)^N \sum_{x\in k^\times} \psi \operatorname{tr}_{k/\mathbb{F}_q}(x) \cdot \chi^{-1}(x) \text{.} \]

If \(\chi\) has for contact details \(\chi_i:k_i^\times \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} E_\lambda^\times\), we have the trivial identity

\[ \tag{4.5.3} \tau_{\mathbb{F}_q}(\chi,\psi) = \varepsilon(k) \prod \tau(\chi_i,\psi\circ \operatorname{tr}_{k_i/\mathbb{F}_q}) \text{.} \]

After a few foreplays, we will give 4.10 a cohomological interpretation of the sums (4.5.2) In 4.12, we will interpret the identity of Hasse-Davenport as a twisted form (cf. 1.12) of the following particular case of (4.5.3): for \(k=\mathbb{F}_q^N\), and \(\chi\) a character of \(\mathbb{F}_q^\times\), we have \(\tau_{\mathbb{F}_q}(\chi\circ N_{k/\mathbb{F}_q},\psi)=\tau(\chi,\psi)^N\).

4.6

It should be recalled that for \(M\) a finished projective module on a ring \(A\), the searcher \(\operatorname{Spec}(B)\mapsto M\otimes_A B\), affine diagrams on \(\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) in \(\mathsf{Ens}\) is represented by the affine scheme \(\mathbb{V}(M^\vee)\) (EGA ratings), the spectrum of \(\operatorname{Sym}_A^\bullet(M^\vee)\). If \(M=A^n\), it is the affin space type of dimension \(n\).

Suppose that \(M\) or \(A\)-Unit algebra, and posons \(V=\mathbb{V}(M^\vee)\). By definition, for any extension \(B\) of the \(A\), the whole \(V(B)\) of points of \(V\) to co-ordinates in \(B\) is \(M\otimes_A B\). The searcher \(B\mapsto M\otimes_A B\) having values in the unit rings, \(V\) is a unit ring diagram on \(\operatorname{Spec}(A)\). Standard morphisms and trace: \(M\otimes_A B\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) are functional in \(B\); they therefore correspond to schematic morphisms \(N\) and \(T\) of the \(V\) in \(\mathbb{G}_a\). We will have to consider the schemes deducted from \(V\) the following:

  1. \(V^\ast\) is the opening of the invertible elements of \(V\) (a group scheme for \(\cdot\));

  2. \(W\) is the hyperplane of equation \(T=0\) and \(W^\ast=W\cap V^\ast\);

  3. \(P\) is the infinity hyperplane \(V\setminus \{0\}/\mathbb{G}_m\) of the affin space \(V\) on \(\operatorname{Spec}(A)\). If \(Q\) is the infinitely hyperplane of \(W\), \(W^\ast/\mathbb{G}_m\) and \(V^\ast/\mathbb{G}_m\) are open projective spaces \(Q\) and \(P\) on \(\operatorname{Spec}(A)\).

    \[ \tag{4.6.1} \xymatrix{ W^\ast \ar@{^{(}->}[r] \ar[d]^-\pi & V^\ast \ar[d]^-\pi \\ W^\ast/\mathbb{G}_m \ar@{^{(}->}[r] & V^\ast/\mathbb{G}_m }\qquad\subset\qquad \xymatrix{ W\setminus \{0\} \ar@{^{(}->}[r] \ar[d]^-\pi & V\setminus \{0\} \ar[d]^-\pi \\ Q \ar@{^{(}->}[r] & P . } \]

If \(M=A^I\), \(I\) a finished set, then \(V\simeq \mathbb{G}_a^I\), \(V^\ast=\mathbb{G}_m^I\), \(V^\ast/\mathbb{G}_m\) is the tore \(\mathbb{G}_m^I/(\mathbb{G}_m\text{ diagonal})\), \(N\) and \(T\) writing \(\prod x_i\) and \(\sum x_i\), and \(Q\) is therefore the projective hyperplane of equation \(\sum x_i=0\) of \(P\).

The case we are interested in is one where \(M\) is finished on \(A\). The previous description then applies locally to \(\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) (for the outer topology) and we can write \(V^\ast=\mathbb{G}_m^I\), for \(I\) a locally constant sheaf of finished assemblies on \(\operatorname{Spec}(A)\). If for example \(A\) is a field, of algebraic fence \(\overline{A}\), and \(M\) is a \(A\)-algebra separable, we place \(I=\hom_A(M,\overline{A})\), and, on \(\overline{A}\), we have \(V\sim \mathbb{G}_a^I\). Via this isomorphism, \(N\) and \(T\) is written \(\prod x_i\) and \(\sum x_i\).

4.7 Kummer torsor

Is \(S\) a scheme, \(n\) an invertible integer on \(S\) and \(G\) a scheme in related fibre commutative groups (noted multifold) on \(S\).

\[ \xymatrix{ 0 \ar[r] & G_n \ar[r] & G \ar[r]^-{x^n} & G \ar[r] & 0 } \]

is exact. It defines a \(G_n\)-Torse \(K_n(G)\) (or simply \(K_n\)) on \(G\). For \(\chi:G_n \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} E_\lambda^\times\) a homomorphism of the \(S\)-Literal sheaf \(G_n\) in the constant sheaf \(E_\lambda^\times\), we note \(\mathscr{K}_n(\chi)\) on \(E_\lambda\)-sheaf \(\chi^{-1}(K_n)\).

The torsors \(K_n\) form a projective system of torsors under the projective system of groups \(G_n\) (transitional morphisms \(x\mapsto x^d:G_{n d}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G_n\)). This expresses the commutativity of diagrams

\[ \xymatrix{ 0 \ar[r] & G_{n d} \ar[r] \ar[d]^-{x^d} & G \ar[r]^-{x^{n d}} \ar[d]^-{x^d} & G \ar[r] \ar@{=}[d] & 0 \\ 0 \ar[r] & G_n \ar[r] & G \ar[r]^-{x^n} & G \ar[r] & 0 . } \]

So we have \(\mathscr{K}_n(\chi) = \mathscr{K}_{n d}(\chi\circ x^d)\).

4.8

Apply construction 4.6 for \(A=\mathbb{F}_q\), and \(M=k\) an algebra spread over \(\mathbb{F}_q\). In accordance with the general conventions, we will note with an index \(0\) the \(\mathbb{F}_q\)-schémas noted \(V,V^\ast,\ldots\) in 4.6. The same letters without an index refer to diagrams on \(\mathbb{F}\) which are deduced by extension of scalar.

We pose \(I=\hom(k,\mathbb{F})\), from where \(V\sim \mathbb{G}_a^I\) and \(T\) is written \((x_i)\mapsto \sum x_i\).

\[ \tag{4.8.1} \mathscr{F}(\psi\circ \operatorname{tr}_{k/\mathbb{F}_q}) = T^\ast \mathscr{F}(\psi) = \bigotimes_i \mathrm{pr}_i^\ast \mathscr{F}(\psi) \text{.} \]

Since \(V^\ast\sim \mathbb{G}+m^I\), a character \(\chi\) of \(V_n^\ast\sim \boldsymbol{\mu}_n^I\), at values in \(E_\lambda^\times\), is written as a family \((\chi_i)_{i\in I}\) of characters of \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_n\) indeled by \(I\). It is defined on \(\mathbb{F}_q\) if, for all \(\sigma\in \operatorname{Gal}(\mathbb{F}/\mathbb{F}_q)\), we have \(\chi_{\sigma_i} = \chi_i\circ \sigma^{-1}\). It then defines a \(E_\lambda\)-sheaf \(\mathscr{K}_n((\chi_i)_{i\in I})\) on \(V_0^\ast\). If the product of \(\chi_i\) is trivial, \(\chi\) is factored into a character of \((V_0^\ast/\mathbb{G}_m)_n\) and \(\mathscr{K}_n((\chi_i)_{i\in I})\) is the reciprocal image of a sheaf, also noted, on \(V-0^\ast/\mathbb{G}_m\). This construction compares to the torsor of Lang.

Lemma 4.9

  1. For \(n\) quite divisible, we have a commutative diagram

    \[ \xymatrix{ 0 \ar[r] & (V_0^\ast)_n \ar[r] \ar[d]^-\tau & V_0^\ast \ar[r]^-{x^n} \ar[d]^-\tau & V_0^\ast \ar[r] \ar@{=}[d] & 0 \\ 0 \ar[r] & k^\times \ar[r] & V_0^\ast \ar[r]^-{\mathfrak{L}} & V_0^\ast \ar[r] & 0 ; } \]

    if \(\chi\) is a character \(\chi:k^\times \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} E_\lambda^\times\), we have \(\mathscr{F}(\chi) = \mathscr{K}_n(\chi\circ\tau)\).

  2. For \(n\) rather divisible, \(\tau\) identifies \(k^\times\) to the variable corners of \(\operatorname{Gal}(\overline{\mathbb{F}}/F)\) acting on \(V_n^\ast\sim \boldsymbol{\mu}_n^I\).

  3. For \(k\) a field, \(N=[k:\mathbb{F}_q]\), \(\omega\in I\) a diving of \(k\) in \(\mathbb{F}\), and \(n=q^N-1\), the index component \(\omega\) of \(\chi\circ\tau\) is \(\chi\circ \omega^{-1}\).

  4. If \(\chi\) is non-trivial on each factor of \(k\), all \((\chi\circ \tau)_i\) are non-trivial.

All you have to do is to prove the lemma when \(k\) is a field, of degree \(N\) on \(\mathbb{F}_q\). In this case, \(n\) is "sufficiently divisible" if \(q^N-1\mid n\). Let's choose a dive \(\omega\) from \(k\) in \(\mathbb{F}_q\); \(I\) is then identified with \(\mathbb{Z}/N\): to \(i\in \mathbb{Z}/N\) corresponds \(\omega_i=\omega^{q^i}\). Via isomorphism \(V_0^\ast(\mathbb{F}) = {F^\times}^I\), we have

  1. \(x\in k^\times\) corresponds to \((\omega_i(x))\in {\mathbb{F}^\times}^I\);

  2. \(F((x_i)_{i\in \mathbb{Z}/N})=(x_{i-1}^q)_{i\in \mathbb{Z}/N}\).

A character \(\chi=(\chi_i)\) of \(V_n^\ast\sim \boldsymbol{\mu}_n^I\) will be set to \(\mathbb{F}_q\) if \(\chi_{-i} =\chi_0(x^{q^i})\) (\(i\in \mathbb{Z}\)). If \(q^N-1\mid n\), there are \(q^N-1\) such characters: \(\chi_0\) is factored by \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_{q^N-1}\), and determines the \(\chi_i\). To prove (ii), it is sufficient to verify (i) and (iii) (or its corollary (iv)) that ensures that \(\chi\mapsto \chi\circ \tau\) is injective.

Prove (i) and (iii), for \(n=q^N-1\). Note the endomorphism group of \(V^\ast\sim \mathbb{G}_m^I\); in particular, note \(n\) the operator \(x\mapsto x^n\). If \(\alpha\) is the circular permutation operator \((x_i)\mapsto (x_{i-1})\), we have \(q^N-1=(q\alpha)^N-1 = (q\alpha-1)((q\alpha)^{N-1}+\cdots + 1)\). This determines \(\tau\): we have \(\tau((x_i)) = \prod_{0\leqslant j<N} x_{i-j}^{q^j}\), and the induced application of \((V_0^\ast)_n\) in \(k^\times\) is written [(a) above] \((x_i)\mapsto \prod x_{-i}^{q^i}\); hence results (iii).

Proposition 4.10

The cohomology of \(V^\ast\) with coefficients in \(\mathscr{F}(\chi^{-1}\cdot \psi\operatorname{tr}_{k/\mathbb{F}_q})\) verifies

  1. \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i = 0\) for \(i\ne N\), and \(\dim \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^N=1\).

  2. On \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^N\), \(F^\ast\) is the multiplication by \(\tau_{\mathbb{F}_q}(\chi,\psi)\).

  3. If \(\chi\) is not trivial on each factor of \(k\), we have \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^\bullet \xrightarrow{\sim} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet\).

Apply 4.9(i): on \(\mathbb{F}\), if \(\chi\circ \tau=(\chi_i)_{i\in I}\), we have \(\mathscr{F}(\chi) = \mathscr{K}_n((\chi_i)_{i\in I})\). Items (i) and (iii) resulting from the following more geometric statement (for (iii), apply 4.9(iv)) and (ii) result by the following formula:

Proposition 4.11

Either \((\chi_i)_{i\in I}\) a family of \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_n(\mathbb{F})\). The cohomology of \(V^\ast\simeq \mathbb{G}_m^I\) with coefficient in \(\mathscr{K}_n((\chi_i)_{i\in I})\otimes \mathscr{F}(\psi\operatorname{tr}_{k/\mathbb{F}_q})\) checks

  1. \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i{}=0\) for \(i\ne N\), and \(\dim \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^N{} = 1\).

  2. If the \(\chi_i\) are all non-trivial, we have \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^\bullet{}\xrightarrow{\sim} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet\).

We have \(V^\ast\sim \mathbb{G}_m^I\), \(\mathscr{K}((\chi_i)) = \bigotimes_i \mathrm{pr}_i^\ast \chi_i(K_n(\mathbb{G}_m))\) and \(\mathscr{F}(\psi\circ \operatorname{tr}_{\mathbb{F}_q/k})=T^\ast \mathscr{F}(\psi) = \bigotimes_i \mathrm{pr}_i^\ast \mathscr{F}(\psi)\). This allows the application of the Künneth formula, and 4.11 as a result of 4.3.

4.12

From 2.4* and ([VI]Cycle, 1.3 example 2), it is also drawn that the permutation group \(\sigma\) of \(I\) such as \(\chi_i = \chi_{\sigma i}\) (\(i\in I\)) acts on \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^N\) by multiplication by signature \(\varepsilon(\sigma)\). We will deduce a second proof of the identity of Hasse-Davenport.

If \(\chi\) is a character of \(\mathbb{F}_q^\times\), the fact that on \(\mathbb{F}\), \(N\) is written \((x_i)\mapsto \prod x_i\) provides: \(\mathscr{F}(\chi\circ N)=N^\ast\mathscr{F}(\chi) = \bigotimes_i \mathrm{pr}_i^\ast\mathscr{F}(\psi)\) and the Künneth formula provides:

\[ \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^\bullet(V^\ast,\mathscr{F}(\chi^{-1}\circ N,\psi\circ \operatorname{tr})) \sim \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^\bullet(\mathbb{G}_m,\mathscr{F}(\chi^{-1}\psi))^{\otimes I} \text{,} \]

where, at the right-hand side, the tensor product is taken in the sense 2.4*. Since \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^1(\mathbb{G}_m,\mathscr{F}(\psi\chi^{-1}))\) is of size \(1\), its tensor power \(\otimes I\), in the ordinary sense, depends only on the cardinal \(n\) of \(I\). Appliant ([IV]Cycle, 1.3 example 2), canonical isomorphism is obtained

\[ \tag{4.12.1} \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^N\left(V,\mathscr{F}(\chi^{-1}\circ N)\otimes T^\ast \mathscr{F}(\psi)\right) \sim \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^1(\mathbb{G}_m,\mathscr{F}(\psi\chi^{-1})) \otimes_\mathbb{Z} \textstyle \bigwedge^N \mathbb{Z}^I \text{.} \]

To calculate the action of \(F^\ast\), the most convenient is to adopt the Galoisian point of view and say that isomorphism (4.12.1) being canonical, it is compatible with the action by structural transport of \(\operatorname{Gal}(\mathbb{F}/\mathbb{F}_q)\). If \(\varepsilon(k)\) is the signature of the permutation \(\varphi\) of \(I\), we find that

\begin{align*} \tau_{\mathbb{F}_q}(\chi\circ N_{k/\mathbb{F}_q},\psi) &= \operatorname{tr}\left(\varphi^{-1},\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^N\left(V^\ast,N^\ast\mathscr{F}(\chi^{-1})\otimes T^\ast \mathscr{F}(\psi)\right)\right) \\ &= \varepsilon(k) \operatorname{tr}\left(\varphi^{-1},\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^1(\mathbb{G}_m,\mathscr{F}(\chi^{-1}\psi))\right)^N \\ &= \varepsilon(k) \tau(\chi,\psi)^N \text{,} \end{align*}

either

\[ \tag{4.12.2} \tau_{\mathbb{F}_q}\left(\chi\circ N_{k/\mathbb{F}_q},\psi\right) = \varepsilon(k) \tau(\chi,\psi)^N \text{.} \]

If \(k\) is a field, \(\varphi\) is a circular permutation of \(I\), \(\varepsilon(k) = (-1)^{N+1}\), and we find the identity of Hasse-Davenport:

\[ \tau\left(\chi\circ N_{k/\mathbb{F}_q},\psi\circ \operatorname{tr}_{k/\mathbb{F}_q}\right) = (-1)^{N+1} \tau_{\mathbb{F}_q}\left(\chi\circ N_{k/\mathbb{F}_q},\psi\right) = \tau(\chi,\psi)^N \text{.} \]
Lemma 4.13

For \(\chi\circ \tau=(\chi_i)_{i\in I}\) as in 4.9(i), the following conditions are equivalent

  1. \(\chi| \mathbb{F}_q^\times\) is trivial

  2. The product of \(\chi_i\) is trivial

  3. \(\mathscr{F}(\chi) = \mathscr{K}_n((\chi_i))\) is a reciprocal image of a (single) sheaf on \(V^\ast/\mathbb{G}_m\).

  4. The reciprocal image of \(\mathscr{F}(\chi)\) on \(\mathbb{G}_m\) (sent in \(V^\ast\) from structural morphism \(\mathbb{F}_q \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} k\)) is trivial.

(i)\(\Rightarrow\)(ii). We look at \(\chi\) as a character of \(V^\ast/\mathbb{G}_m(\mathbb{F}_q)=k^\times/\mathbb{F}_q^\times\), and we take \(\mathscr{F}(\chi)\) on \(V^\ast/\mathbb{G}_m\).

(ii)\(\Rightarrow\)(iii). Similarly, we look at \((\chi_i)\) as a character of \((V^\ast/\mathbb{G}_m)_n\). The uniqueness in (iii) results from the fact that \(V^\ast\) is a related fibre fibride on \(V^\ast/\mathbb{G}_m\), and (iii)\(\Rightarrow\)(iv) is trivial.

(iv)\(\Rightarrow\)(i), (ii). This reciprocal image is \(\mathscr{F}(\chi|\mathbb{F}_q^\times)\) and \(\mathscr{K}_n(\prod \chi_i)\).

4.14

Been \(k\) an algebra spreads on \(\mathbb{F}_q\), dimensional \(N+1\) and \(\chi\) a non-trivial character of \(k^\times\), trivial on \(\mathbb{F}_q^\times\). The sum of Jacobi \(J(\chi)\) is defined by

\[ \tag{4.14.1} J(\chi) = (-1)^{N-1} \sum_{\substack{x\in k^\times/\mathbb{F}_q^\times \\ \operatorname{tr}(x)=0}} \chi^{-1}(x) \text{.} \]

We have between Gauss and Jacobi the following identity.

Proposition 4.15

For \(\chi\) as above and \(\psi\) a non-trivial additive character of \(\mathbb{F}_q\),

\[ q J(\chi) = \tau_{\mathbb{F}_q}(\chi,\psi) \text{.} \]

In the particular case where \(k=\mathbb{F}_q^n\), this formula is rewritten by (4.5.3)

\[ \tag{4.15.1} q J(\chi) = \prod_i \tau(\chi_i,\psi) \text{,} \]

more written in the form

\begin{align*} \chi_0(-1) J(\chi) &= (-1)^{N-1} \sum_{\substack{x_1,\dots,x_N\in \mathbb{F}_q^\times \\ \sum x_i = 1}} \prod_{i=1}^N \chi_i^{-1}(x_i) = \tau(\chi_0^{-1},\psi)^{-1} \prod_{i=1}^N \tau(\chi_i,\psi) \\ \chi_0^{-1} &= \prod_{i=1}^N \chi_i \end{align*}
Proof of 4.15▾
\[ \tau_{\mathbb{F}_q}(\chi,\psi) = (-1)^{N+1} \sum_{x\in k^\times} \chi(x)^{-1} \psi \operatorname{tr}(x) = (-1)^{N+1} \sum_{x\in k^\times/\mathbb{F}_q^\times} \chi(x)^{-1} \sum_{\lambda\in \mathbb{F}_q^\times} \psi(\lambda \operatorname{tr} x) \text{.} \]

The sum \(\sum_{\lambda\in \mathbb{F}_q^\times} \psi(\lambda \operatorname{tr} x)\) is \(q-1\) if \(\operatorname{tr}(x)=0\), and \(-1\) if \(\operatorname{tr}(x)\ne 0\).

\[ \tau_{\mathbb{F}_q}(\chi,\psi) = (-1)^{N+1} \left(q\sum_{x\in k^\times/\mathbb{F}_q^\times} \chi(x)^{-1} - \sum_{x\in k^\times/\mathbb{F}_q^\times} \chi(x)^{-1}\right) \text{.} \]

The second term in the second member is null, because sum of the values of a character, and 4.15 As a result.

C.Q.F.D.

The proposal 4.15 translates into cohomology as follows:

The cohomology of \(W^\ast/\mathbb{G}_m\) (4.6, 4.8) with coefficient in \(\mathscr{F}(\chi^{-1})\) checks

The assertion (ii) results from (i) and the formula of the traces; (i) and (iii) result from 4.11 and the following more geometric statement.

Proposition 4.17

Either \((\chi_i)_{i\in I}\) a non-trivial character family of \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_n(\mathbb{F})\), product \(1\). So, \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^{i-1}\left(W^\ast/\mathbb{G}_m,\mathscr{K}_m((\chi_i)_{i\in I})\right)\) is canonically isomorphic to \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^{i+1}\left(V^\ast,\mathscr{K}_m((\chi_i)_{i\in I})\otimes \mathscr{F}(\psi\operatorname{tr}_{k/\mathbb{F}_q})\right)(1)\).

The first line of 4.15 becomes (\(\pi\) as in (4.6.1)).

\[ \tag{4.17.1} \mathsf{R}^i \pi_!\left(\mathscr{K}_n((\chi_i)_{i\in I})\otimes \mathscr{F}(\psi \operatorname{tr}_{k/\mathbb{F}_q})\right) = \mathscr{K}_n((\chi_i)_{i\in I}) \otimes \mathsf{R}^i \pi_! \mathscr{F}(\psi\operatorname{tr}_{k/\mathbb{F}_q}) \]

(on \(V^\ast/\mathbb{G}_m\)). Let's calculate the sheaves \(\mathsf{R}^i\pi_! \mathscr{F}(\psi\operatorname{tr}_{k/\mathbb{F}_q}) = \mathsf{R}^i \pi_! T^\ast \mathscr{F}(\psi)\). If \(x\in P\), \(x\notin Q\), the right \(D=\pi^{-1}(x)\) is sent isomorphically on \(\mathbb{G}_a\) by \(T v\), so we have 2.7*)

(T v)^\ast \mathscr{F}(\psi) &= \begin{cases} 0 & \text{for } i\ne 2 \\ E_\lambda(-1)_Q & \text{for } i=2 \end{cases} \end{align*}

Conjugating (4.17.3) and (4.17.5), we finally find

4.18

Let's calculate the sheaf's compact support cohomology \(\mathscr{K}_n((\chi_i))\otimes T^\ast \mathscr{F}(\psi)\) on \(V^\ast\) using the Leray spectral sequence of \(\pi:V^\ast\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} V^\ast/\mathbb{G}_m\). 4.19

The sheaves \(\mathscr{K}_n((\chi_i)_{i\in I})\) have a meaning on any field, or even on any basic scheme. This will allow us to generalize 4.16(i) With the ratings of 4.6, suppose \(M\) finished spreads everywhere in the range \(N\) on \(A\), and be \(n\) an invertible integer in \(A\). Proposition 4.20

The diagram \(W^\ast/\mathbb{G}_m\) is the complement of a divisor with normal relative crosses in the diagram \(Q\), clean and smooth on \(\operatorname{Spec}(A)\), and \(\mathscr{K}_n(\chi)\) is locally constant on \(W^\ast/\mathbb{G}_m\), with moderate branching to infinity. As a result, the \(\mathsf{R}^i a_!\) and \(\mathsf{R}^i a_\ast\) are smooth, of formation compatible with any basic change. A standard argument then leads us to assume that \(A\) is a finite field, and (i) results from 4.17 and 4.11(i) To prove (ii), the action of \(\sigma\) is compatible with isomorphism 4.17, and 4.12. For (iii), we note that if \(\chi_i\) are all non-trivial, then \(\mathscr{K}_n(\chi)\) on \(W^\ast/\mathbb{G}_m\subset Q\) is branched along each divisor to infinity, and 1.19.1.

More

Proposition 4.2

The cohomology of \(\mathbb{G}_m\) with coefficient in \(\mathscr{F}(\psi\chi^{-1})\) checks

If \(k\) a \(q\) elements, we have on \(k\): \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_{q-1}=k^\times\), and the torsor of Lang on \(\mathbb{G}_m/k\) is \(K_{q-1}\). From then on, \(\mathscr{F}(\chi^{-1})=\chi(K_{q-1})\). The assertion 4.2(ii) results from (i,iii), themselves contained in the following geometric statement, where \(\mathscr{F}(\psi)\) designates the sheaf on \(\mathbb{G}_m/\mathbb{F}\) deduced by extension from \(k\) to \(\mathbb{F}\) from \(\mathscr{F}(\psi)\) on \(\mathbb{G}_m/k\) (1.8.b).

Either \(\chi:\boldsymbol{\mu}_n \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} E_\lambda^\times\). The cohomology of \(\mathbb{G}_m\) with coefficient in \(\mathscr{F}(\psi)\otimes \chi(K_n)\) checks

blocks.title: Proof
blocks.html:
Proof▾

The sheaf \(\mathscr{F}(\psi)\) is the restriction to \(\mathbb{G}_m\) of a locally constant sheaf on \(\mathbb{G}_a\), wildly branched to infinity, of Swan conductor \(1\). The sheaf \(\chi(K_n)\) is constant if \(\chi=1\); if \(\chi\ne 1\), it is branched into \(0\) and \(\infty\), moderately.

The sheaf \(\mathscr{F}(\psi)\otimes\chi(K_n)\) is therefore branched to \(\infty\); applying 1.18(b,c), it is found that \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(\mathbb{G}_m,\mathscr{F}(\psi)\otimes \chi(K_n))=0\) for \(i\ne 1\). If \(\chi\ne 1\), it is branched into \(0\) and \(\infty\) and (ii) results from 1.19.a.

Swan's drivers are \(0\) in \(0\) and \(1\) in \(\infty\).3.2.1), the Euler-Poincaré feature is therefore \(-1\) and this completes the demonstration.

C.Q.F.D.

If \(\chi\) is not trivial, 4.2(iii) and the duality of Poincaré show that \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^1(\mathbb{G}_m,\mathscr{F}(\psi\chi^{-1}))\) and \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^1(\mathbb{G}_m,\mathscr{F}(\psi^{-1}\chi))\) are in duality (valued duality in \(E_\lambda(-1)\)). So we have \(\tau(\chi,\psi)\cdot \tau(\chi^{-1},\psi^{-1}) = q\), i.e. \(|\tau(\chi,\psi)|=1\).

If \(k\) is a degree extension \(N\) of \(\mathbb{F}_q\), you can also look at (4.1.1) as a sum to \(N\) variables on \(\mathbb{F}_q\). Or more generally \(k\) an algebra spread over \(\mathbb{F}_q\), degree \(N\) on \(\mathbb{F}_q\). It is a field product \(k_i\), degree \(N_i\), and is installed
\[ \tag{4.5.1} \varepsilon(k) = (-1)^{\sum (N_i+1)} \text{.} \]
This is the signature of the permutation of \(S=\hom_{\mathbb{F}_q}(k,\mathbb{F})\) induced by the substitution of Frobenius \(\varphi\in \operatorname{Gal}(\mathbb{F}/\mathbb{F}_q)\).

Be \(\psi:\mathbb{F}_q\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} E_\lambda^\times\) a non-trivial character, and \(\chi:k^\times \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} E_\lambda^\times\).
\[ \tag{4.5.2} \tau_{\mathbb{F}_q}(\chi,\psi) = (-1)^N \sum_{x\in k^\times} \psi \operatorname{tr}_{k/\mathbb{F}_q}(x) \cdot \chi^{-1}(x) \text{.} \]
If \(\chi\) has for contact details \(\chi_i:k_i^\times \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} E_\lambda^\times\), we have the trivial identity
\[ \tag{4.5.3} \tau_{\mathbb{F}_q}(\chi,\psi) = \varepsilon(k) \prod \tau(\chi_i,\psi\circ \operatorname{tr}_{k_i/\mathbb{F}_q}) \text{.} \]

After a few foreplays, we will give 4.10 a cohomological interpretation of the sums (4.5.2) In 4.12, we will interpret the identity of Hasse-Davenport as a twisted form (cf. 1.12) of the following particular case of (4.5.3): for \(k=\mathbb{F}_q^N\), and \(\chi\) a character of \(\mathbb{F}_q^\times\), we have \(\tau_{\mathbb{F}_q}(\chi\circ N_{k/\mathbb{F}_q},\psi)=\tau(\chi,\psi)^N\).

It should be recalled that for \(M\) a finished projective module on a ring \(A\), the searcher \(\operatorname{Spec}(B)\mapsto M\otimes_A B\), affine diagrams on \(\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) in \(\mathsf{Ens}\) is represented by the affine scheme \(\mathbb{V}(M^\vee)\) (EGA ratings), the spectrum of \(\operatorname{Sym}_A^\bullet(M^\vee)\). If \(M=A^n\), it is the affin space type of dimension \(n\).

Suppose that \(M\) or \(A\)-Unit algebra, and posons \(V=\mathbb{V}(M^\vee)\). By definition, for any extension \(B\) of the \(A\), the whole \(V(B)\) of points of \(V\) to co-ordinates in \(B\) is \(M\otimes_A B\). The searcher \(B\mapsto M\otimes_A B\) having values in the unit rings, \(V\) is a unit ring diagram on \(\operatorname{Spec}(A)\). Standard morphisms and trace: \(M\otimes_A B\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) are functional in \(B\); they therefore correspond to schematic morphisms \(N\) and \(T\) of the \(V\) in \(\mathbb{G}_a\). We will have to consider the schemes deducted from \(V\) the following:

1. \(V^\ast\) is the opening of the invertible elements of \(V\) (a group scheme for \(\cdot\));

  2. \(W\) is the hyperplane of equation \(T=0\) and \(W^\ast=W\cap V^\ast\);

If \(Q\) is the infinitely hyperplane of \(W\), \(W^\ast/\mathbb{G}_m\) and \(V^\ast/\mathbb{G}_m\) are open projective spaces \(Q\) and \(P\) on \(\operatorname{Spec}(A)\).

    \[ \tag{4.6.1} \xymatrix{ W^\ast \ar@{^{(}->}[r] \ar[d]^-\pi & V^\ast \ar[d]^-\pi \\ W^\ast/\mathbb{G}_m \ar@{^{(}->}[r] & V^\ast/\mathbb{G}_m }\qquad\subset\qquad \xymatrix{ W\setminus \{0\} \ar@{^{(}->}[r] \ar[d]^-\pi & V\setminus \{0\} \ar[d]^-\pi \\ Q \ar@{^{(}->}[r] & P . } \]

If \(M=A^I\), \(I\) a finished set, then \(V\simeq \mathbb{G}_a^I\), \(V^\ast=\mathbb{G}_m^I\), \(V^\ast/\mathbb{G}_m\) is the tore \(\mathbb{G}_m^I/(\mathbb{G}_m\text{ diagonal})\), \(N\) and \(T\) writing \(\prod x_i\) and \(\sum x_i\), and \(Q\) is therefore the projective hyperplane of equation \(\sum x_i=0\) of \(P\).

The case we are interested in is one where \(M\) is finished on \(A\). The previous description then applies locally to \(\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) (for the outer topology) and we can write \(V^\ast=\mathbb{G}_m^I\), for \(I\) a locally constant sheaf of finished assemblies on \(\operatorname{Spec}(A)\). 4.7 Kummer torsor
Is \(S\) a scheme, \(n\) an invertible integer on \(S\) and \(G\) a scheme in related fibre commutative groups (noted multifold) on \(S\).
\[ \xymatrix{ 0 \ar[r] & G_n \ar[r] & G \ar[r]^-{x^n} & G \ar[r] & 0 } \]

is exact. It defines a \(G_n\)-Torse \(K_n(G)\) (or simply \(K_n\)) on \(G\). For \(\chi:G_n \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} E_\lambda^\times\) a homomorphism of the \(S\)-Literal sheaf \(G_n\) in the constant sheaf \(E_\lambda^\times\), we note \(\mathscr{K}_n(\chi)\) on \(E_\lambda\)-sheaf \(\chi^{-1}(K_n)\).

The torsors \(K_n\) form a projective system of torsors under the projective system of groups \(G_n\) (transitional morphisms \(x\mapsto x^d:G_{n d}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G_n\)). This expresses the commutativity of diagrams
\[ \xymatrix{ 0 \ar[r] & G_{n d} \ar[r] \ar[d]^-{x^d} & G \ar[r]^-{x^{n d}} \ar[d]^-{x^d} & G \ar[r] \ar@{=}[d] & 0 \\ 0 \ar[r] & G_n \ar[r] & G \ar[r]^-{x^n} & G \ar[r] & 0 . } \]
So we have \(\mathscr{K}_n(\chi) = \mathscr{K}_{n d}(\chi\circ x^d)\).

Apply construction 4.6 for \(A=\mathbb{F}_q\), and \(M=k\) an algebra spread over \(\mathbb{F}_q\). In accordance with the general conventions, we will note with an index \(0\) the \(\mathbb{F}_q\)-schémas noted \(V,V^\ast,\ldots\) in 4.6. \[ \tag{4.8.1} \mathscr{F}(\psi\circ \operatorname{tr}_{k/\mathbb{F}_q}) = T^\ast \mathscr{F}(\psi) = \bigotimes_i \mathrm{pr}_i^\ast \mathscr{F}(\psi) \text{.} \]

Since \(V^\ast\sim \mathbb{G}+m^I\), a character \(\chi\) of \(V_n^\ast\sim \boldsymbol{\mu}_n^I\), at values in \(E_\lambda^\times\), is written as a family \((\chi_i)_{i\in I}\) of characters of \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_n\) indeled by \(I\). blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.9

  1. For \(n\) quite divisible, we have a commutative diagram

    \[ \xymatrix{ 0 \ar[r] & (V_0^\ast)_n \ar[r] \ar[d]^-\tau & V_0^\ast \ar[r]^-{x^n} \ar[d]^-\tau & V_0^\ast \ar[r] \ar@{=}[d] & 0 \\ 0 \ar[r] & k^\times \ar[r] & V_0^\ast \ar[r]^-{\mathfrak{L}} & V_0^\ast \ar[r] & 0 ; } \]

    if \(\chi\) is a character \(\chi:k^\times \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} E_\lambda^\times\), we have \(\mathscr{F}(\chi) = \mathscr{K}_n(\chi\circ\tau)\).

  2. For \(n\) rather divisible, \(\tau\) identifies \(k^\times\) to the variable corners of \(\operatorname{Gal}(\overline{\mathbb{F}}/F)\) acting on \(V_n^\ast\sim \boldsymbol{\mu}_n^I\).

  3. For \(k\) a field, \(N=[k:\mathbb{F}_q]\), \(\omega\in I\) a diving of \(k\) in \(\mathbb{F}\), and \(n=q^N-1\), the index component \(\omega\) of \(\chi\circ\tau\) is \(\chi\circ \omega^{-1}\).

  4. If \(\chi\) is non-trivial on each factor of \(k\), all \((\chi\circ \tau)_i\) are non-trivial.

All you have to do is to prove the lemma when \(k\) is a field, of degree \(N\) on \(\mathbb{F}_q\). In this case, \(n\) is "sufficiently divisible" if \(q^N-1\mid n\). Let's choose a dive \(\omega\) from \(k\) in \(\mathbb{F}_q\); \(I\) is then identified with \(\mathbb{Z}/N\): to \(i\in \mathbb{Z}/N\) corresponds \(\omega_i=\omega^{q^i}\). Via isomorphism \(V_0^\ast(\mathbb{F}) = {F^\times}^I\), we have

1. \(x\in k^\times\) corresponds to \((\omega_i(x))\in {\mathbb{F}^\times}^I\);

  2. \(F((x_i)_{i\in \mathbb{Z}/N})=(x_{i-1}^q)_{i\in \mathbb{Z}/N}\).

A character \(\chi=(\chi_i)\) of \(V_n^\ast\sim \boldsymbol{\mu}_n^I\) will be set to \(\mathbb{F}_q\) if \(\chi_{-i} =\chi_0(x^{q^i})\) (\(i\in \mathbb{Z}\)). If \(q^N-1\mid n\), there are \(q^N-1\) such characters: \(\chi_0\) is factored by \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_{q^N-1}\), and determines the \(\chi_i\). To prove (ii), it is sufficient to verify (i) and (iii) (or its corollary (iv)) that ensures that \(\chi\mapsto \chi\circ \tau\) is injective.

Prove (i) and (iii), for \(n=q^N-1\). Note the endomorphism group of \(V^\ast\sim \mathbb{G}_m^I\); in particular, note \(n\) the operator \(x\mapsto x^n\). If \(\alpha\) is the circular permutation operator \((x_i)\mapsto (x_{i-1})\), we have \(q^N-1=(q\alpha)^N-1 = (q\alpha-1)((q\alpha)^{N-1}+\cdots + 1)\). This determines \(\tau\): we have \(\tau((x_i)) = \prod_{0\leqslant j<N} x_{i-j}^{q^j}\), and the induced application of \((V_0^\ast)_n\) in \(k^\times\) is written [(a) above] \((x_i)\mapsto \prod x_{-i}^{q^i}\); hence results (iii).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.10

The cohomology of \(V^\ast\) with coefficients in \(\mathscr{F}(\chi^{-1}\cdot \psi\operatorname{tr}_{k/\mathbb{F}_q})\) verifies

  1. \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i = 0\) for \(i\ne N\), and \(\dim \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^N=1\).

  2. On \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^N\), \(F^\ast\) is the multiplication by \(\tau_{\mathbb{F}_q}(\chi,\psi)\).

  3. If \(\chi\) is not trivial on each factor of \(k\), we have \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^\bullet \xrightarrow{\sim} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet\).

Apply 4.9(i): on \(\mathbb{F}\), if \(\chi\circ \tau=(\chi_i)_{i\in I}\), we have \(\mathscr{F}(\chi) = \mathscr{K}_n((\chi_i)_{i\in I})\). Items (i) and (iii) resulting from the following more geometric statement (for (iii), apply 4.9(iv)) and (ii) result by the following formula:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.11

Either \((\chi_i)_{i\in I}\) a family of \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_n(\mathbb{F})\). The cohomology of \(V^\ast\simeq \mathbb{G}_m^I\) with coefficient in \(\mathscr{K}_n((\chi_i)_{i\in I})\otimes \mathscr{F}(\psi\operatorname{tr}_{k/\mathbb{F}_q})\) checks

  1. \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i{}=0\) for \(i\ne N\), and \(\dim \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^N{} = 1\).

  2. If the \(\chi_i\) are all non-trivial, we have \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^\bullet{}\xrightarrow{\sim} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet\).

We have \(V^\ast\sim \mathbb{G}_m^I\), \(\mathscr{K}((\chi_i)) = \bigotimes_i \mathrm{pr}_i^\ast \chi_i(K_n(\mathbb{G}_m))\) and \(\mathscr{F}(\psi\circ \operatorname{tr}_{\mathbb{F}_q/k})=T^\ast \mathscr{F}(\psi) = \bigotimes_i \mathrm{pr}_i^\ast \mathscr{F}(\psi)\). This allows the application of the Künneth formula, and 4.11 as a result of 4.3.

From 2.4* and ([VI]Cycle, 1.3 example 2), it is also drawn that the permutation group \(\sigma\) of \(I\) such as \(\chi_i = \chi_{\sigma i}\) (\(i\in I\)) acts on \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^N\) by multiplication by signature \(\varepsilon(\sigma)\). We will deduce a second proof of the identity of Hasse-Davenport.

If \(\chi\) is a character of \(\mathbb{F}_q^\times\), the fact that on \(\mathbb{F}\), \(N\) is written \((x_i)\mapsto \prod x_i\) provides: \(\mathscr{F}(\chi\circ N)=N^\ast\mathscr{F}(\chi) = \bigotimes_i \mathrm{pr}_i^\ast\mathscr{F}(\psi)\) and the Künneth formula provides:

\[ \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^\bullet(V^\ast,\mathscr{F}(\chi^{-1}\circ N,\psi\circ \operatorname{tr})) \sim \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^\bullet(\mathbb{G}_m,\mathscr{F}(\chi^{-1}\psi))^{\otimes I} \text{,} \]

where, at the right-hand side, the tensor product is taken in the sense 2.4*. Since \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^1(\mathbb{G}_m,\mathscr{F}(\psi\chi^{-1}))\) is of size \(1\), its tensor power \(\otimes I\), in the ordinary sense, depends only on the cardinal \(n\) of \(I\). Appliant ([IV]Cycle, 1.3 example 2), canonical isomorphism is obtained

\[ \tag{4.12.1} \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^N\left(V,\mathscr{F}(\chi^{-1}\circ N)\otimes T^\ast \mathscr{F}(\psi)\right) \sim \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^1(\mathbb{G}_m,\mathscr{F}(\psi\chi^{-1})) \otimes_\mathbb{Z} \textstyle \bigwedge^N \mathbb{Z}^I \text{.} \]

To calculate the action of \(F^\ast\), the most convenient is to adopt the Galoisian point of view and say that isomorphism (4.12.1) being canonical, it is compatible with the action by structural transport of \(\operatorname{Gal}(\mathbb{F}/\mathbb{F}_q)\). If \(\varepsilon(k)\) is the signature of the permutation \(\varphi\) of \(I\), we find that

\begin{align*} \tau_{\mathbb{F}_q}(\chi\circ N_{k/\mathbb{F}_q},\psi) &= \operatorname{tr}\left(\varphi^{-1},\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^N\left(V^\ast,N^\ast\mathscr{F}(\chi^{-1})\otimes T^\ast \mathscr{F}(\psi)\right)\right) \\ &= \varepsilon(k) \operatorname{tr}\left(\varphi^{-1},\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^1(\mathbb{G}_m,\mathscr{F}(\chi^{-1}\psi))\right)^N \\ &= \varepsilon(k) \tau(\chi,\psi)^N \text{,} \end{align*}
either
\[ \tag{4.12.2} \tau_{\mathbb{F}_q}\left(\chi\circ N_{k/\mathbb{F}_q},\psi\right) = \varepsilon(k) \tau(\chi,\psi)^N \text{.} \]
If \(k\) is a field, \(\varphi\) is a circular permutation of \(I\), \(\varepsilon(k) = (-1)^{N+1}\), and we find the identity of Hasse-Davenport:

\[ \tau\left(\chi\circ N_{k/\mathbb{F}_q},\psi\circ \operatorname{tr}_{k/\mathbb{F}_q}\right) = (-1)^{N+1} \tau_{\mathbb{F}_q}\left(\chi\circ N_{k/\mathbb{F}_q},\psi\right) = \tau(\chi,\psi)^N \text{.} \]

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.13

For \(\chi\circ \tau=(\chi_i)_{i\in I}\) as in 4.9(i), the following conditions are equivalent

  2. The product of \(\chi_i\) is trivial

  4. The reciprocal image of \(\mathscr{F}(\chi)\) on \(\mathbb{G}_m\) (sent in \(V^\ast\) from structural morphism \(\mathbb{F}_q \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} k\)) is trivial.
(i)\(\Rightarrow\)(ii). We look at \(\chi\) as a character of \(V^\ast/\mathbb{G}_m(\mathbb{F}_q)=k^\times/\mathbb{F}_q^\times\), and we take \(\mathscr{F}(\chi)\) on \(V^\ast/\mathbb{G}_m\).

(ii)\(\Rightarrow\)(iii). Similarly, we look at \((\chi_i)\) as a character of \((V^\ast/\mathbb{G}_m)_n\). The uniqueness in (iii) results from the fact that \(V^\ast\) is a related fibre fibride on \(V^\ast/\mathbb{G}_m\), and (iii)\(\Rightarrow\)(iv) is trivial.
(iv)\(\Rightarrow\)(i), (ii). This reciprocal image is \(\mathscr{F}(\chi|\mathbb{F}_q^\times)\) and \(\mathscr{K}_n(\prod \chi_i)\).

Been \(k\) an algebra spreads on \(\mathbb{F}_q\), dimensional \(N+1\) and \(\chi\) a non-trivial character of \(k^\times\), trivial on \(\mathbb{F}_q^\times\). The sum of Jacobi \(J(\chi)\) is defined by
\[ \tag{4.14.1} J(\chi) = (-1)^{N-1} \sum_{\substack{x\in k^\times/\mathbb{F}_q^\times \\ \operatorname{tr}(x)=0}} \chi^{-1}(x) \text{.} \]
We have between Gauss and Jacobi the following identity.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.15

For \(\chi\) as above and \(\psi\) a non-trivial additive character of \(\mathbb{F}_q\),

\[ q J(\chi) = \tau_{\mathbb{F}_q}(\chi,\psi) \text{.} \]
In the particular case where \(k=\mathbb{F}_q^n\), this formula is rewritten by (4.5.3)
\[ \tag{4.15.1} q J(\chi) = \prod_i \tau(\chi_i,\psi) \text{,} \]
more written in the form

\begin{align*} \chi_0(-1) J(\chi) &= (-1)^{N-1} \sum_{\substack{x_1,\dots,x_N\in \mathbb{F}_q^\times \\ \sum x_i = 1}} \prod_{i=1}^N \chi_i^{-1}(x_i) = \tau(\chi_0^{-1},\psi)^{-1} \prod_{i=1}^N \tau(\chi_i,\psi) \\ \chi_0^{-1} &= \prod_{i=1}^N \chi_i \end{align*}

blocks.title: Proof of 4.15
blocks.html:
Proof of 4.15▾
\[ \tau_{\mathbb{F}_q}(\chi,\psi) = (-1)^{N+1} \sum_{x\in k^\times} \chi(x)^{-1} \psi \operatorname{tr}(x) = (-1)^{N+1} \sum_{x\in k^\times/\mathbb{F}_q^\times} \chi(x)^{-1} \sum_{\lambda\in \mathbb{F}_q^\times} \psi(\lambda \operatorname{tr} x) \text{.} \]

The sum \(\sum_{\lambda\in \mathbb{F}_q^\times} \psi(\lambda \operatorname{tr} x)\) is \(q-1\) if \(\operatorname{tr}(x)=0\), and \(-1\) if \(\operatorname{tr}(x)\ne 0\).

\[ \tau_{\mathbb{F}_q}(\chi,\psi) = (-1)^{N+1} \left(q\sum_{x\in k^\times/\mathbb{F}_q^\times} \chi(x)^{-1} - \sum_{x\in k^\times/\mathbb{F}_q^\times} \chi(x)^{-1}\right) \text{.} \]

The second term in the second member is null, because sum of the values of a character, and 4.15 As a result.

The assertion (ii) results from (i) and the formula of the traces; (i) and (iii) result from 4.11 and the following more geometric statement.

Proposition 4.17

Either \((\chi_i)_{i\in I}\) a non-trivial character family of \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_n(\mathbb{F})\), product \(1\). So, \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^{i-1}\left(W^\ast/\mathbb{G}_m,\mathscr{K}_m((\chi_i)_{i\in I})\right)\) is canonically isomorphic to \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^{i+1}\left(V^\ast,\mathscr{K}_m((\chi_i)_{i\in I})\otimes \mathscr{F}(\psi\operatorname{tr}_{k/\mathbb{F}_q})\right)(1)\).
The first line of 4.15 becomes (\(\pi\) as in (4.6.1)).

\[ \tag{4.17.1} \mathsf{R}^i \pi_!\left(\mathscr{K}_n((\chi_i)_{i\in I})\otimes \mathscr{F}(\psi \operatorname{tr}_{k/\mathbb{F}_q})\right) = \mathscr{K}_n((\chi_i)_{i\in I}) \otimes \mathsf{R}^i \pi_! \mathscr{F}(\psi\operatorname{tr}_{k/\mathbb{F}_q}) \]

(on \(V^\ast/\mathbb{G}_m\)). Let's calculate the sheaves \(\mathsf{R}^i\pi_! \mathscr{F}(\psi\operatorname{tr}_{k/\mathbb{F}_q}) = \mathsf{R}^i \pi_! T^\ast \mathscr{F}(\psi)\). Either \(v_0:\widetilde V_0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} V_0\) the burst of \(V_0\) in \(\{0\}\). In the diagram

The sheaf \(T^\ast\mathscr{F}(\psi)\) on \(V-0\setminus \{0\}\) extends into \((T v_0)^\ast \mathscr{F}(\psi)\) on \(\widetilde V_0\), and the restriction of this sheaf to \(Z_0\) is the constant sheaf \(E_\lambda\), because \(T_0 v_0\) is null on \(Z_0\). The exact long sequence of compact support cohomology is written therefore

The reciprocal image \(\overline{\pi}^{-1}(Q_0)\) is the burst \(\widetilde W_0\) of \(W_0\) en \(0\); \(T_0 V_0\) is canceled on \(\widetilde W_0\); we have \((T v)^\ast \mathscr{F}(\psi)=E_\lambda\) on \(\pi^{-1}(Q_0)\) and, \(\overline{\pi}\) being a straight fibrous

If \(x\in P\), \(x\notin Q\), the right \(D=\pi^{-1}(x)\) is sent isomorphically on \(\mathbb{G}_a\) by \(T v\), so we have 2.7*)

(T v)^\ast \mathscr{F}(\psi) &= \begin{cases} 0 & \text{for } i\ne 2 \\ E_\lambda(-1)_Q & \text{for } i=2 \end{cases} \end{align*}
Conjugating (4.17.3) and (4.17.5), we finally find
\[ \tag{4.17.6} \mathsf{R}^i \pi_! T^\ast\mathscr{F}(\psi) = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{for } i\ne 1,2 \\ E_\lambda & \text{for } i=1 \\ E_\lambda(-1)_Q & \text{for } i=2 \text{.} \end{cases} \]

Let's calculate the sheaf's compact support cohomology \(\mathscr{K}_n((\chi_i))\otimes T^\ast \mathscr{F}(\psi)\) on \(V^\ast\) using the Leray spectral sequence of \(\pi:V^\ast\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} V^\ast/\mathbb{G}_m\). Appliant (4.17.1) and (4.17.6), the initial terms are the \(E_2^{p,1} = \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^p(V^\ast/\mathbb{G}_m,\mathscr{K}_n(\chi_i)) = 0\) (car \((\chi_i)\ne 0\)) and the \(E_2^{p,2} = \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^p(W^\ast/\mathbb{G}_m,\mathscr{K}(\chi_i))(-1)\).
The spectral sequence is reduced to isomorphism, and 4.17 As a result.

The sheaves \(\mathscr{K}_n((\chi_i)_{i\in I})\) have a meaning on any field, or even on any basic scheme. This will allow us to generalize 4.16(i) With the ratings of 4.6, suppose \(M\) finished spreads everywhere in the range \(N\) on \(A\), and be \(n\) an invertible integer in \(A\). We note \(a\) the projection of \(W^\ast/\mathbb{G}_m\) on \(\operatorname{Spec}(A)\). Or also \(\chi\) a character (sheaf morphism) \(\chi:(V^\ast/\mathbb{G}_m)_n \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} E_\lambda^\times\), and \(\mathscr{K}_n(\chi)\) the corresponding sheaf. Locally for the spreading topology, one can look at \(\chi\) as a family of characters \((\chi_i)_{i\in I}\) of the \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_n\), of a trivial product.

blocks.html:
Proposition 4.20

  1. If \(\chi\) is (in any case) non-trivial, we have \(\mathsf{R}^i a_! (\mathscr{K}_n(\chi)) = 0\) for \(i\ne N-1\), and \(\mathsf{R}^{N-1} a_!(\mathscr{K}_n(\chi))\) is smooth, rank \(1\).

Proof▾

The diagram \(W^\ast/\mathbb{G}_m\) is the complement of a divisor with normal relative crosses in the diagram \(Q\), clean and smooth on \(\operatorname{Spec}(A)\), and \(\mathscr{K}_n(\chi)\) is locally constant on \(W^\ast/\mathbb{G}_m\), with moderate branching to infinity. As a result, the \(\mathsf{R}^i a_!\) and \(\mathsf{R}^i a_\ast\) are smooth, of formation compatible with any basic change. A standard argument then leads us to assume that \(A\) is a finite field, and (i) results from 4.17 and 4.11(i) To prove (ii), the action of \(\sigma\) is compatible with isomorphism 4.17, and 4.12. For (iii), we note that if \(\chi_i\) are all non-trivial, then \(\mathscr{K}_n(\chi)\) on \(W^\ast/\mathbb{G}_m\subset Q\) is branched along each divisor to infinity, and 1.19.1.

pages.title: Characters of Hecke
pages.html:
5 Characters of Hecke

5.1

Is \(F\) a field of numbers (of degree finished on \(\mathbb{Q}\)) and \(k\) a field of characteristic \(0\). Here are various equivalent ways to say what homomorphism is algebraic \(\alpha:F^\times \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} k^\times\).

  1. Be \(\{e_i\}\) a basis of \(F\) on \(\mathbb{Q}\). Homomorphism \(\alpha\) is algebraic if given by a formula \(\alpha(\sum x^i e_i) = A(x^i)\), \(A\in k(X^i)\).

This means that \(\alpha\) coincides with \(F^\times\) with a rational application defined on \(k\) of \(\operatorname{R}_{F/\mathbb{Q}}(\mathbb{G}_m)\) in \(\mathbb{G}_m\). By Zariski density of \(F^\times\), and the fact that a birational homomorphism is everywhere defined, such an application is a homomorphism of diagrams in groups:

  1. \(\alpha\) is induced by a homomorphism of \(k\)-schemes in groups

    \[ \operatorname{R}_{F/\mathbb{Q}}(\mathbb{G}_m)\otimes_\mathbb{Q} k \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbb{G}_m \text{.} \]

If \(k\) is a number field, the addition property of the scalar restriction \(R\) shows that this is equivalent to

  1. (\(k\) a field of numbers) \(\alpha\) is induced by a homomorphism of \(\mathbb{Q}\)-schemes in groups: \(\operatorname{R}_{F/\mathbb{Q}}(\mathbb{G}_m) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{R}_{k/\mathbb{Q}}(\mathbb{G}_m)\).

We have \(\overline{k}\) an algebraic fence of \(k\), and \(I=\hom(F,\overline{k})\). On \(\overline{k}\), the character group of \(\operatorname{R}_{F/\mathbb{Q}}(\mathbb{G}_m)\) is \(\mathbb{Z}^I\), base the dives of \(F\) in \(\overline{k}\). The characters defined on \(k\) are those invariant by \(\operatorname{Gal}(\overline{k}/k)\); we can describe them either as those sending \(F^\times\) in \(k^\times\subset \overline{k}\), or in terms of the orbits of \(\operatorname{Gal}(\overline{k}/k)\) in \(I\), corresponding itself to the factors of \(F\otimes k\).

  1. \(\alpha\) is of the form \(\alpha=\prod_{\omega\in I} \omega^{n_\omega}\). The families of exhibitors \((n_\omega)\) permitted are those such as \(n_\omega=n_{\omega'}\) for \(\omega\) and \(\omega'\) in the same orbit of \(\operatorname{Gal}(\overline{k}/k)\). These are still those such as \(\prod_\omega (x)^{n_\omega}\in k\) for everything \(x\in F\).

  2. Poisons \(F\otimes k=\prod_{j\in J} F_j\), the \(F_j\) being fields. So, \(\alpha\) is written \(\alpha=\prod N_{F_j/k}^{m_j}\).

In the particular case where \(k\) contains a normal fence of \(F\), these expressions become: \(\alpha=\prod \omega^{n_\omega}\), where \(\omega\) searches \([F:\mathbb{Q}]\) dives of \(F\) in \(k\).

5.2

Suppose that \(k\) either a field of numbers, and or \(\alpha\) an algebraic homomorphism of \(F^\times\) in \(k^\times\). There is then one and only one homomorphism, still noted \(\alpha\), of the group \(I(F)\) fractional ideals of \(F\) in that of \(k\), such as \(\alpha((x))=(\alpha(x))\). Uniqueness results from what every ideal \(a\) a power which is a main ideal, and of what \(I(k)\) To prove existence, for example, we use 5.1(v): we have \(\alpha(a) = \prod N_{F_j/k}^{m_j}((a))\).

5.3

Recall the definition of the characters of Hecke algebraic, called Weil characters of Hecke (or: grössencharaktere) of type \(A_0\). Be \(F\) a field of numbers, \(\mathfrak{m}\) an ideal of \(F\) (i.e., ring of integers of \(F\)), \(I_\mathfrak{m}\) the group of fractional ideals of \(F\) first to \(\mathfrak{m}\), and \(k\) a field of characteristic \(0\). A homomorphism \(\chi:I_\mathfrak{m} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} k^\times\) is a Character of Hecke algebraic (of driver \(\leqslant \mathfrak{m}\)) if there is algebraic homomorphism \(\chi_\text{alg}:F^\times \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} k^\times\) checking

\[ \tag{*} \text{For } x\in F^\times \text{, first to } \mathfrak{m} \text{Totally positive and } \equiv 1\pmod\mathfrak{m} \text{, we have } \chi((x))=\chi_\text{alg}(x) \text{.} \]

By density of all \(x\) of*), \(\chi_\text{alg}\) is entirely determined by \(\chi\). Algebraic part of \(\chi\). If \(\chi((x))=\chi_\text{alg}(x)\) for \(x\) fully positive and \(\equiv 1\pmod{\mathfrak{m}'}\), \(\chi\) extends into a driver's Hecke character \(\leqslant \inf(\mathfrak{m},\mathfrak{m}')\): \(I_{\mathfrak{m}+\mathfrak{m}'}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} k^\times\). The characters of Hecke that coincide with their common domain of definition will be identified, and they are called driver of \(\chi\) the smallest \(\mathfrak{m}'\) such that \(\chi\) is driver \(\leqslant \mathfrak{m}'\).

5.4. Note

If an algebraic Hecke character \(\chi\) takes its values in a subcorp \(k'\) of \(k\), it is already a Hecke character with values in \(k'\): it is enough or even that \(\chi_\text{alg}:\operatorname{R}_{F/\mathbb{Q}}(\mathbb{G}_m) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbb{G}_m\) is already defined on \(k'\), and this results from the density of Zariski in \(\operatorname{R}_{F/\mathbb{Q}}(\mathbb{G}_m)\) of all \(x\) totally positive \(\equiv 1\pmod\mathfrak{m}\). To always take for \(k'\) a subcorp of \(k\) of degree finished on \(\mathbb{Q}\).

5.5

If \(\varepsilon\) is a totally positive unit \(\equiv 1\pmod\mathfrak{m}\), we have \(\chi_\text{alg}(\varepsilon) = \chi((\varepsilon)) = 1\). The homomorphism \(\chi_\text{alg}\) is therefore factored by the quotient \(T_\mathfrak{m}\) of \(\operatorname{R}_{F/\mathbb{Q}}(\mathbb{G}_m)\) by the Zariski adhesion of the group \(E_\mathfrak{m}\subset F^\times\) of the totally positive units \(\equiv 1\pmod\mathfrak{m}\).

According to Serre [35], if \(k\) is an algebraic fence of \(\mathbb{Q}\), and \(\mathfrak{m}\) is quite large, the characters \(\prod \omega^{n_\omega}\) of \(\operatorname{R}_{F/\mathbb{Q}}(\mathbb{G}_m)\) of the form \(\chi_\text{alg}\) are characterized as follows: there must be an integer \(N\), the weight of \(\chi\) (or \(\chi_\text{alg}\)), such as for any element \(\sigma\) of \(\operatorname{Gal}(k/\mathbb{Q})\) combined with complex conjugation, one has \(n_\omega+n_{\sigma \omega}=N\). If \(F_1\) is the largest sub-field of \(F\) which is a quadratic extension totally imaginary of a totally real field \(F_1'\), this amounts to saying that \(\chi_\text{alg}\) is of the form \(\chi_1\circ N_{F/F_1}\), and that \(\chi_1|F_1=(N_{F_1/\mathbb{Q}})^N\).

If \(\chi\) is of weight \(N\), for any ideal \(\mathfrak{a}\) of \(F\), \(\chi(\mathfrak{a})\) is an algebraic number with all conjugates of absolute value \(N(\mathfrak{a})^{N/2}\) [35].

5.6

For \(k\) a fixed field of numbers, additional conditions are imposed on \(\chi_\text{alg}\). For any ideal \(\mathfrak{a}\) from \(F\) first to the driver, we have indeed

\[ \tag{5.6.1} \chi_\text{alg}(\mathfrak{a}) = (\chi(\mathfrak{a})) \text{,} \]

So that \(\chi_\text{alg}(\mathfrak{a})\) is principal. Since the group of fractional ideals of \(k\) is under twist, just prove the power \(n\)-th of (5.6.1), \(n\ne 0\) suitable; this makes it possible to replace \(\mathfrak{a}\) by \(\mathfrak{a}^n\), so assume \(\mathfrak{a}=(x)\), with \(x\) totally positive \(\equiv 1\pmod\mathfrak{m}\). Only the definitions have to be used.

This, attached to 5.5, shows that \(\chi_\text{alg}\) determines the standard of \(\chi(\mathfrak{a})\) in all seats of \(k\).

5.7

The Serre \(S_\mathfrak{m}\) group could be characterized as the multiplicative type group whose character group (on any field) is the driver's algebraic Hecke character group \(\leqslant \mathfrak{m}\). (cf. [35]) Its relationship with the representations \(\ell\)-adics is explained in [35].

Theorem 5.8 ([35])

Be \(\chi\) an algebraic Hecke character of \(F\) in \(E_\lambda\), for \(E_\lambda\) a finite extension of \(\mathbb{Q}_\ell\). There is then one (and only one) homomorphism \(\chi_\lambda:\operatorname{Gal}(\overline{F}/F)^\textnormal{ab} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} E_\lambda\), such as

  1. \(\chi_\lambda\) is unbranched outside the driver \(\mathfrak{f}\) of \(\chi\) and \(\ell\).

  2. For \(\mathfrak{p}\) a first ideal of \(F\) first to \(\mathfrak{f}\) and \(\ell\), and \(F_\mathfrak{p}\in \operatorname{Gal}(\overline{F}/F)^\textnormal{ab}\) the geometric Frobenius in \(\mathfrak{p}\), we have

    \[ \chi_\lambda(F_\mathfrak{p}) = \chi(\mathfrak{p}) \text{.} \]

5.9

In the end of this paragraph, we will give a criterion for a representation \(\lambda\)-adic to come from a Hecke character, and in the following paragraph we will apply it to the sum of Jacobi. We will find thus, a little generalized of the results of Weil [42, 44], with the second part of the Weil demonstration replaced by a cohomology argument \(\ell\)-adic.

Theorem 5.10

Either \(\chi'\) an algebraic Hecke character with values in a finished Galoisian extension \(k'\) of \(k\), algebraic part \(\chi_0\) (5.5). Quit to enlarge \(T\), it can be assumed that the driver of \(\chi'\) is supported in \(T\). Be \(\lambda'\) a place of \(k'\) above \(\lambda\), \(\chi_{\lambda'}\) the compound \(\operatorname{Gal}(\overline{F}/F)^\text{ab}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} k_\lambda^\times \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} {k'}_{\lambda'}^\times\), and \(\chi_{\lambda'}':\operatorname{Gal}(\overline{F}/F)^\text{ab}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} {k'}_{\lambda'}^\times\) defined by \(\chi'\) (5.8) Let's put it \(\varepsilon = \chi_{\lambda'} \cdot {\chi'}_{\lambda'}^{-1}\).

The hypothesis (ii), and 5.5, 5.6, 5.8 ensure that for \(\mathfrak{p}\notin T\), we have \(\varepsilon(F_\mathfrak{p})\in {k'}^\times\), and that, in all completed \(k'\), this number is standard \(1\). As a result, \(\varepsilon(F_\mathfrak{p})\) belongs to the group (finished) \(\boldsymbol{\mu}\) roots of the unit of \(k'\). Based on the density theorem of Cebotarev, and the density hypothesis made on \(T\), the \(F_\mathfrak{p}\) (\(\mathfrak{p}\notin T\)) are dense in \(\operatorname{Gal}(\overline{F}/F)^\text{ab}\), so that \(\varepsilon(\sigma)\in \mu\) for everything \(\sigma\in \operatorname{Gal}(\overline{F}/F)^\text{ab}\): the character \(\varepsilon\) is a finite order character \(\operatorname{Gal}(\overline{F}/F)^\text{ab}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mu\). According to the theory of the class field, it corresponds to a finite order character of the group of idol classes of \(F\), i.e. to a character of Algebraic Hecke of trivial algebraic part. Correcting \(\chi'\) by this character, we simply assume that \(\chi_{\lambda'}=\chi_{\lambda'}'\), and it remains to show that \(\chi'\), a priori to values in \(k'\), is actually at values in \(k\). If \(\sigma\in \operatorname{Gal}(k'/k)\), \(\chi'\) and \({\chi'}^\sigma\) coincide on any first ideal \(\mathfrak{p}\notin T\), since \(\chi'(\mathfrak{p}) = \chi'_{\lambda'}(F_\mathfrak{p}) = \chi_{\lambda'}(F_\mathfrak{p}) \in k^\times\). Characters \(\chi_{\lambda'}'\), and \({\chi_{\lambda'}'}^\sigma\), thus coincide on a dense part of \(\operatorname{Gal}(\overline{F}/F)^\text{ab}\), so are equal, so that \(\chi'={\chi'}^\sigma\): \(\chi'\) is at values in \(k\).

Is \(F\) a field of numbers (of degree finished on \(\mathbb{Q}\)) and \(k\) a field of characteristic \(0\). Here are various equivalent ways to say what homomorphism is algebraic \(\alpha:F^\times \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} k^\times\).
1. Be \(\{e_i\}\) a basis of \(F\) on \(\mathbb{Q}\). Homomorphism \(\alpha\) is algebraic if given by a formula \(\alpha(\sum x^i e_i) = A(x^i)\), \(A\in k(X^i)\).

This means that \(\alpha\) coincides with \(F^\times\) with a rational application defined on \(k\) of \(\operatorname{R}_{F/\mathbb{Q}}(\mathbb{G}_m)\) in \(\mathbb{G}_m\). By Zariski density of \(F^\times\), and the fact that a birational homomorphism is everywhere defined, such an application is a homomorphism of diagrams in groups:

\[ \tag{5.6.1} \chi_\text{alg}(\mathfrak{a}) = (\chi(\mathfrak{a})) \text{,} \]
