pages.title: The characters of Hecke defined by the sums of Jacobi
pages.html:
6 Hecke characters defined by Jacobi's sums

The idea of this paragraph is the following: either a sum of Gauss \(\tau_{\mathbb{F}_q}(\chi,\psi)\). It itself, and the corresponding sheaf \(\mathscr{F}(\chi^{-1}\psi)\) are twice related to the characteristic \(p\), and to the base field \(\mathbb{F}_q\):

  1. because it shows the additive character \(\psi\), and the sheaf \(\sf(\psi)\);

  2. because \(\mathscr{F}(\chi)\) is defied from the exact sequence of Lang

    \[ \xymatrix{ 0 \ar[r] & k^\times \ar[r] & V^\ast \ar[r] & V^\ast \ar[r] & 0 . } \]

Two difficulties if one wants to identify on a field of numbers \(F\) the corresponding cohomological constructions, and find representations \(\ell\)-adics of \(\operatorname{Gal}(\overline{F}/F)\) where the eigenvalues of Frobenius are sums of Gauss.

If \(\chi\) is trivial on \(\mathbb{F}_q^\times\), the sum \(\tau_{\mathbb{F}_q}(\chi,\psi)\) is independent of \(\psi\), and \(q\) times a sum of Jacobi. This sum is related to the cohomology of \(W^\ast/\mathbb{G}_m\), values in a sheaf deduced from \(\chi\): \(\psi\), and difficulty a), have disappeared. To solve (b), it is sufficient to describe the sheaves used using exact Kümmer sequences (which have a meaning in any characteristic). This is possible thanks to 4.9.

Once the representations are found \(\ell\)-adics, we use 5.10 and Stickelberger's theorem to show that they come from characters of Algebraic Hecke.

6.1

If \(E\) is an algebraic enclosure field \(\overline{E}\), and \(\lambda\) is a dividing order character \(n\) of \(\mu_d(\overline{E})\), we note \(_n\lambda\) the character of \(\mu_n(\overline{E})\) such that \(\lambda\) and \(_n \lambda\) induce the same character of \(\widehat{\mathbb{Z}}(1)_{\overline{E}} = \varinjlim \mu_n(\overline{E})\). Similarly for \(\lambda\) a character of \(\widehat{\mathbb{Z}}(1)_{\overline{E}}\).

We have \(F\) and \(k\) two fields of numbers, \(\overline{F}\) an algebraic fence of \(F\), \(I\) a finite set with an action of \(\operatorname{Gal}(\overline{F}/F)\) and \(\lambda=(\lambda_i)_{i\in I}\) a family of characters \(\lambda_i:\widehat{\mathbb{Z}}(1)_{\overline{F}} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} k^\times\). It is assumed that \(\lambda_i\ne 1\) (\(i\in I\)), that the product of \(\lambda_i\) is trivial and that the family \(\lambda\) is defined on \(F\), i.e. for everything \(\sigma\in \operatorname{Gal}(\overline{F}/F)\), we have \(\lambda_{\sigma(i)}=\lambda_i\circ \sigma^{-1}\).

The Galoisian ensemble \(I\) is the whole of homomorphisms in \(\overline{F}\) Of an algbra \(E\) separable on \(F\). If \(C\) is the total of the orbits of \(\operatorname{Gal}(\overline{F}/F)\) in \(I\), we have a decomposition of \(E\) as a field product \(E=\prod_{\alpha\in C} E_\alpha\), where \(\alpha\) is identified with all of the dives of \(E_\alpha\) in \(\overline{F}\). If the characters \(\lambda_i\), for \(i\in \alpha\), are of order \(d_\alpha\), the field \(E_\alpha\) contains the roots \(d_\alpha\)-thousands of \(1\) and there is \(\lambda_\alpha:\mu_{d_\alpha}(E_\alpha) \hookrightarrow k^\times\) such that, if \(i\in \alpha\) corresponds to the dive \(\sigma_i\) of the \(E_\alpha\) in \(\overline{F}\), we've got \(_{d_\alpha} \lambda_i = \lambda_\alpha \sigma_i^{-1}\).

6.2

Be \(n>0\) a multiple of \(d_\alpha\), \(\Sigma\) all seats of \(F\) where \(E/F\) branches or divides \(n\), \(\mu\) a place of \(k\) residual characteristic \(\ell\), and \(A\) the ring of the elements of \(F\) whole outside \(\Sigma\) and \(\ell\). The integral fence \(M\) of \(A\) is finished range \(N=|I|\) on \(A\). 4.6, 4.8, \(_n \lambda_i\) define

\[ _n\lambda:(V^\ast/\mathbb{G}_m)_n \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} k^\times \subset k_\mu^\times \text{.} \]

Either \(a\) the projection of \(W^\ast/\mathbb{G}_m\) on \(\operatorname{Spec}(A)\). 4.20, the sheaf \(\mathsf{R}^{N-2} a_! \mathscr{K}_n(_n \lambda)\) is smooth in rank one; it defines a representation \(\ell\)-adic

\[ j_\mu[\lambda]:\operatorname{Gal}(\overline{F}/F)^\text{ab} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} k_\mu^\times \]

(or simply \(j_\mu\)) not branched outside \(\sigma\) and \(\ell\).

6.3

Calculate \(j_\mu(F_\mathfrak{p})\). Based on 4.20, just do it after reduction modulo \(\mathfrak{p}\). Therefore \(f=A/\mathfrak{p}\), \(e=M/\mathfrak{p} M\), and \(\overline{f}\) the algebraic fence of \(f\) defined by a place of \(\overline{F}\) above \(\mathfrak{p}\). We still have \(I=\hom_f(e,\overline{f})\). After reduction, \(_n\lambda\) still admits a description 6.1:

  1. Either \(D\) all the orbits of the \(\operatorname{Gal}(\overline{f}/f)\) in \(I\). Decomposition of \(e\) as a field product is written \(e=\prod_{\beta\in D} e_\beta\), where the \(\sigma_i\) (\(i\in \beta\)) identify with the dives of \(e_\beta\) in \(F\).   2. pages.title: Generalized Kloosterman
pages.html:
7 Generalised Kloosterman

7.1

Is \(\mathbb{F}_q\) a field finished to \(q\) elements, and \(\psi\) a nontrivial additive character. In this paragraph, we do a cohomological study of generalized Kloosterman amounts

\[ \tag{7.1.1} \mathrm{K}_{n,a} = \sum_{x_1\dotsm x_n = a} \psi(x_1+\cdots + x_n) \qquad (n\geqslant 1) \text{.} \]

For \(a=0\), this sum is elementary (cf. 7.7):

\[ \tag{7.1.2} \mathrm{K}_{n,0} = (-1)^{n-1} \text{.} \]

The interesting case is where \(a\ne 0\). The \(x_i\) are then in \(\mathbb{F}_q^\times\). In this case we will prove an increase

\[ \tag{7.1.3} \left|K_{n,a}\right| \leqslant n q^{\frac{n-1}{2}} \text{.} \]

We essential tools will be the cohomological reflection statements the following identities

\begin{align*} \tag{7.1.4} \mathrm{K}_{n,a} &= \sum_{x_1} \psi(x_i) \sum_{x_2\dotsm x_n = a/x_1} \psi(x_2+\cdots + x_n) \\ \notag &= \sum_{x\in \mathbb{F}_q^\times} \psi(x) \mathrm{K}_{n-1,a/x} \qquad (a\ne 0,n\geqslant 2) \\ \tag{7.1.5} \sum_a \mathrm{K}_{n,a} &= \sum_a \sum_{x_1 \dotsm x_n = a} \psi\left(\sum x_i\right) = \sum_{x_1,\dots,x_n} \psi\left(\sum x_i\right) = 0 \end{align*}

For \(\chi\) a non-trivial character of \(\mathbb{F}_q^\times\),

\[ \tag{7.1.6} \sum \chi(a) \mathrm{K}_{n,a} = \sum_{x_1,\dots,x_n} \chi\left(\prod x_i\right) \cdot \psi\left(\sum x_i\right) = (-\tau(\chi^{-1},\psi))^n \]

(sum of Gauss).

7.2

Is \(k\) an algebra of extremity \(n\) on \(\mathbb{F}_q\), and \(\varepsilon(k)\) as in (4.5.1) Let's put it down.

\[ \mathrm{K}_{k,a} = \sum_{N_{k/\mathbb{F}_q}(x)=a} \psi \operatorname{tr}(x) \text{.} \]

We still have

\begin{align*} \tag{7.2.1} \mathrm{K}_{k,0} &= (-1)^{n-1} \cdot \varepsilon(k) \\ \tag{7.2.2} \sum_a \mathrm{K}_{k,a} &= 0 \\ \tag{7.2.3} \sum_a \chi(a) \mathrm{K}_{k,a} &= (-1)^n \tau_{\mathbb{F}_q}\left(\chi^{-1}\circ N_{k/\mathbb{F}_q},\psi\right) \text{.} \end{align*}

By inversion of Fourier on \(\mathbb{F}_q^\times\), an expression of \(\mathrm{K}_{k,a}\) in terms of sums of Gauss is deduced:

The amounts \(\mathrm{K}_{n,a}\) of this paragraph have been studied by S. Sperber, in his thesis, and in [38] by methods \(p\)-adics inspired by Dwork and Bombieri's article [6]. For \(p\ne 2\), these methods give it the dependency in \(q\) 7.5, the functional equation \(\alpha_i = q^{n-1} \beta_i^{-1}\) between the Frobenius eigenvalues for the sums \(\mathrm{K}_{n,a}\) and \(\mathrm{K}_{n,b}\) if \(b=(-1)^n a\) (cf. 7.6), and the value 7.15 For \(p\geqslant n+3\), it further determines the values \(p\)-adics of the roots. The result is very beautiful: if you place the roots \(\alpha_i\) in a suitable order, with \(0\leqslant i\leqslant n\), the \(\alpha_i q^{-i}\) are units. Restrictions on \(p\) are probably not essential.

blocks.html: 7 Generalised Kloosterman
Is \(\mathbb{F}_q\) a field finished to \(q\) elements, and \(\psi\) a nontrivial additive character. In this paragraph, we do a cohomological study of generalized Kloosterman amounts
\[ \tag{7.1.1} \mathrm{K}_{n,a} = \sum_{x_1\dotsm x_n = a} \psi(x_1+\cdots + x_n) \qquad (n\geqslant 1) \text{.} \]
For \(a=0\), this sum is elementary (cf. 7.7):
\[ \tag{7.1.2} \mathrm{K}_{n,0} = (-1)^{n-1} \text{.} \]
The interesting case is where \(a\ne 0\). The \(x_i\) are then in \(\mathbb{F}_q^\times\). In this case we will prove an increase
\[ \tag{7.1.3} \left|K_{n,a}\right| \leqslant n q^{\frac{n-1}{2}} \text{.} \]
We essential tools will be the cohomological reflection statements the following identities

\begin{align*} \tag{7.1.4} \mathrm{K}_{n,a} &= \sum_{x_1} \psi(x_i) \sum_{x_2\dotsm x_n = a/x_1} \psi(x_2+\cdots + x_n) \\ \notag &= \sum_{x\in \mathbb{F}_q^\times} \psi(x) \mathrm{K}_{n-1,a/x} \qquad (a\ne 0,n\geqslant 2) \\ \tag{7.1.5} \sum_a \mathrm{K}_{n,a} &= \sum_a \sum_{x_1 \dotsm x_n = a} \psi\left(\sum x_i\right) = \sum_{x_1,\dots,x_n} \psi\left(\sum x_i\right) = 0 \end{align*}
For \(\chi\) a non-trivial character of \(\mathbb{F}_q^\times\),
\[ \tag{7.1.6} \sum \chi(a) \mathrm{K}_{n,a} = \sum_{x_1,\dots,x_n} \chi\left(\prod x_i\right) \cdot \psi\left(\sum x_i\right) = (-\tau(\chi^{-1},\psi))^n \]
(sum of Gauss).
Is \(k\) an algebra of extremity \(n\) on \(\mathbb{F}_q\), and \(\varepsilon(k)\) as in (4.5.1) Let's put it down.
\[ \mathrm{K}_{k,a} = \sum_{N_{k/\mathbb{F}_q}(x)=a} \psi \operatorname{tr}(x) \text{.} \]
We still have

\begin{align*} \tag{7.2.1} \mathrm{K}_{k,0} &= (-1)^{n-1} \cdot \varepsilon(k) \\ \tag{7.2.2} \sum_a \mathrm{K}_{k,a} &= 0 \\ \tag{7.2.3} \sum_a \chi(a) \mathrm{K}_{k,a} &= (-1)^n \tau_{\mathbb{F}_q}\left(\chi^{-1}\circ N_{k/\mathbb{F}_q},\psi\right) \text{.} \end{align*}
By inversion of Fourier on \(\mathbb{F}_q^\times\), an expression of \(\mathrm{K}_{k,a}\) in terms of sums of Gauss is deduced:

\begin{align*} \mathrm{K}_{k,a} &= \frac{1}{q-1} \sum_\chi \chi(a) \sum_{b\in \mathbb{F}_q^\times} \chi^{-1}(b) \mathrm{K}_{k,b} \\ \tag{7.2.4} (-1)^n \mathrm{K}_{k,a} &= \frac{1}{q-1} \left(\varepsilon(k) + \sum_{\chi\ne 1} \chi(a) \tau_{\mathbb{F}_q}\left(\chi\circ N_{k/\mathbb{F}_q},\psi\right) \right) \text{.} \end{align*}
The identity of Hasse-Davenport now compares \(\mathrm{K}_{k,a}\) to \(\mathrm{K}_{n,a} = \mathrm{K}_{\mathbb{F}_{q^n},a}\):
\[ \tag{7.2.5} \mathrm{K}_{k,a} = \varepsilon(k) \mathrm{K}_{n,a} \text{.} \]

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 7.4

The cohomology of \(V_a^{n-1}\) to values in \(\mathscr{F}(\psi(\sum x_i))\) verifies

  1. \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i=0\) for \(i\ne n-1\);

  2. \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^\bullet{} \xrightarrow{\sim} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet{}\);

  3. for \(a\ne 0\), \(\dim \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^{n-1}{}=n\);

  4. for \(a=0\), \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^{n-1}{}\) is canonically isomorphic to \(E_\lambda\).

As usual, this theorem also controls the dependence of \(\mathrm{K}_{n,a}\) in \(q\): for each \(a\in \mathbb{F}_q^\times\), there are \(n\) Frobenius eigenvalues \(\alpha_1,\dots,\alpha_n\), of complex absolute value \(q^{\frac{n-1}{2}}\), such as
\[ \sum_{\substack{x_1\dotsm x_n = a \\ x_i\in \mathbb{F}_{q^m}}} \psi\circ \operatorname{tr}\left(\sum x_i\right) = (-1)^{n-1} \left(\alpha_1^m + \cdots + \alpha_n^m \right) \text{.} \]

For \(n\) pair, \(x\mapsto -x\) is an involution of \(V_a\), which transforms \(\mathscr{F}(\psi)\) into a dual sound. 3.6, an alternate form is deduced not degenerate canonical on \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^{n-1}(V_a,\mathscr{F}(\psi))\), at values in \(\mathbb{Q}_\ell(-(n-1))\) and the

There remains an isomorphism \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^0{}\xrightarrow{\sim} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^0{}=E_\lambda\) that justifies 7.4.

We'll prove 7.4 and 7.8 below by a simultaneous recurrence on \(n\), and we rely on the spectral sequence of which (7.1.4) is the reflection. This requires a control of the dependence in \(a\) of \(\mathrm{K}_{n,a}\).

blocks.title: Theorem

Theorem 7.8

  1. The sheaf \(\mathsf{R}^{n-1}\pi_! \mathscr{F}(\psi\sigma)\) is smooth \(n\) on \(\mathbb{A}^1\setminus \{0\}\).

  2. its extension by \(0\) on \(\mathbb{P}^1\) is the direct image of its restriction to \(\mathbb{A}^1\setminus \{0\}\).

  4. in \(\infty\), wild inertia acts with no fixed point \(\ne 0\), and Swan's driver is \(1\),

  5. we have \(\mathsf{R}^i \pi_! \mathscr{F}(\psi\sigma) \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathsf{R}^i \pi_\ast \mathscr{F}(\psi\sigma)\) (none for \(i\ne n-1\)).

Note
\(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^1(V_a^1,\mathscr{F}(\psi\sigma))\) is also the first group of cohomology of the completed elliptical curve of the equation affine curve
\begin{align*} y^2-y &= x_1+x_2 \\ x_1 x_2 &= a \text{;} \end{align*}
its modular invariant is \(j=a^{-2}\).
For \(\chi\) a multiplicative character of \(\mathbb{F}_q^\times\), and \(a\ne 0\), the method of 7.14 applies to the study of the sum
\[ s = \sum_{x_0\dotsm x_n = a} \chi(x_0) \psi(x_0+\cdots + x_n) \]

(replace sheaf \(\mathscr{F}(\psi)\) on \(\mathbb{G}_m\) by \(\mathscr{F}(\chi\cdot \psi)\)). There is still a cohomology of dimension \(n+1\), and \(|S|\leqslant (n+1)q^{n/2}\). Such sums have been considered by Salie and Mordell [28].
We can hope that our methods will allow us to study the
\[ S_{k,\chi,a} = \sum_{N(x)=a} \chi(x) \psi \operatorname{tr}(x) \]
(\(k\) algebra spreads over \(\mathbb{F}_q\), \(a\in \mathbb{F}_q^\times\), \(\chi\) character of \(k^\times\)). 7.4 (for \(a\ne 0\)) should be true.

The symmetrical group \(S_n\) acts on \(\mathbb{A}^n\) respecting \(V_a\) and implementation \(\sigma\), so also the sheaf \(\sf(\psi\sigma)\), reciprocal image by \(\sigma\) of the sheaf \(\sf(\psi)\) on \(\mathbb{G}_a\). It is therefore a matter of \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^{n-1}(V_a,\mathscr{F}(\psi\sigma))\). The formula (7.2.5) admits the following cohomological interpretation

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 7.20

The action of \(S_n\) on \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^{n-1}(V_a,\mathscr{F}(\psi\sigma))\) is multiplication by sign character.

This amounts to showing that a transposition \(\tau\) acts by multiplication by \(-1\). Since \(\tau^2=1\), \(\tau\) can have as its eigenvalue only \(\pm 1\). Either \(V_a/\tau\) the quotient of \(V_a\) for \(\{\operatorname{id},\tau\}\). The application \(\sigma\), therefore the sheaf \(\sf(\psi\sigma)\), pass to the quotient, and the subspace of \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^{n-1}(V_a,\mathscr{F}(\psi))\) fixed by \(\tau\) is \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^{n-1}(V_a/\tau,\mathscr{F}(\psi\sigma))\). We must prove that this cohomology is null.

Suppose that \(a\ne 0\). For \(\tau=(1,2)\) the quotient \(V_a/\tau\) is identified with \(\mathbb{G}_a\times \mathbb{G}_m^{n-2}\), with for application of passage to the quotient \((x_1,x_2,x_3,\dots,x_n)\mapsto (x_1+x_2,x_3,\dots,x_n)\). Indeed, \(x_1\) and \(x_2\) are determined to the nearest order by \(x_1+x_2\) and \(x_1x_2 = a/x_3\dotsm x_n\). In these coordinates \((t,x_3,\dots,x_n)\), the sheaf is written \(\mathscr{F}(\psi(t+x_3+\cdots + x_n)) = \mathscr{F}(\psi(t))\otimes \mathscr{F}(\psi(x_3+\cdots + x_n))\). The formula of Künnet, and \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_0^\bullet(\mathbb{G}_a,\mathscr{F}(\psi)) = 0\), therefore provide the requested nullity.
For \(a=0\), you can deduct 7.20 for calculation 7.7.

The amounts \(\mathrm{K}_{n,a}\) of this paragraph have been studied by S. Sperber, in his thesis, and in [38] by methods \(p\)-adics inspired by Dwork and Bombieri's article [6]. For \(p\ne 2\), these methods give it the dependency in \(q\) 7.5, the functional equation \(\alpha_i = q^{n-1} \beta_i^{-1}\) between the Frobenius eigenvalues for the sums \(\mathrm{K}_{n,a}\) and \(\mathrm{K}_{n,b}\) if \(b=(-1)^n a\) (cf. 7.6), and the value 7.15 For \(p\geqslant n+3\), it further determines the values \(p\)-adics of the roots. The result is very beautiful: if you place the roots \(\alpha_i\) in a suitable order, with \(0\leqslant i\leqslant n\), the \(\alpha_i q^{-i}\) are units. Restrictions on \(p\) are probably not essential.

pages.title: Other applications
pages.html:
8 Other applications

8.1

Identity 1.9.4 sometimes can be used to calculate the number of rational points of an algebraic variety on \(\mathbb{F}_q\). In most cases where an exact result has been obtained, cohomology is expressed in terms of algebraic cycles; this is the case for rational surfaces, quadrics, smooth intersections of two quadrics of odd dimension, varieties of flags.... However, this is always the case; in the beautiful Lusztig article: [27], algebraic cycles do not appear.

8.2

For reductive groups, cohomology can be calculated by rising to characteristic \(0\): if \(B\) is a sub-group of Borel of \(G\), \(G\) is a \(B\)-torseur on \(G/B\); the cohomology of the projective and smooth variety \(G/B\) is invariant for specialization, and the spectral sequence of Leray of \(G\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G/B\) is used to prove the same result for \(G\). It is expressed conveniently in term of the maximum \(T\) of \(G\) and of the action of the Weil group \(W\) on \(T\) (for definition of the maximum tore, cf. [7] or [10]) It is the external algebra of its primitive part, which coincides with a close offset with the quotient \(I(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(B G,\mathbb{Q}_\ell))\) of the cohomology of \(B G\) formed of the "uncomposable elements," and \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(B G,\mathbb{Q}_\ell)=\underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(B T,\mathbb{Q}_\ell)^W\). If \(X\) is the character group of \(T\), we have in total

\[ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(G,\mathbb{Q}_\ell) = \bigwedge \left(I\left(\operatorname{Sym}^\bullet(X\otimes \mathbb{Q}_\ell(-1))^W\right)[-1]\right) \text{.} \]

If \(G\) is defined on a finished field \(\mathbb{F}_q\), \(\operatorname{Gal}(\mathbb{F}/\mathbb{F}_q)\) acts on \(X\) and this isomorphism is compatible with Galois. Moving from there to the compact support cohomology, we find the classical formulae for the number of rational points of \(G\). If \(F^\ast:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) is defined by the morphism of Frobenius \(F:T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T\), and \(\deg_G(F)\) is the degree \(q^{\dim G}\) of \(F:G\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G\), we have

\[ \tag{8.2.1} \left|G_0(\mathbb{F}_q)\right| = \deg_G(F) \cdot \det\left(1-{F^\ast}^{-1},I(\operatorname{Sym}^\bullet(X\otimes \mathbb{Q})^W)\right) \text{.} \]

The same formula applies to Ree and Suzuki groups; these groups are of the form \(G^F\) (\(G\) reductive, \(F^2\) a Frobenius), and it is sufficient to verify that the formula of the traces of Lefschetz is true for \(F\) acting on \(G\). If \(B\) is a sub-group of Borel stable by \(F\), it is verified directly for \(F\) acting on \(B\), and for the action on \(G/B\), clean and smooth, one can invoke the general theorems.

8.3

The trace formula was used by Deligne-Lusztig [10], Kazhdan [25] and Springer [39] in the study of complex representations of finite groups \(G_0(\mathbb{F}_q)\), for \(G_0\) reductive on \(\mathbb{F}_q\). Kazhdan and Springer's work contain admirable examples of how the trace formula allows the "analytic extension" of a situation deployed to an undeployed situation (cf. 1.13).
